<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>For all redox problems, make sure the electrons on both half reaction has the same coefficient.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""slide_19.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;kinetic&amp;nbsp&#x3B;collision&#x20;theory&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[theory]</span> says that the rate of a reaction is <i>dictated </i>by the rate of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular collisions</u></span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic&nbsp;collision theory</b></span> says that the rate of a reaction is <i>dictated </i>by the rate of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular collisions</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Number of collisions per second.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c9e7bce973617b5f4cb7a8f595383e5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic&nbsp;collision theory</b></span> says that the rate of a reaction is <i>dictated </i>by the rate of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;molecular&#x20;collisions&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic&nbsp;collision theory</b></span> says that the rate of a reaction is <i>dictated </i>by the rate of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular collisions</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Number of collisions per second.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c9e7bce973617b5f4cb7a8f595383e5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""></div>"
"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;effective&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>collisions</b>&nbsp;lead to product formation by colliding in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>right orientation</u></span> &amp; with <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u></span>.</div>"	"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>effective</b></span> <b>collisions</b>&nbsp;lead to product formation by colliding in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>right orientation</u></span> &amp; with <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This ""kinetic energy"" they need to collide with is the <b><u>activation energy</u></b>&nbsp;(energy sufficient enough to break their existing bonds &amp; form new ones).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c9e7bce973617b5f4cb7a8f595383e5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>effective</b></span> <b>collisions</b>&nbsp;lead to product formation by colliding in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;right&#x20;orientation&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; with <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>effective</b></span> <b>collisions</b>&nbsp;lead to product formation by colliding in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>right orientation</u></span> &amp; with <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This ""kinetic energy"" they need to collide with is the <b><u>activation energy</u></b>&nbsp;(energy sufficient enough to break their existing bonds &amp; form new ones).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c9e7bce973617b5f4cb7a8f595383e5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u> needed for an <i>effective </i>collision</span>&nbsp;is known as the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;activation&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u> needed for an <i>effective </i>collision</span>&nbsp;is known as the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>activation energy</b></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Effective collsions require this amount of energy along with being oriented the right way in order to form products.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c9e7bce973617b5f4cb7a8f595383e5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ineffective&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<b>collisions</b>&nbsp;<i>do not</i> lead to product formation since they <i>lack </i>the <u>right orientation</u> &amp; <u>kinetic energy</u>.<br></div>"	"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ineffective</b></span>&nbsp;<b>collisions</b>&nbsp;<i>do not</i> lead to product formation since they <i>lack </i>the <u>right orientation</u> &amp; <u>kinetic energy</u>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Effective collsions require that the molecules have sufficient kinetic energy (""<b>activation energy</b>"") and are in the right orientation in order to form products.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c9e7bce973617b5f4cb7a8f595383e5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the reaction's <u>rate constant (k)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> when <b>frequency factor (A)</b> increases.</div>"	"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the reaction's <u>rate constant (k)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> when <b>frequency factor (A)</b> increases.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Frequency factor (A)</b> measures the frequency of molecular collisions.</div><div>- Shown in the&nbsp;<u style=""font-weight: bold;"">Arrhenius equation</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the reaction's <u>rate constant (k)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> exponentially when <b>temperature (T)</b>&nbsp;increases.</div>"	"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the reaction's <u>rate constant (k)</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> exponentially when <b>temperature (T)</b>&nbsp;increases.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Frequency factor (A), which is a measure of the frequency of collisions, also causes the rate constant (k) to increase.</div><div>- Shown in the&nbsp;<u style=""font-weight: bold;"">Arrhenius equation</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;frequency&#x20;factor&#x20;&#x28;A&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>amount </i>of <u>molecular collisions</u></span>.<br></div>"	"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>frequency factor (A)</b></span> represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>amount </i>of <u>molecular collisions</u></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Increasing frequency factor (A) increases the reaction's rate constant (k).</div><div>- Shown in the&nbsp;<u style=""font-weight: bold;"">Arrhenius equation</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the <b>frequency factor (A)</b>&nbsp;is&nbsp;<i>increased</i> when&nbsp;the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;concentration&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;of&#x20;a&#x20;reagent"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in a reaction is increased - <i>increasing</i> the rate of the reaction.</div>"	"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the <b>frequency factor (A)</b>&nbsp;is&nbsp;<i>increased</i> when&nbsp;the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>concentration</u>&nbsp;of a reagent</span> in a reaction is increased - <i>increasing</i> the rate of the reaction.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Frequency factor (A) represents the number of moleuclar collisions.</div><div>- Increasing frequency factor (A) increases the reaction's rate constant (k). Therefore, increasing concentration increases the reaction constant (k) - increasing reaction rate.</div><div>- Shown in the&nbsp;<u style=""font-weight: bold;"">Arrhenius equation</u></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""1d56f6a3a3fcbec157f2319d4025db75.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Another <i>name </i>for a reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>transition state</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;activated&#x20;complex&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"Another <i>name </i>for a reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>transition state</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>activated complex</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6510e8a58dc771b5e87499d3c1ab3d48.png""><br></div></div>"
"Another <i>name </i>for a reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;transition&#x20;state&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>activated complex</u></span>."	"Another <i>name </i>for a reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>transition state</b></span> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>activated complex</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6510e8a58dc771b5e87499d3c1ab3d48.png""><br></div></div>"
"On a reaction diagram, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>transition state</b></span> has the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;greatest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[least/greatest]</span> amount of <u>energy</u>."	"On a reaction diagram, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>transition state</b></span> has the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greatest</i></span> amount of <u>energy</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is the point that needs to be overcome by the <b>activation energy</b> (i.e. the kinetic energy of the colliding molecules).</div><div><br></div><img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d8b6ef45dc3171c8f4fe071e9a1a33d69c8cadc8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"On a reaction diagram, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;transition&#x20;state&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greatest</i></span> amount of <u>energy</u>."	"On a reaction diagram, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>transition state</b></span> has the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greatest</i></span> amount of <u>energy</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is the point that needs to be overcome by the <b>activation energy</b> (i.e. the kinetic energy of the colliding molecules).</div><div><br></div><img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d8b6ef45dc3171c8f4fe071e9a1a33d69c8cadc8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Once a reaction has reached its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;transition&#x20;state&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, the molecules can <i>either </i>form products or revert back to its reactants.<br></div>"	"<div>Once a reaction has reached its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>transition state</b></span>, the molecules can <i>either </i>form products or revert back to its reactants.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Flowing ""down"" to form products at this point requires no additional input of energy (energy was only needed to reach the transition state in the first place)</div><div>- This is the point that needs to be overcome by the <b>activation energy</b> (i.e. the kinetic energy of the colliding molecules).</div><div><br></div><img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d8b6ef45dc3171c8f4fe071e9a1a33d69c8cadc8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"On a reaction diagram, the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;intermediate&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[transition/intermediate]</span> has <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;<u>energy</u><i style="""">&nbsp;</i>than the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""transition&#x20;state"" data-ordinal=""1"">[transition/intermediate]</span>."	"On a reaction diagram, the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediate</b></span> has <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;<u>energy</u><i style="""">&nbsp;</i>than the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">transition state</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Intermediates</b> are unstable molecules (relative to reactants and products) that have short lifespans.</div><div>- This is an actual molecule that exists - formed by the reactants before reaching the products.</div><div><br></div><img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6510e8a58dc771b5e87499d3c1ab3d48.png""><br></div></div>"
"On a reaction diagram, the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediate</b></span> has <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[least/greatest]</span>&nbsp;<u>energy</u><i style="""">&nbsp;</i>than the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">transition state</span>."	"On a reaction diagram, the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediate</b></span> has <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span>&nbsp;<u>energy</u><i style="""">&nbsp;</i>than the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">transition state</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Intermediates</b> are unstable molecules (relative to reactants and products) that have short lifespans.</div><div>- This is an actual molecule that exists - formed by the reactants before reaching the products.</div><div><br></div><img src=""719cc502707f2ffcec10b2dccea094cc.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6510e8a58dc771b5e87499d3c1ab3d48.png""><br></div></div>"
"On a reaction diagram, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ΔG<sub>rxn</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">product</span>'s <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;free&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reactant</span>'s <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;free&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"On a reaction diagram, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ΔG<sub>rxn</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">product</span>'s <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reactant</span>'s <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""54f286d9c466adcd3f0a5b8b74e561d7.png""></div>"
"On a reaction diagram, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ΔG<sub>rxn</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""product"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reactant"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span>."	"On a reaction diagram, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ΔG<sub>rxn</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">product</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">reactant</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""54f286d9c466adcd3f0a5b8b74e561d7.png""></div>"
"On a reaction diagram, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ΔG&lt;sub&gt;rxn&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">product</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reactant</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span>."	"On a reaction diagram, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ΔG<sub>rxn</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>difference </i>between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">product</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span> &amp; the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reactant</span>'s <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>free energy</u></span>.

"<i>Most </i>reactions prefer to happen in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;polar&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[polar/non-polar]</span> <b>solvents</b>, since the <u>dipoles</u> in each molecule <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaken </i>their bonds</span> - permitting higher reactivity." "<i>Most </i>reactions prefer to happen in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>polar</b></span> <b>solvents</b>, since the <u>dipoles</u> in each molecule <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaken </i>their bonds</span> - permitting higher reactivity.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hetero</b></span><b>geneous catalysts </b>are catalysts in a&nbsp;<i>different&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>phase</u>&nbsp;(solid/liquid/gas)</span>&nbsp;as the reactants.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Usually, heterogeneous catalysts are <b>solids</b> catalyzing reactions in the liquid phase.</div><div><br></div><img src=""53f474361450bae0e220c90a07328d08.png""></div>"
"Catalysts <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> alter the reaction's <u>free energy</u>."	"Catalysts <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> alter the reaction's <u>free energy</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Can only speed up reaction rates by lowering the activation energy</div><div><br></div><img src=""53f474361450bae0e220c90a07328d08.png""></div>"
"<b>Catalysts </b>change the rates of reactions by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;the&#x20;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/different]</span> factor for its <u>forward</u> &amp; <u>backwards</u> direction."	"<b>Catalysts </b>change the rates of reactions by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>the same</i></span> factor for its <u>forward</u> &amp; <u>backwards</u> direction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If it doubles the speed of proceeding forward, it also doubles the speed of it reversing.</div><div><br></div><img src=""53f474361450bae0e220c90a07328d08.png""></div>"
"Rate equations for the <i>disappearance </i>of <b>reactants</b> have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""negative"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> signs."	"Rate equations for the <i>disappearance </i>of <b>reactants</b> have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">negative</span> signs.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The small letters represent stoichiometric coefficients from the equation (moles), since - depending on its coefficient - it might proceed at faster or slower rate relative to the others.</div><div>For the reaction below, the rate equation for the <u>disappearance of reactants</u>...<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""240779a3bb985073b4586db731d21aeb.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""e60a16e464072193a2e52391e4e2d6e0.png""></div>"
"<u>Rate equations</u> for the <i>appearance </i>of <b>products&nbsp;</b>have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""positive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span> signs."	"<u>Rate equations</u> for the <i>appearance </i>of <b>products&nbsp;</b>have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">positive</span> signs.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The small letters represent stoichiometric coefficients from the equation (moles), since - depending on its coefficient - it might proceed at faster or slower rate relative to the others.<br></div><div>For the reaction below, the rate equation for the <u>appearance of products</u>...</div><div><img src=""240779a3bb985073b4586db731d21aeb.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""e60a16e464072193a2e52391e4e2d6e0.png""></div>"
"In the below&nbsp;<b>rate law</b>, the <i>exponents </i>are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rate&#x20;orders"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<div><br></div><div><img src=""bb27cbaf76e7dddbd2dd8d945375db6c.png""><br></div>"	"In the below&nbsp;<b>rate law</b>, the <i>exponents </i>are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">rate orders</span>.<div><br></div><div><img src=""bb27cbaf76e7dddbd2dd8d945375db6c.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The exponents are <b><u>almost never</u></b> stoichiometric coefficients (unless it's an elementary reaction).</div><div>- The <u>overall</u> rate order of the reaction is the <u>sum</u> of each reactant's rate order.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""851709f6829eeb6b8f1240fc1f73741d.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""bb27cbaf76e7dddbd2dd8d945375db6c.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;equilibrium&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is the <i>same </i>as the <u><i>forward </i>rate constant</u> <u>(</u>k<u>)</u> divided by the <i style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">backwards </i><u>rate constant</u> <u>(</u>k<sub style=""""><b>-</b>1</sub><u>)</u>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is the <i>same </i>as the <u><i>forward </i>rate constant</u> <u>(</u>k<u>)</u> divided by the <i style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">backwards </i><u>rate constant</u> <u>(</u>k<sub style=""""><b>-</b>1</sub><u>)</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Shows two different <b>rate equations (k<sub>f</sub> &amp; k<sub>r</sub>)</b>. Dividing k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub> tells you the dynamic equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>).</div><div>- k<sub>f</sub> &amp; k<sub>r</sub> are set equal to each other because at equilibrium, the rates of forward and backward are the same.</div><div><br></div><img src=""be0964ed9732f1bf43ad4898519a05a2.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c602a260f1a06043a1aaea43d6198d8a.png""><br></div></div>"
"Review how to determine <b>rate orders</b> using <u>experimental data</u>...<div><br></div><div><a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VXfbzwAv2Dc""><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""https&#x3A;&#x2F;&#x2F;www&#x2E;youtube&#x2E;com&#x2F;watch&#x3F;v&#x3D;VXfbzwAv2Dc"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></a>&nbsp;(skip to 1:35)<br></div>"	"Review how to determine <b>rate orders</b> using <u>experimental data</u>...<div><br></div><div><a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VXfbzwAv2Dc""><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VXfbzwAv2Dc</span></a>&nbsp;(skip to 1:35)<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""deda5287645bfcb8ac9c024730a4c595.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""82b693fdde1f256fa671fb963eab9915.png""></div><div></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rate&#x20;order"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> a particular <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reactant&#x20;&#x28;&#x5B;B&#x5D;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> has using experimental data:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""99b2641c90cae27c1fba7a8129aec6d5.png""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation to determine the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">rate order</span> a particular <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">reactant ([B])</span> has using experimental data:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""99b2641c90cae27c1fba7a8129aec6d5.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""82b693fdde1f256fa671fb963eab9915.png""></div>"
"<div>This represents a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;zero&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&lt;b&gt;order&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[order]</span>&nbsp;reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png"">"	"<div>This represents a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>zero&nbsp;</b><b>order</b></span>&nbsp;reaction.</div><div><br></div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Notice how it's linear when plotting [A] vs. time (difference between this and 1<sup>st</sup>/2<sup>nd</sup> order)...</div><div>- Zero order reactions are <u>linear</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""></div>"
"Reactions where changes to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">concentrations</span> of [A] or [B] have <i><u>no effect</u></i> on the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">rate</span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;zero&#x20;order&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[order]</span>."	"Reactions where changes to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">concentrations</span> of [A] or [B] have <i><u>no effect</u></i> on the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">rate</span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>zero order</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Zero order reactions are <u>linear</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions where changes to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""concentrations"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of [A] or [B] have <i><u>no effect</u></i> on the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rate"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>zero order</b></span>."	"Reactions where changes to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">concentrations</span> of [A] or [B] have <i><u>no effect</u></i> on the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">rate</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>zero order</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Zero order reactions are <u>linear</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Zero order</b> reactions can&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;changed&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;be&#x20;first&#x20;or&#x20;second&#x20;order"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> by <i>increasing </i>the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> or <i>adding </i>a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>catalyst</u></span>."	"<b>Zero order</b> reactions can&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>changed</i> to be first or second order</span> by <i>increasing </i>the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> or <i>adding </i>a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>catalyst</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the <b>rate constant (k)</b> is dependent on temperature &amp; E<sub>activation</sub> (Arrhenius equation).</div><div>- Zero order reactions are <u>linear</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Zero order</b> reactions can&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>changed</i> to be first or second order</span> by <i>increasing </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;temperature&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> or <i>adding </i>a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;catalyst&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Zero order</b> reactions can&nbsp;be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>changed</i> to be first or second order</span> by <i>increasing </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> or <i>adding </i>a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>catalyst</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the <b>rate constant (k)</b> is dependent on temperature &amp; E<sub>activation</sub> (Arrhenius equation).</div><div>- Zero order reactions are <u>linear</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>zero order</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>reactions, the slope is equal in magnitude and&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/opposite]</span> in sign from the&nbsp;<u>rate constant (k)</u>&nbsp;when plotting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">[A]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">time</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>zero order</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>reactions, the slope is equal in magnitude and&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign from the&nbsp;<u>rate constant (k)</u>&nbsp;when plotting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">[A]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">time</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Zero order</b> reactions are linear.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;zero&#x20;order&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[order]</span><b>&nbsp;</b>reactions, the slope is equal in magnitude and&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign from the&nbsp;<u>rate constant (k)</u>&nbsp;when plotting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">[A]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">time</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>zero order</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>reactions, the slope is equal in magnitude and&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign from the&nbsp;<u>rate constant (k)</u>&nbsp;when plotting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">[A]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">time</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Zero order</b> reactions are linear.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions where <i>changes </i>to the concentration of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>only 1</i></span> reactant affect the <u>reaction's rate</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;first&#x20;order&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[order]</span>."	"Reactions where <i>changes </i>to the concentration of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>only 1</i></span> reactant affect the <u>reaction's rate</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>first order</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""fd9c32b7531ff4806abc933a5a490f02.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c62415e35786dcda17ffb406b16c8cc0.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions where <i>changes </i>to the concentration of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;only&#x20;1&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[1/multiple]</span> reactant affect the <u>reaction's rate</u> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>first order</b></span>."	"Reactions where <i>changes </i>to the concentration of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>only 1</i></span> reactant affect the <u>reaction's rate</u> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>first order</b></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""fd9c32b7531ff4806abc933a5a490f02.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c62415e35786dcda17ffb406b16c8cc0.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Radioactive decay</b> is <i>always </i>a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""first&#x20;order"" data-ordinal=""1"">[order]</span> reaction."	"<b>Radioactive decay</b> is <i>always </i>a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">first order</span> reaction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In radioactive decay, changes to the concentration of 1 single reactant (decaying material itself) result in changes to the reaction's rate.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""hqdefault-9798dc6ee61932fbb5539dd3d0e9d117b1ac3000.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <u>concentration</u> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decaying </i><b>radioactive substance</b></span>&nbsp;left over after some amount of time (t):<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <u>concentration</u> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decaying </i><b>radioactive substance</b></span>&nbsp;left over after some amount of time (t):<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Radioactive decay is always <b style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">first order</b><span style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">,</span>&nbsp;since its reaction rate is only affected by the concentration of 1 single molecule (the decaying material itself).</div><div>- k is the same as <b>λ</b> (decay constant... 0.7/T<sub>1/2</sub>).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""hqdefault-9798dc6ee61932fbb5539dd3d0e9d117b1ac3000.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <u>concentration</u> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decaying&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;b&gt;radioactive&#x20;substance&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;left over after some amount of time (t):<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <u>concentration</u> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decaying </i><b>radioactive substance</b></span>&nbsp;left over after some amount of time (t):<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Radioactive decay is always <b style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">first order</b><span style=""text-decoration-line: underline;"">,</span>&nbsp;since its reaction rate is only affected by the concentration of 1 single molecule (the decaying material itself).</div><div>- k is the same as <b>λ</b> (decay constant... 0.7/T<sub>1/2</sub>).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e341f4b11043b7942de48db6cbeff2ca.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""hqdefault-9798dc6ee61932fbb5539dd3d0e9d117b1ac3000.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- First order since it's a slope with an opposite sign to k while plotting ln([A]) vs. time (zero order instead plots [A] vs. time).</div><div><br></div><img src=""fd9c32b7531ff4806abc933a5a490f02.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c62415e35786dcda17ffb406b16c8cc0.png""></div></div>"

"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>zero order</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>reactions, the slope is equal in magnitude and&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign from the&nbsp;<u>rate constant (k)</u>&nbsp;when plotting <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x5B;A&#x5D;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""time"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>zero order</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>reactions, the slope is equal in magnitude and&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> in sign from the&nbsp;<u>rate constant (k)</u>&nbsp;when plotting <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">[A]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">time</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Zero order</b> reactions are linear.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""06bfeb141df8c388eda5912c089b73eb.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""1b34213e0f5d933dbd8f69c74511a305.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions where&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>to the concentration of <i>either </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""both&#x20;reactants"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> or the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""square&#x20;of&#x20;one&#x20;reactant"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> affects the reaction rate are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second order</b></span>."	"Reactions where&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>to the concentration of <i>either </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">both reactants</span> or the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">square of one reactant</span> affects the reaction rate are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second order</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Key distinction is that second plots&nbsp;<b>1/([A]) vs. time</b>.</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""33b41e475bf2466e3e0909b003cd8c69.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""d736a1e5ecc54db1fde1dafe8b08e20a.png""><br></div>"
"Reactions where&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>to the concentration of <i>either </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">both reactants</span> or the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">square of one reactant</span> affects the reaction rate are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;second&#x20;order&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[order]</span>."	"Reactions where&nbsp;<i>changes&nbsp;</i>to the concentration of <i>either </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">both reactants</span> or the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">square of one reactant</span> affects the reaction rate are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second order</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Key distinction is that second plots&nbsp;<b>1/([A]) vs. time</b>.</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""33b41e475bf2466e3e0909b003cd8c69.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""d736a1e5ecc54db1fde1dafe8b08e20a.png""><br></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Second&#x20;order&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[order]</span> reaction rates are dictated by the <u>collisions</u> between <i>multiple </i>molecules (either 2 of the same or two different reactants).<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Second order</b></span> reaction rates are dictated by the <u>collisions</u> between <i>multiple </i>molecules (either 2 of the same or two different reactants).<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>- Key distinction is that second plots&nbsp;<b>1/([A]) vs. time</b>.</div></div><div>- This is in opposition to <b>first order</b> reactions, where only 1 single reactant dictates rate (like radioactive decay).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""33b41e475bf2466e3e0909b003cd8c69.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""d736a1e5ecc54db1fde1dafe8b08e20a.png""><br></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Mixed&#x20;order&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>reactions </b>are those that&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>change </i>in <u>rate order</u></span>&nbsp;as the reaction progresses."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mixed order</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are those that&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>change </i>in <u>rate order</u></span>&nbsp;as the reaction progresses.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Ex: reaction starts as a first order reaction but later progresses to second order.</div><div>- <b>A</b> = single reactant; <b>C</b> = catalyst</div><div><br></div><img src=""e1aedf524b550603c1ee4b34245e8165.png""></div>"
"A <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;dynamic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>equilibrium </b>is when the rates of <u>forward</u> &amp; <u>backward</u> reactions are <i>equal </i>- leading to no <i>net</i> change."	"A <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dynamic</b></span> <b>equilibrium </b>is when the rates of <u>forward</u> &amp; <u>backward</u> reactions are <i>equal </i>- leading to no <i>net</i> change.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Shows how at dynamic equilibrium, it's possible for the reactants and products to be at different concentrations.</div><div><br></div><img src=""df6e9cd0a0148a6e303c6cb5e9a5b03c.png""></div>"
"<div>After reaching <b>dynamic equilibrium</b>, the <i>concentrations </i>of the reactants and products <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> be <u>unequal</u>.<br></div>"	"<div>After reaching <b>dynamic equilibrium</b>, the <i>concentrations </i>of the reactants and products <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> be <u>unequal</u>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The only thing at equilibrium that's the exact same are the <u>rates</u> of forward and backwards reactions - NOT concentrations.</div><div><br></div><img src=""df6e9cd0a0148a6e303c6cb5e9a5b03c.png""></div>"
"<div>As a chemical reaction proceeds towards its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;equilibrium&#x20;point&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>entropy</b></span> always <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>.<br></div>"	"<div>As a chemical reaction proceeds towards its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>equilibrium point</u></span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>entropy</b></span> always <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- At equilibrium, entropy is maximized since the <b>system's energy</b> is <u>evenly distributed</u>.<br></div></div>"
"<div>As a chemical reaction proceeds towards its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>equilibrium point</u></span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;entropy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[thermo. property]</span> always <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>.<br></div>"	"<div>As a chemical reaction proceeds towards its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>equilibrium point</u></span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>entropy</b></span> always <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- At equilibrium, entropy is maximized since the <b>system's energy</b> is <u>evenly distributed</u>.<br></div></div>"
"<div>As a chemical reaction proceeds towards its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>equilibrium point</u></span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>entropy</b></span> always <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[increases/decreases]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>As a chemical reaction proceeds towards its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>equilibrium point</u></span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>entropy</b></span> always <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- At equilibrium, entropy is maximized since the <b>system's energy</b> is <u>evenly distributed</u>.<br></div></div>"
"The <u>equilibrium constant</u> is symbolized by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""K&lt;sub&gt;p&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> when dealing with <b>gases</b>, since it's in terms of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""partial&#x20;pressure"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The <u>equilibrium constant</u> is symbolized by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">K<sub>p</sub></span> when dealing with <b>gases</b>, since it's in terms of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">partial pressure</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

In contrast, K<sub>eq</sub> (same as K<sub>c</sub>) is in terms of concentration (hence ""c"").</div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span> at <i>dynamic </i>equilibrium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;48bf4c5fa25222bde9718ed1e1fc5324&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span> at <i>dynamic </i>equilibrium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""48bf4c5fa25222bde9718ed1e1fc5324.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""4b29b68b750055123d78fdf9d2cb8834.png""></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;equilibrium&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> at <i>dynamic </i>equilibrium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""48bf4c5fa25222bde9718ed1e1fc5324.png""></span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span> at <i>dynamic </i>equilibrium:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""48bf4c5fa25222bde9718ed1e1fc5324.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""4b29b68b750055123d78fdf9d2cb8834.png""></div>"
"<div>In the expression for <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> at dynamic equilibrium, the <i>superscripts</i> are each reactant's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""stoichiometric&#x20;coefficient&#x20;&#x28;moles&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>In the expression for <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> at dynamic equilibrium, the <i>superscripts</i> are each reactant's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">stoichiometric coefficient (moles)</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""4b29b68b750055123d78fdf9d2cb8834.png""></div>"
"In a reversible reaction, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;reaction&#x20;quotient&#x20;&#x28;Q&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> measures the ratio of <u>products</u> to <u>reactants</u> <i>before </i>reaching equilibrium (K<sub>eq</sub>)."	"In a reversible reaction, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>reaction quotient (Q)</b></span> measures the ratio of <u>products</u> to <u>reactants</u> <i>before </i>reaching equilibrium (K<sub>eq</sub>).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div><b>Reaction quotient (Q)</b>&nbsp;is useful when&nbsp;<u>compared</u>&nbsp;to&nbsp;<b>K<sub>eq</sub></b>&nbsp;to figure out which direction the reaction will move.</div><div><br></div><div>According to&nbsp;<b><u>LeChatlier's principle</u></b>...</div><div>- Q &lt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows there's currently more reactants... will proceed forward.</div><div>- Q = K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows that the reaction is at equilibrium.</div><div>- Q &gt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows there's currently more product... will proceed backwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e03652c8b9b055b32df188b471e11c38.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""></div></div></div>"
"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;greater&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[greater/less]</span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>backwards</u></span>."	"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater</i></span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>backwards</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Reaction quotient (Q)</b> is useful when <u>compared</u> to <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> to figure out which direction the reaction will move.</div><div><br></div><div>According to <b><u>LeChatlier's principle</u></b>...</div><div>- Q &lt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;says the reaction&nbsp;will proceed forward.</div><div>- Q = K<sub>eq</sub> shows that the reaction is at equilibrium.</div><div>- Q &gt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;says the reaction&nbsp;will proceed backwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e03652c8b9b055b32df188b471e11c38.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""><br></div></div>"
"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater</i></span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;backwards&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[forwards/backwards]</span>."	"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater</i></span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>backwards</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Reaction quotient (Q)</b> is useful when <u>compared</u> to <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> to figure out which direction the reaction will move.</div><div><br></div><div>According to <b><u>LeChatlier's principle</u></b>...</div><div>- Q &lt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;says the reaction&nbsp;will proceed forward.</div><div>- Q = K<sub>eq</sub> shows that the reaction is at equilibrium.</div><div>- Q &gt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;says the reaction&nbsp;will proceed backwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e03652c8b9b055b32df188b471e11c38.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""><br></div></div>"
"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[greater/less]</span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>forwards</u></span>."	"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>forwards</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Reaction quotient (Q)</b> is useful when <u>compared</u> to <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> to figure out which direction the reaction will move.</div><div><br></div><div>According to <b><u>LeChatlier's principle</u></b>...</div><div>- Q &lt; K<sub>eq</sub> says the reaction will proceed forward.</div><div>- Q = K<sub>eq</sub> shows that the reaction is at equilibrium.</div><div>- Q &gt; K<sub>eq&nbsp;</sub>says the reaction will proceed backwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e03652c8b9b055b32df188b471e11c38.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""><br></div></div>"
"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;forwards&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[forwards/backwards]</span>."	"In a reversible reaction, a <b>reaction quotient (Q)</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <i>than </i><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> causes the reaction to proceed <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>forwards</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Reaction quotient (Q)</b> is useful when <u>compared</u> to <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> to figure out which direction the reaction will move.</div><div><br></div><div>According to <b><u>LeChatlier's principle</u></b>...</div><div>- Q &lt; K<sub>eq</sub> says the reaction will proceed forward.</div><div>- Q = K<sub>eq</sub> shows that the reaction is at equilibrium.</div><div>- Q &gt; K<sub>eq&nbsp;</sub>says the reaction will proceed backwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e03652c8b9b055b32df188b471e11c38.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions proceed <i>towards </i><b>dynamic equilibrium</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""spontaneously"" data-ordinal=""1"">[spont./non-spont.]</span>, but <i>leave </i>equilibrium <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""non&#x2D;spontaneously"" data-ordinal=""1"">[spont./non-spont.]</span>."	"Reactions proceed <i>towards </i><b>dynamic equilibrium</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">spontaneously</span>, but <i>leave </i>equilibrium <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">non-spontaneously</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Doesn't take energy to move towards equilibrium (spontaneous), but to <u>leave</u> equilibrium requires energy (non-spontaneous) in order to form more products/reactants.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>To find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant</b>&nbsp;(<b>K<sub>eq</sub>)</b> for the <i>reverse </i>direction</span>&nbsp;in a reaction, take the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;reciprocal&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x28;1&#x2F;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>To find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant</b>&nbsp;(<b>K<sub>eq</sub>)</b> for the <i>reverse </i>direction</span>&nbsp;in a reaction, take the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>reciprocal</u> of K<sub>eq</sub> (1/K<sub>eq</sub>)</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because K<sub>eq</sub> = k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub>. If you want the inverse (i.e. to go backwards), you'll have to take the inverse of the equation (1/K<sub>eq</sub> = <b>k<sub>reverse</sub>/k<sub>forward</sub></b>).</div><div>- Remember, k represents the reaction constant (k) (related to kinetic collision theory).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""><br></div></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>p</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;of gases:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;Screen&#x2D;Shot&#x2D;2014&#x2D;07&#x2D;23&#x2D;at&#x2D;10&#x2E;55&#x2E;15&#x2D;AM&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>p</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;of gases:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>""P"" represents that we're looking at <b><u>partial pressures</u></b> when dealing with gases - NOT concentrations like we would with K<sub>eq</sub>.</div><div>- Exponents are stoichiometric coefficients</div></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;equilibrium&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;p&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of gases:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""></span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>p</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;of gases:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>""P"" represents that we're looking at <b><u>partial pressures</u></b> when dealing with gases - NOT concentrations like we would with K<sub>eq</sub>.</div><div>- Exponents are stoichiometric coefficients</div></div>"
"<i>Increasing </i>or <i>decreasing</i> the <u>volume</u><i> </i>of a gaseous system <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> alter the reaction's <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>p</sub>)</b>."	"<i>Increasing </i>or <i>decreasing</i> the <u>volume</u><i> </i>of a gaseous system <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does</i></span> alter the reaction's <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>p</sub>)</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since volume is inversely related to pressure. The equilibrium constant (K<sub>p</sub>) for gases looks at the partial pressures of each gas...so it changes.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""></div>"
"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreased</i></span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;moles&#x20;of&#x20;gas"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreased</i></span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less </i>moles of gas</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b style=""""><u>LeChatelier's principle</u></b></div><div>- Counteracts&nbsp;the increased&nbsp;<u>pressure</u></div><div>- LeChatelier's principle says systems point in the direction opposite of the increased stress. ""Stress"" comes from increased pressure... shifts to negate that by shifting towards less moles (thereby countering pressure).</div><div>- <b>PV = nRT</b> shows that P and n are directly proportional.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8f9cfc846cd4435a5aa8fd299566aa2b.png""><br></div></div>"
"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreased&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[inc/dec]</span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less </i>moles of gas</span>."	"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreased</i></span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less </i>moles of gas</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b style=""""><u>LeChatelier's principle</u></b></div><div>- Counteracts&nbsp;the increased&nbsp;<u>pressure</u></div><div>- LeChatelier's principle says systems point in the direction opposite of the increased stress. ""Stress"" comes from increased pressure... shifts to negate that by shifting towards less moles (thereby countering pressure).</div><div>- <b>PV = nRT</b> shows that P and n are directly proportional.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8f9cfc846cd4435a5aa8fd299566aa2b.png""><br></div></div>"
"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increased</i></span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;moles&#x20;of&#x20;gas"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increased</i></span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more&nbsp;</i>moles of gas</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- LeChatelier's principle says systems point in the direction opposite of the increased stress. ""Stress"" comes from decreased pressure... shifts to negate that by shifting towards more moles (thereby increasing pressure back).</div><div>- <b>PV = nRT</b> shows that P and n are directly proportional.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8f9cfc846cd4435a5aa8fd299566aa2b.png""><br></div></div>"
"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increased&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[inc/dec]</span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more&nbsp;</i>moles of gas</span>."	"When the <u>volume</u> of a gaseous container is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increased</i></span>, the <b>equilibrium </b><i>shifts</i> towards the side with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more&nbsp;</i>moles of gas</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- LeChatelier's principle says systems point in the direction opposite of the increased stress. ""Stress"" comes from decreased pressure... shifts to negate that by shifting towards more moles (thereby increasing pressure back).</div><div>- <b>PV = nRT</b> shows that P and n are directly proportional.</div><div><br></div><img src=""Screen-Shot-2014-07-23-at-10.55.15-AM.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8f9cfc846cd4435a5aa8fd299566aa2b.png""><br></div></div>"
"Changing the <u>temperature</u> or <u>pressure</u> of a system changes <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;itself"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;- <i>not </i>the reaction quotient (Q)."	"Changing the <u>temperature</u> or <u>pressure</u> of a system changes <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> itself</span>&nbsp;- <i>not </i>the reaction quotient (Q).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- In contrast, changing <b>concentration </b>(i.e. adding reactants or products) or changing <b>pressure/volume </b>(for gases) only affects the reaction quotient (Q).</div><div>- K<sub>eq</sub> is k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub>... rate constants (k) are calculated using temperature (<b>Arrhenius </b>equation).</div><div><br></div><img src=""maxresdefault-5fc28fe7a04da769507b3b0db8c62ce888bac5d3.jpg""></div>"
"According to LeChatelier's principle, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreasing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[inc/dec]</span> <b>temperature </b>shifts the reaction towards whichever side is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>exothermic</u></span>."	"According to LeChatelier's principle, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreasing</i></span> <b>temperature </b>shifts the reaction towards whichever side is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>exothermic</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Tries to ""counteract"" this decreased temperature by <u>releasing</u> some of its own (hence endothermic).</div><div>- Endothermic could be either forwards or backwards... depends on the reaction graph (be careful... don't confuse ΔG graph with PE graph)</div><div><br></div><img src=""maxresdefault-5fc28fe7a04da769507b3b0db8c62ce888bac5d3.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8cd4b2ea8d26b3592c2383732aea7ebb.png""><br></div></div>"
"According to LeChatelier's principle, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreasing</i></span> <b>temperature </b>shifts the reaction towards whichever side is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;exothermic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"According to LeChatelier's principle, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreasing</i></span> <b>temperature </b>shifts the reaction towards whichever side is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>exothermic</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Tries to ""counteract"" this decreased temperature by <u>releasing</u> some of its own (hence endothermic).</div><div>- Endothermic could be either forwards or backwards... depends on the reaction graph (be careful... don't confuse ΔG graph with PE graph)</div><div><br></div><img src=""maxresdefault-5fc28fe7a04da769507b3b0db8c62ce888bac5d3.jpg""><div><br></div><div><img src=""8cd4b2ea8d26b3592c2383732aea7ebb.png""><br></div></div>"
"In a reaction, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;kinetic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/thermodynamic]</span> <b>product </b>is favored when temperature is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span>."	"In a reaction, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> <b>product </b>is favored when temperature is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If temperatures were high and it could ""afford"" to spend more energy on a nicer, more stable product, it will. Otherwise, quickly forms the somewhat less stable product.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"
"In a reaction, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> <b>product </b>is favored when temperature is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[high/low]</span>."	"In a reaction, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> <b>product </b>is favored when temperature is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span>.
<hr>
