<div>- If temperatures were high and it could ""afford"" to spend more energy on a nicer, more stable product, it will. Otherwise, quickly forms the somewhat less stable product.</div><div>- Product is more stable because its free energy is lower.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-47d5ab0c8662d2c532f6347cbfedf00de4095e3b.jpg""><br></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>internal energy (U)</b></span> of a&nbsp;<u>reacting&nbsp;system</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>internal energy (U)</b></span> of a&nbsp;<u>reacting&nbsp;system</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is a restatement of the first law of thermodynamics, which says that the energy of a system is always conserved.</div>- U and <u>temeprature</u> are directly proportional.<div>- <b>Q</b> = heat that's added <i>to </i>the system; <b>W</b> = work performed <i>by </i>the system.</div><div><br></div><div>- This equation applies to the surrounding system (environment), not the reaction (process) itself.</div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;internal&#x20;energy&#x20;&#x28;U&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of a&nbsp;<u>reacting&nbsp;system</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>internal energy (U)</b></span> of a&nbsp;<u>reacting&nbsp;system</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is a restatement of the first law of thermodynamics, which says that the energy of a system is always conserved.</div>- U and <u>temeprature</u> are directly proportional.<div>- <b>Q</b> = heat that's added <i>to </i>the system; <b>W</b> = work performed <i>by </i>the system.</div><div><br></div><div>- This equation applies to the surrounding system (environment), not the reaction (process) itself.</div></div>"
"<div>In thermochemistry, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;surrounding&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the <i>external </i>environment and the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;system&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the <i>reaction </i>causing changes to the environment's properties.</div>"	"<div>In thermochemistry, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>surrounding</b></span> is the <i>external </i>environment and the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>system</b></span> is the <i>reaction </i>causing changes to the environment's properties.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: a hot coffee cup (system) changes its temperature due to interactions with surroundings.</div></div>"
"The 4 main types of <b>thermochemical processes</b> that cause <i>changes </i>in the <u>system's properties</u>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Isothermal"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Adiabatic"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Isobaric"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>4. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Isovolumetric&#x20;&#x28;isochoric&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"The 4 main types of <b>thermochemical processes</b> that cause <i>changes </i>in the <u>system's properties</u>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Isothermal</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Adiabatic</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Isobaric</span></div><div>4. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Isovolumetric (isochoric)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- In each of these processes, the system holds 1 of its properties constant while the others change</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""pvdiagram.JPG""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Isothermal&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>processes </b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> remains constant, so the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added <i>to</i> (Q)</u></span>&nbsp;the system <i>cancels out</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>work done <i>by </i>(W)</u></span> the system."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isothermal</b></span> <b>processes </b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> remains constant, so the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added <i>to</i> (Q)</u></span>&nbsp;the system <i>cancels out</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>work done <i>by </i>(W)</u></span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The system's internal energy (U) and its temperature are <u>directly proportional</u>. For U to remain unchanged, Q and W must balance out.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""eb6c31869c4d7229ef3d61ca34c53e3d.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isothermal</b></span> <b>processes </b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;temperature&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> remains constant, so the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added <i>to</i> (Q)</u></span>&nbsp;the system <i>cancels out</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>work done <i>by </i>(W)</u></span> the system."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isothermal</b></span> <b>processes </b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> remains constant, so the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added <i>to</i> (Q)</u></span>&nbsp;the system <i>cancels out</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>work done <i>by </i>(W)</u></span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The system's internal energy (U) and its temperature are <u>directly proportional</u>. For U to remain unchanged, Q and W must balance out.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""eb6c31869c4d7229ef3d61ca34c53e3d.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isothermal</b></span> <b>processes </b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> remains constant, so the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;heat&#x20;added&#x20;&lt;i&gt;to&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&#x28;Q&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;the system <i>cancels out</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;work&#x20;done&#x20;&lt;i&gt;by&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x28;W&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> the system."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isothermal</b></span> <b>processes </b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> remains constant, so the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added <i>to</i> (Q)</u></span>&nbsp;the system <i>cancels out</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>work done <i>by </i>(W)</u></span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The system's internal energy (U) and its temperature are <u>directly proportional</u>. For U to remain unchanged, Q and W must balance out.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""eb6c31869c4d7229ef3d61ca34c53e3d.png""><br></div></div>"
"On a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""isothermal"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""adiabatic"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> processes are <i>hyperbolic</i>."	"On a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">isothermal</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">adiabatic</span> processes are <i>hyperbolic</i>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>P vs. V graphs use <b>PV = nRT</b>... hence why changing the values of T results in hyperbolic graphs (results in different P and V values).</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""pvdiagram.JPG""><br></div></div>"
"On a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""isobaric"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""isovolumetric"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> processes are <i>linear</i>."	"On a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">isobaric</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">isovolumetric</span> processes are <i>linear</i>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""pvdiagram.JPG""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Adiabatic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<b>processes&nbsp;</b>are when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>no </i>heat is exchanged</u><u>&nbsp;(Q=0)</u></span> between the system &amp; the surroundings, so changes to the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">internal energy (ΔU)</span> are due<i> </i>to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">work done <i>to</i> (-W)</span> the system."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Adiabatic</b></span>&nbsp;<b>processes&nbsp;</b>are when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>no </i>heat is exchanged</u><u>&nbsp;(Q=0)</u></span> between the system &amp; the surroundings, so changes to the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">internal energy (ΔU)</span> are due<i> </i>to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">work done <i>to</i> (-W)</span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Dotted lines show how different temperature values can give you different lines (since adiabatic processes can have varying temperatures).</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""27a726866a0322da0c307f017e26d73f.png""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Dotted lines show how different temperature values can give you different lines (since adiabatic processes can have varying temperatures).</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""27a726866a0322da0c307f017e26d73f.png""><br></div></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Dotted lines show how different temperature values can give you different lines (since adiabatic processes can have varying temperatures).</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""27a726866a0322da0c307f017e26d73f.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Isobaric&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<b>processes&nbsp;</b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressure</u></span> is constant - leaving the internal energy equation <i>unchanged</i> (U = Q-W)."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isobaric</b></span>&nbsp;<b>processes&nbsp;</b>are when the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressure</u></span> is constant - leaving the internal energy equation <i>unchanged</i> (U = Q-W).

<div>P vs. V graph is linear since pressure is held constant.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3f5b5e47b79c133d83e966727b0ce12a.png""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>P vs. V graph is linear since pressure is held constant.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3f5b5e47b79c133d83e966727b0ce12a.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;isothermal&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>processes</b>, the area under the curve of a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">work done <i>by&nbsp;</i>(W)</span> the system."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isothermal</b></span> <b>processes</b>, the area under the curve of a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">work done <i>by&nbsp;</i>(W)</span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Represents work done by the system since Q=W, and W represents work done <i>by </i>the system.</div><div><br></div><div>- Can also represent <b>Q</b> since temperature is constant (making U=0), meaning Q=W (area under the curve can represent either Q or W).</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""eb6c31869c4d7229ef3d61ca34c53e3d.png""><br></div></div>"

"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isothermal</b></span> <b>processes</b>, the area under the curve of a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">work done <i>by&nbsp;</i>(W)</span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Represents work done by the system since Q=W, and W represents work done <i>by </i>the system.</div><div><br></div><div>- Can also represent <b>Q</b> since temperature is constant (making U=0), meaning Q=W (area under the curve can represent either Q or W).</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""eb6c31869c4d7229ef3d61ca34c53e3d.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;adiabatic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<b>processes</b>, the area under the curve of a&nbsp;<u>pressure vs. volume</u>&nbsp;graph represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">work done&nbsp;<i>to&nbsp;</i>(-W)</span> the system."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>adiabatic</b></span>&nbsp;<b>processes</b>, the area under the curve of a&nbsp;<u>pressure vs. volume</u>&nbsp;graph represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">work done&nbsp;<i>to&nbsp;</i>(-W)</span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Represents work done <i>to </i>(-W) the system since Q = 0, meaning U = -W. W normally is work done <i>by </i>the system, so the negative is work done <i>to </i>the system.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""27a726866a0322da0c307f017e26d73f.png""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>adiabatic</b></span>&nbsp;<b>processes</b>, the area under the curve of a&nbsp;<u>pressure vs. volume</u>&nbsp;graph represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""work&#x20;done&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;i&gt;to&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x28;&#x2D;W&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> the system."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>adiabatic</b></span>&nbsp;<b>processes</b>, the area under the curve of a&nbsp;<u>pressure vs. volume</u>&nbsp;graph represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">work done&nbsp;<i>to&nbsp;</i>(-W)</span> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Represents work done <i>to </i>(-W) the system since Q = 0, meaning U = -W. W normally is work done <i>by </i>the system, so the negative is work done <i>to </i>the system.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""27a726866a0322da0c307f017e26d73f.png""><br></div></div>"
"The slope of an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;adiabatic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>process </b>on a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph is <i>steeper</i> than isothermal processes, since they usually have higher pressures."	"The slope of an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>adiabatic</b></span> <b>process </b>on a <u>pressure vs. volume</u> graph is <i>steeper</i> than isothermal processes, since they usually have higher pressures.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- In each of these processes, the system holds 1 of its properties constant while the others change</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not absorb heat with the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""pvdiagram.JPG""></div></div>"
"<div>In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressure"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> vs. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""volume"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> graphs are used to represent the effects certain changes have on a <u>gas' properties</u> in an internal system.</div>"	"<div>In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pressure</span> vs. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">volume</span> graphs are used to represent the effects certain changes have on a <u>gas' properties</u> in an internal system.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Doesn't mean thermochemistry only applies to gases... the ideal gas law ties together pressure, temp., and volume into 1 equation so it's easier to use.</div><div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""pvdiagram.JPG""></div></div>"
"<div>A <u>system</u> has 3 <b>thermodynamic variables </b>- 1 of which is held constant while the other 2 change:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Pressure"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Volume"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Temperature"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>A <u>system</u> has 3 <b>thermodynamic variables </b>- 1 of which is held constant while the other 2 change:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Pressure</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Volume</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Temperature</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>PV = nRT</b> is used to represent how these things affect each other, so gases are used out of convenience's sake.</div><div><br></div><div><div>4 different processes that describe how these variables interact with each other...</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""pvdiagram.JPG""></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Isovolumetric&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>processes </b>are when the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> of the gas is constant, meaning the <u>system</u> performs&nbsp;<i>no work</i>&nbsp;(W=0), so the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>internal energy</u></span> <i>only </i>depends on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added (Q)</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isovolumetric</b></span> <b>processes </b>are when the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> of the gas is constant, meaning the <u>system</u> performs&nbsp;<i>no work</i>&nbsp;(W=0), so the system's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>internal energy</u></span> <i>only </i>depends on <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added (Q)</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""30l.GIF""><br></div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Isovolumetric</b></span> <b>processes </b>are when the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> of the gas is constant, meaning the <u>system</u> performs&nbsp;<i>no work</i>&nbsp;(W=0), so the system's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>internal energy</u></span> <i>only </i>depends on <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>heat added (Q)</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""30l.GIF""><br></div></div>"
"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>isovolumetric</b></span> processes are <i>also </i>called <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;isochoric&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> processes."	"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>isovolumetric</b></span> processes are <i>also </i>called <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isochoric</b></span> processes.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>4 different processes that describe how these variables interact with each other...</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""30l.GIF""><br></div></div>"
"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;state&#x20;functions&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> describe the <u>system's properties</u> <i>at </i>equilibrium - <i>not </i>the path it took to get there."	"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>state functions</b></span> describe the <u>system's properties</u> <i>at </i>equilibrium - <i>not </i>the path it took to get there.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Left </b>= process function; <b>right </b>= state function.</div><div><div>- Path functions are work (W) and heat (Q).</div></div><div><br></div><img src=""statefnc.gif""></div>"
"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;path&#x2F;process&#x20;functions&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> describe the <i>path </i>the <u>system's properties</u> took to reach its equilibrium."	"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>path/process functions</b></span> describe the <i>path </i>the <u>system's properties</u> took to reach its equilibrium.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Left </b>= process function; <b>right </b>= state function.</div><div>- Path functions are work (W) and heat (Q).</div><div><br></div><img src=""statefnc.gif""></div>"
"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>standard conditions</b></span>&nbsp;are:<div><br></div><div>Pressure = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x20;atm"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>Temperature = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""298&#x20;K&#x20;&#x28;25&#xB0;C&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>Concentration = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x20;M"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>standard conditions</b></span>&nbsp;are:<div><br></div><div>Pressure = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 atm</span></div><div>Temperature = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">298 K (25°C)</span></div><div>Concentration = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 M</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

These standard conditions are standardized because the system's final equilibrium will be different depending on what the pressure (P), temperature (T), or concentration of molecules (M) is.</div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;standard&#x20;state&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a compound is its physical state (solid, liquid, gas) where it's <u>most stable</u>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>standard state</b></span> of a compound is its physical state (solid, liquid, gas) where it's <u>most stable</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>H<sub>2</sub>O </b>standard state: liquid<div><b>H<sub>2</sub> </b>standard state: gas</div><div><b>NaCl </b>standard state: solid</div><div><b>O<sub>2</sub> </b>standard state: gas</div><div><b>Carbon </b>standard state: solid (as graphite)</div></div>"
"ΔG°, ΔS°, and ΔH° represent free energy, entropy, and enthalpy under <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""standard&#x20;conditions"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"ΔG°, ΔS°, and ΔH° represent free energy, entropy, and enthalpy under <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">standard conditions</span>.

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Evaporation&#x2F;vaporization&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[phase change]</span> occurs when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> molecules gain <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>kinetic energy</u></span> to turn into the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phase."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Evaporation/vaporization</b></span> occurs when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> molecules gain <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>kinetic energy</u></span> to turn into the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phase.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Liquid → gas</div><div>- Notice how liquid → gas occurs at higher temperatures. That's because temperature is a measure of the kinetic energy something has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Liquid → gas</div><div>- Notice how liquid → gas occurs at higher temperatures. That's because temperature is a measure of the kinetic energy something has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Evaporation/vaporization</b></span> occurs when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> molecules gain <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> to turn into the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phase."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Evaporation/vaporization</b></span> occurs when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> molecules gain <i>enough </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>kinetic energy</u></span> to turn into the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phase.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Liquid → gas</div><div>- Notice how liquid → gas occurs at higher temperatures. That's because temperature is a measure of the kinetic energy something has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<b>Evaporation/vaporization</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""endo"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>thermic from the perspective of the liquid water, since its molecules <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;absorb&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[release/absorb]</span> energy to enter the <u>gas</u> phase."	"<b>Evaporation/vaporization</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">endo</span>thermic from the perspective of the liquid water, since its molecules <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>absorb</i></span> energy to enter the <u>gas</u> phase.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Liquid → gas</div><div>- Notice how liquid → gas occurs at higher temperatures. That's because temperature is a measure of the kinetic energy something has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<b>Condensation</b> is when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""gas"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> molecules enter the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""liquid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> phase due to the <u><i>force </i>they exert</u> <i>on</i> liquid surfaces."	"<b>Condensation</b> is when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">gas</span> molecules enter the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">liquid</span> phase due to the <u><i>force </i>they exert</u> <i>on</i> liquid surfaces.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Gas → liquid</div><div>- Notice how gas → liquid is associated with decreased temperatures. That's because temperature is a measure of the kinetic energy something has (loses kinetic energy by re-entering liquid phase).</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div><b>Condensation</b> can occur by either <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreasing</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;temperature&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increasing</i></span><i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, since both encourage gas molecules to re-enter the liquid phase.</div>"	"<div><b>Condensation</b> can occur by either <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreasing</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increasing</i></span><i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u></span>, since both encourage gas molecules to re-enter the liquid phase.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Gas → liquid</div><div>- Decreased <b>temperature</b>: gas molecules <u>lose their kinetic energy</u> (re-enter liquid phase)</div><div>- Increased <b>pressure</b>: makes gas molecules <u>exert higher forces</u> on the liquid surface (causing them to re-enter the liquid phase).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div><b>Condensation</b> can occur by either <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreasing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[inc/dec]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increasing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[inc/dec]</span><i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u></span>, since both encourage gas molecules to re-enter the liquid phase.</div>"	"<div><b>Condensation</b> can occur by either <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreasing</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increasing</i></span><i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u></span>, since both encourage gas molecules to re-enter the liquid phase.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Gas → liquid</div><div>- Decreased <b>temperature</b>: gas molecules <u>lose their kinetic energy</u> (re-enter liquid phase)</div><div>- Increased <b>pressure</b>: makes gas molecules <u>exert higher forces</u> on the liquid surface (causing them to re-enter the liquid phase).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<b>Condensation</b> from gas → liquid is encouraged by <i>decreasing </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""temperature"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> since it causes gas molecules to <i>lose </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Condensation</b> from gas → liquid is encouraged by <i>decreasing </i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">temperature</span> since it causes gas molecules to <i>lose </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>kinetic energy</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Gas → liquid</div><div>- Decreased <b>temperature</b>: gas molecules <u>lose their kinetic energy</u> (re-enter liquid phase)</div><div>- Increased <b>pressure</b>: makes gas molecules <u>exert higher forces</u> on the liquid surface (causing them to re-enter the liquid phase).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<b>Condensation</b> from gas → liquid is encouraged by <i>increasing&nbsp;</i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressure"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> since it causes gas molecules to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>exert <i>higher </i>forces</u></span> on the liquid surface."	"<b>Condensation</b> from gas → liquid is encouraged by <i>increasing&nbsp;</i>the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pressure</span> since it causes gas molecules to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>exert <i>higher </i>forces</u></span> on the liquid surface.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Gas → liquid</div><div>- Decreased <b>temperature</b>: gas molecules <u>lose their kinetic energy</u> (re-enter liquid phase)</div><div>- Increased <b>pressure</b>: makes gas molecules <u>exert higher forces</u> on the liquid surface (causing them to re-enter the liquid phase).</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<b>Condensation</b> from gas → liquid is encouraged by <i>increasing&nbsp;</i>the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">pressure</span> since it causes gas molecules to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;exert&#x20;&lt;i&gt;higher&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;forces&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> on the liquid surface."	"<b>Condensation</b> from gas → liquid is encouraged by <i>increasing&nbsp;</i>the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">pressure</span> since it causes gas molecules to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>exert <i>higher </i>forces</u></span> on the liquid surface.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Gas → liquid</div><div>- Decreased <b>temperature</b>: gas molecules <u>lose their kinetic energy</u> (re-enter liquid phase)</div><div>- Increased <b>pressure</b>: makes gas molecules <u>exert higher forces</u> on the liquid surface (causing them to re-enter the liquid phase).</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Vapor&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the pressure exerted <i>by </i>gas molecules <i>on</i> the liquid surface during <u>boiling</u>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Vapor pressure</b></span> is the pressure exerted <i>by </i>gas molecules <i>on</i> the liquid surface during <u>boiling</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- High vapor pressure leads to <b>condensation </b>since it causes gas molecules to re-enter the liquid phase.</div><div>- The <b><u>boiling point</u></b> occurs when <b>vapor pressure</b> (from gas molecules) = <b>atmospheric pressure</b> (surrounding air).</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Fusion&#x2F;melting&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[phase changes]</span> occurs when molecules in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">solid</span> state <i>absorb </i>enough <u>energy</u> to transition to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> phase."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fusion/melting</b></span> occurs when molecules in the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">solid</span> state <i>absorb </i>enough <u>energy</u> to transition to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> phase.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fusion/melting</b></span> occurs when molecules in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""solid"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> state <i>absorb </i>enough <u>energy</u> to transition to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""liquid"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> phase."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Fusion/melting</b></span> occurs when molecules in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">solid</span> state <i>absorb </i>enough <u>energy</u> to transition to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> phase.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Crystallization&#x2F;freezing&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> occurs when liquid molecules <i>lose</i> enough <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">kinetic energy</span> to where they can <i>no longer</i> overcome their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermolecular forces</u></span>.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Crystallization/freezing</b></span> occurs when liquid molecules <i>lose</i> enough <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">kinetic energy</span> to where they can <i>no longer</i> overcome their <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermolecular forces</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Freezing point</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Crystallization/freezing</b></span> occurs when liquid molecules <i>lose</i> enough <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""kinetic&#x20;energy"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> to where they can <i>no longer</i> overcome their <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;intermolecular&#x20;forces&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Crystallization/freezing</b></span> occurs when liquid molecules <i>lose</i> enough <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">kinetic energy</span> to where they can <i>no longer</i> overcome their <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermolecular forces</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Freezing point</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div>The transition from a <u>liquid</u> → <u>solid</u> is marked by the substance's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;freezing&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b>&nbsp;point</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>The transition from a <u>liquid</u> → <u>solid</u> is marked by the substance's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>freezing</b></span><b>&nbsp;point</b>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Liquid molecules lose kinetic energy (due to lowered temperature) - meaning they can no longer overcome their intermolecular forces.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div>The transition from a <u>solid</u> → <u>liquid</u>&nbsp;is marked by the substance's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;melting&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b>&nbsp;point</b>.<br></div>"	"<div>The transition from a <u>solid</u> → <u>liquid</u>&nbsp;is marked by the substance's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>melting</b></span><b>&nbsp;point</b>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Solid molecules absorb enough heat from their surroundings to cause them to enter the liquid phase.</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Sublimation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[phase change]</span> is when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">solids</span> transition <i>directly </i>into the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phase.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Sublimation</b></span> is when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">solids</span> transition <i>directly </i>into the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phase.</div>
<hr>

"In organic chemistry labs, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;cold&#x20;fingers&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are used to <i>purify </i>products by causing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">pure</span> substance to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>sublimate</u></span> from <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">solid</span> → <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> - leaving <i>behind&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">impurities</span>." <div>Water's triple point is around 0°C.</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"In a phase diagram, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;critical&#x20;point&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the point where there is <u><i>no </i>discernable difference</u> between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phases."	"In a phase diagram, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>critical point</b></span> is the point where there is <u><i>no </i>discernable difference</u> between the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phases.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The <u>densities</u> of these <b>supercritical fluids</b> become equal.</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"In a phase diagram, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>critical point</b></span> is the point where there is <u><i>no </i>discernable difference</u> between the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""liquid"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""gas"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> phases."	"In a phase diagram, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>critical point</b></span> is the point where there is <u><i>no </i>discernable difference</u> between the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">liquid</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">gas</span> phases.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The <u>densities</u> of these <b>supercritical fluids</b> become equal.</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;zeroth&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> <b>law of thermodynamics</b> says that if systems B ⇌ A &amp; C ⇌ A, then B and C are <i>also </i>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>thermal equilibrium</u>&nbsp;(same temperatures)</span>." "<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Calorimetry</b></span> is the process of <i>measuring </i><u>heat transfer</u> between two substances of different temperatures due to a chemical reaction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<div>Equation that measures the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat <i>absorbed </i>or <i>released</i></b></span> by a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation that measures the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat <i>absorbed </i>or <i>released</i></b></span> by a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>m</b> = mass; <b>c</b> = substance's specific heat; <b>ΔT</b> = temp. change</div>"
"<div>Equation that measures the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;heat&#x20;&lt;i&gt;absorbed&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;or&#x20;&lt;i&gt;released&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> by a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation that measures the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat <i>absorbed </i>or <i>released</i></b></span> by a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""></span><br>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>m</b> = mass; <b>c</b> = substance's specific heat; <b>ΔT</b> = temp. change</div>"
"<div>In the equation for the <i>amount </i>of&nbsp;&nbsp;<u>heat absorbed/released</u> (q) by a system, <b>c</b> represents the substance's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""specific&#x20;heat&#x20;&#x28;c&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png"">"	"<div>In the equation for the <i>amount </i>of&nbsp;&nbsp;<u>heat absorbed/released</u> (q) by a system, <b>c</b> represents the substance's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">specific heat (c)</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>Specific heat</b>&nbsp;<b>(c)</b> is the <i>amount </i>of heat needed to raise 1 gram of a substance by 1 degree Celcius/Kelvin (stick with the same units for T).</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Specific&#x20;heat&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;b&gt;&#x28;c&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is the&nbsp;<i>amount&nbsp;</i>of heat needed to <u>raise</u><i> </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 gram</span> of some substance by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 degree celcius/Kelvin</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Specific heat</b>&nbsp;<b>(c)</b></span>&nbsp;is the&nbsp;<i>amount&nbsp;</i>of heat needed to <u>raise</u><i> </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 gram</span> of some substance by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 degree celcius/Kelvin</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Specific heat (c)</b> is <u>not</u> restricted to water.</div><div>- Difference between <b>specific heat (c)</b> &amp; <b>heat capacity (mc)</b> is that specific heat is ""standardized"" to only 1 gram, whereas heat capacity (mc) factors in mass (<u>not</u> standardized).</div><div>- Match the 1 degree change units for whatever T is.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""><br></div></div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Specific heat</b>&nbsp;<b>(c)</b></span>&nbsp;is the&nbsp;<i>amount&nbsp;</i>of heat needed to <u>raise</u><i> </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 gram</span> of some substance by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 degree celcius/Kelvin</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Specific heat (c)</b> is <u>not</u> restricted to water.</div><div>- Difference between <b>specific heat (c)</b> &amp; <b>heat capacity (mc)</b> is that specific heat is ""standardized"" to only 1 gram, whereas heat capacity (mc) factors in mass (<u>not</u> standardized).</div><div>- Match the 1 degree change units for whatever T is.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""><br></div></div>"

<div>Equation below for the heat absorbed/released (q) by a system:</div><div>- Rearrange the equation to better understand the units for <b>specific heat (c)</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Heat&#x20;capacity&#x20;&#x28;C&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the <i>amount </i>of <u>heat</u> needed to raise the temperature of some amount of a substance by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1° celcius/Kelvin</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Heat capacity (C)</b></span> is the <i>amount </i>of <u>heat</u> needed to raise the temperature of some amount of a substance by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1° celcius/Kelvin</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Different from&nbsp;<b>specific heat (c)</b>, since specific heat is ""standardized"" by restricting mass to only 1 gram.<br></div><div>- It's easier to raise the temperature of a glass of water by 1 degree than it is to raise the temperature of a pool by 1 degree, because they have different <b>heat capacities (mc)</b>.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>m</b> = mass; <b>c</b> = specific heat (c).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-69fda4a09a7cb72a1d7bd3c1299aa65b4797245b.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-30b192e74ee4291753c484e19fd8245e0a75b01c.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>No work is done since the volume of the gas is unchanged.</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>System</b>: combustion chamber</div><div>- <b>Surroundings</b>: water</div><div>Has been used to determine the calorie content of food additives (how much heat is needed to break the food down).</div><div><br></div><img src=""870b9bf388a0258c0f678f7a3c9f8c60.png""></div>"
"In <b>constant-</b><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>volume</b></span>&nbsp;<b>calorimeters</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;zero&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <u>work</u> is done."	"In <b>constant-</b><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>volume</b></span>&nbsp;<b>calorimeters</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>zero</i></span> <u>work</u> is done.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>No work is done since the volume of the gas is unchanged.</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>System</b>: combustion chamber</div><div>- <b>Surroundings</b>: water</div><div>Has been used to determine the calorie content of food additives (how much heat is needed to break the food down).</div><div><br></div><img src=""870b9bf388a0258c0f678f7a3c9f8c60.png""></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;adiabatic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> happen - the system can <i>only </i>have work performed <i>on</i> it (-W)."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>adiabatic</b></span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> happen - the system can <i>only </i>have work performed <i>on</i> it (-W).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- U = Q - W<div>- Adiabatic processes (Q = 0; U = -W).</div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>adiabatic</b></span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[can/cannot]</span> happen - the system can <i>only </i>have work performed <i>on</i> it (-W)."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>adiabatic</b></span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> happen - the system can <i>only </i>have work performed <i>on</i> it (-W).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- U = Q - W<div>- Adiabatic processes (Q = 0; U = -W).</div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;isothermal&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>can</i></span> happen - the internal energy (U) is always zero, so the heat added (Q) must <i>cancel out&nbsp;</i>work (W) done <i>by</i> the system."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isothermal</b></span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>can</i></span> happen - the internal energy (U) is always zero, so the heat added (Q) must <i>cancel out&nbsp;</i>work (W) done <i>by</i> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- U = Q - W<div>- Isothermal processes (U = 0; <b>Q = W</b>).&nbsp;</div><div>- Heat added <u>to</u> the system by the surroundings (Q) must <u>cancel out</u> the work that the system does to the surroundings (W).</div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isothermal</b></span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[can/cannot]</span> happen - the internal energy (U) is always zero, so the heat added (Q) must <i>cancel out&nbsp;</i>work (W) done <i>by</i> the system."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isothermal</b></span><b> processes</b>, <u>heat transfer (Q)</u> between the system and surroundings <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>can</i></span> happen - the internal energy (U) is always zero, so the heat added (Q) must <i>cancel out&nbsp;</i>work (W) done <i>by</i> the system.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- U = Q - W<div>- Isothermal processes (U = 0; <b>Q = W</b>).&nbsp;</div><div>- Heat added <u>to</u> the system by the surroundings (Q) must <u>cancel out</u> the work that the system does to the surroundings (W).</div></div>"
"<b>Heating curves</b> depict how <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""temperature&#x20;&#x28;&#xB0;C&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>increases </i>and then temporarily remains <u>constant</u> as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""heat"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is added <i>during</i>&nbsp;phase changes."	"<b>Heating curves</b> depict how <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">temperature (°C)</span> <i>increases </i>and then temporarily remains <u>constant</u> as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">heat</span> is added <i>during</i>&nbsp;phase changes.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The amount of heat that gets added to transition phases (solid → liquid) without changing temperature is the <b>latent heat (L)</b>.</div><div>- Regions caught between phase transitions (freezing vs. melting point) <u>do not</u> increase in temperature.</div><div>- Once the entire sample transitions from being 50/50 liquid/ice → 100/0 liquid, temperature increases again.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation that determines <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;heat&#x20;absorbed&#x2F;released&#x20;&#x28;q&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;i&gt;during&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;the&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;u&gt;phase&#x20;change&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation that determines <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>heat absorbed/released (q)</b>&nbsp;<i>during </i>the&nbsp;<u>phase change</u></span> of a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>m</b> = mass; <b>L</b> = latent heat (cal/g).</div><div>- <b>Latent heat (L)</b> is the amount of heat that can get added to change phases (solid → liquid) without changing temperature (see flattening of the curve below).&nbsp;</div><div>- If we used q = mcΔT (as we do with non-phase changing substances), ΔT would be 0, so it'd erroneously give 0 for q.</div><div>- If it's 50/50 liquid/ice, temperature will <u>not</u> increase. Once it's 100/0 liquid ice, temperature will rise again if heat gets added. See graph.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation that determines <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>heat absorbed/released (q)</b>&nbsp;<i>during </i>the&nbsp;<u>phase change</u></span> of a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation that determines <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>heat absorbed/released (q)</b>&nbsp;<i>during </i>the&nbsp;<u>phase change</u></span> of a substance:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""></span><br>

"Temperature (°C) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> change during the process of a phase transitions (i.e. solid → liquid)."	"Temperature (°C) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> change during the process of a phase transitions (i.e. solid → liquid).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Latent heat (L)</b> is the amount of heat that gets added to undergo phase changes (solid → liquid) without increasing temperature.</div><div>- If we used q = mcΔT (as we do with non-phase changing substances), ΔT would be 0, so it'd erroneously give 0 for q.</div><div>- If it's 50/50 liquid/ice, temperature will <u>not</u> increase. Once it's 100/0 liquid ice, temperature will rise again if heat gets added. See graph.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Standard&#x20;enthalpy&#x20;of&#x20;formation&#x20;&#x28;ΔH&#xB0;&lt;sub&gt;f&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>change</i> in enthalpy (ΔH°)</u></span>&nbsp;required to <i>form </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 mole of product</span> using its reactants in their <i>standard </i>states."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°<sub>f</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>change</i> in enthalpy (ΔH°)</u></span>&nbsp;required to <i>form </i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 mole of product</span> using its reactants in their <i>standard </i>states.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>Standard states</b> are the states (solid, liquid, gas) where they're the most stable (like how H<sub>2</sub>O is more stable as a liquid than a solid/gas).<div><br></div><div><img src=""7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°<sub>f</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;change&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;in&#x20;enthalpy&#x20;&#x28;ΔH&#xB0;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;required to <i>form </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x20;mole&#x20;of&#x20;product"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> using its reactants in their <i>standard </i>states."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°<sub>f</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>change</i> in enthalpy (ΔH°)</u></span>&nbsp;required to <i>form </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">1 mole of product</span> using its reactants in their <i>standard </i>states.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>Standard states</b> are the states (solid, liquid, gas) where they're the most stable (like how H<sub>2</sub>O is more stable as a liquid than a solid/gas).<div><br></div><div><img src=""7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&lt;i&gt;standard&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;enthalpy&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;reaction&#x20;&#x28;ΔH&#xB0;rxn&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87.png""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b><i>standard</i> enthalpy of a reaction (ΔH°rxn)</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If you wanted to calculate the non-strandard enthalpy, that's when you'd subtract the energy of the bonds broken - bonds formed.</div><div>- ΔH°<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;represents the <u>enthalpy</u> needed to form 1 mole of products using reactants in their standard forms (i.e. state where' they're the most stable)... <b><u>standard heat of formation</u></b>.</div><div><b><br></b></div><div><img src=""7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b><i>standard</i> enthalpy of a reaction (ΔH°rxn)</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b><i>standard</i> enthalpy of a reaction (ΔH°rxn)</b></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If you wanted to calculate the non-strandard enthalpy, that's when you'd subtract the energy of the bonds broken - bonds formed.</div><div>- ΔH°<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;represents the <u>enthalpy</u> needed to form 1 mole of products using reactants in their standard forms (i.e. state where' they're the most stable)... <b><u>standard heat of formation</u></b>.</div><div><b><br></b></div><div><img src=""7b62fafd3986e0850011c785dca00c87.png""><br></div></div>"
"In thermodynamics, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Hess&#x27;s&#x20;Law&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> says that when we <i>add&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chemical equations</u></span>, we can <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add </i>their associated <u>enthalpies (ΔH)</u></span> as well."	"In thermodynamics, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hess's Law</b></span> says that when we <i>add&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chemical equations</u></span>, we can <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add </i>their associated <u>enthalpies (ΔH)</u></span> as well.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If one chemical reaction is the reverse of the other (A + B → C / C → A + B), their enthalpies have the <u>same</u> magnitude but <u>opposite</u> signs.</div><div>- This applies to all state functions as well (enthalpy, free energy, entropy).</div><div>- Look at the below example.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-385f70ec6962c1b3141e0d58541ab4101a57bf0a.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e4498756c36967924da695fa1f7acd1b.png""><br></div></div>"
"In thermodynamics, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hess's Law</b></span> says that when we <i>add&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;chemical&#x20;equations&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, we can <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;add&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;their&#x20;associated&#x20;&lt;u&gt;enthalpies&#x20;&#x28;ΔH&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> as well."	"In thermodynamics, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hess's Law</b></span> says that when we <i>add&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chemical equations</u></span>, we can <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>add </i>their associated <u>enthalpies (ΔH)</u></span> as well.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If one chemical reaction is the reverse of the other (A + B → C / C → A + B), their enthalpies have the <u>same</u> magnitude but <u>opposite</u> signs.</div><div>- This applies to all state functions as well (enthalpy, free energy, entropy).</div><div>- Look at the below example.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-385f70ec6962c1b3141e0d58541ab4101a57bf0a.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e4498756c36967924da695fa1f7acd1b.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>The <b>enthalpy (ΔH)</b> associated with a reaction in the <i>forward </i>direction has the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""same"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/opposite]</span> <u>magnitude</u> but <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""opposite"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/opposite]</span> <u>sign</u> as the <i>reverse </i>reaction.</div>"	"<div>The <b>enthalpy (ΔH)</b> associated with a reaction in the <i>forward </i>direction has the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">same</span> <u>magnitude</u> but <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">opposite</span> <u>sign</u> as the <i>reverse </i>reaction.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>A + B → C (ΔH = 800J)</div><div>C → A +B (ΔH = -800J)</div><div>- Look at the below example.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a4f716d80a58fe091bb84c75cfb9d9e4.png"">&nbsp;<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e4498756c36967924da695fa1f7acd1b.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Bond&#x20;dissociation&#x20;enthalpy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the <u>enthalpy (ΔH)</u> required to <i>break </i>a bond in a compound."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Bond dissociation enthalpy</b></span> is the <u>enthalpy (ΔH)</u> required to <i>break </i>a bond in a compound.

"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;second&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>law of thermodynamics</b> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>entropy</u> is <i>always </i>increasing</span>&nbsp;&amp; heat flows from <i>higher</i> temperature to <i>lower</i> temperature objects."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>second</b></span> <b>law of thermodynamics</b> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>entropy</u> is <i>always </i>increasing</span>&nbsp;&amp; heat flows from <i>higher</i> temperature to <i>lower</i> temperature objects.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>As a system becomes more ordered (-S), the entropy of the surroundings becomes even more disordered (++S).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""58778a4aa58bd4923326aa451e51380c.png""><br></div></div>"
