<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Represents the <b>heat </b>either <u>absorbed</u> or <u>released</u> to the surroundings at some given temperature.</div><div>- Can be thoughout of a measure of ""disorder.""</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""58778a4aa58bd4923326aa451e51380c.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""354e757b39ac161548ebcb80b7f0da05.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Entropy&#x20;&#x28;S&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>heat absorbed/released</u> (q)</span> by a system at a <i>given&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature (K)</u></span> - how ""spread out"" the energy is."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Entropy (S)</b></span> represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>heat absorbed/released</u> (q)</span> by a system at a <i>given&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature (K)</u></span> - how ""spread out"" the energy is.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""58778a4aa58bd4923326aa451e51380c.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""354e757b39ac161548ebcb80b7f0da05.png""><br></div><div><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Entropy (S)</b></span> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;heat&#x20;absorbed&#x2F;released&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;q&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> by a system at a <i>given&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;temperature&#x20;&#x28;K&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - how ""spread out"" the energy is."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Entropy (S)</b></span> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>heat absorbed/released</u> (q)</span> by a system at a <i>given&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature (K)</u></span> - how ""spread out"" the energy is.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

ΔH units: <b><u>J</u></b>/mol<div>T units: <b><u>Kelvin</u></b></div><div>ΔS units: <b><u>J</u></b>/molK</div></div>"
"The <i>change </i>in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;free&#x20;energy&#x20;&#x28;ΔG&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> represents the <i>maximum</i> amount of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>energy</u></span> released by a reaction that can be <i>used </i>to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">perform work</span>."	"The <i>change </i>in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>free energy (ΔG)</b></span> represents the <i>maximum</i> amount of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>energy</u></span> released by a reaction that can be <i>used </i>to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">perform work</span>.

"The <i>change </i>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>free energy (ΔG)</b></span> represents the <i>maximum</i> amount of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> released by a reaction that can be <i>used </i>to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""perform&#x20;work"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <i>change </i>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>free energy (ΔG)</b></span> represents the <i>maximum</i> amount of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>energy</u></span> released by a reaction that can be <i>used </i>to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">perform work</span>.

"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔS</b> are <i style="""">always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;spontaneous&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[spont./non-spont.]</span>."	"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔS</b> are <i style="""">always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>spontaneous</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because no matter how big ΔH or ΔS are in magnitude, they'll only ever give a <u>negative</u> ΔG.</div><div><br></div><img src=""9596b1d7de1b59a0b658472c0277af1c.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""7af963d632df60d5e7c1be63539cab1e.png""><br></div></div>"

"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔS</b> are <i style="""">always</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>spontaneous</u></span>.

<img src=""9596b1d7de1b59a0b658472c0277af1c.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""7af963d632df60d5e7c1be63539cab1e.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[+/-]</span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[+/-]</span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are <i>only </i><u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">high</span> temperatures."	"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are <i>only </i><u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">high</span> temperatures.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because it's ΔH - (positive), so - as long as ΔH is positive - you want to ""overpower"" ΔH's positive effects by making T really high.&nbsp;<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""9596b1d7de1b59a0b658472c0277af1c.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""7af963d632df60d5e7c1be63539cab1e.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are <i>only </i><u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""high"" data-ordinal=""3"">[high/low]</span> temperatures."	"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>positive</i></span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are <i>only </i><u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">high</span> temperatures.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because it's ΔH - (positive), so - as long as ΔH is positive - you want to ""overpower"" ΔH's positive effects by making T really high.&nbsp;<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""9596b1d7de1b59a0b658472c0277af1c.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""7af963d632df60d5e7c1be63539cab1e.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[+/-]</span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[+/-]</span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are only <u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">low</span> temperatures."	"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are only <u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">low</span> temperatures.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because it's ΔH - (negative), so - as long as ΔH is negative - you want to mitigate its effects by making T small.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""9596b1d7de1b59a0b658472c0277af1c.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""7af963d632df60d5e7c1be63539cab1e.png""><br></div></div>"
"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are only <u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""low"" data-ordinal=""3"">[high/low]</span> temperatures."	"Reactions with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔH</b> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>negative</i></span> <b>ΔS</b>&nbsp;are only <u>spontaneous</u> at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">low</span> temperatures.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because it's ΔH - (negative), so - as long as ΔH is negative - you want to mitigate its effects by making T small.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""9596b1d7de1b59a0b658472c0277af1c.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""7af963d632df60d5e7c1be63539cab1e.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Standard&#x20;free&#x20;energy&#x20;&#x28;ΔG&#xB0;&lt;sub&gt;rxn&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;represents the free energy change of a reaction carried out by compounds in their standard states."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Standard free energy (ΔG°<sub>rxn</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;represents the free energy change of a reaction carried out by compounds in their standard states.

"When <b>ΔG°<sub>rnx</sub> </b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>zero</i></span>, <b>K</b><sub><b>eq</b> </sub>is at <u>equilibrium</u>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Determined using the equation below. Plugging in 0 for ΔG yields a ln(K<sub>eq</sub>) = 0, K<sub>eq</sub> = 1.</div><div><br></div><img src=""5a7fb0c8101e2ce92ed48ffe9c9a024f.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""23be6ca9a15f734658ffafbb1702e671.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>zeroth</b></span> <b>law of thermodynamics</b> says that if systems B ⇌ A &amp; C ⇌ A, then B and C are <i>also </i>in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;thermal&#x20;equilibrium&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;same&#x20;temperatures&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>zeroth</b></span> <b>law of thermodynamics</b> says that if systems B ⇌ A &amp; C ⇌ A, then B and C are <i>also </i>in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>thermal equilibrium</u>&nbsp;(same temperatures)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""main-qimg-ff347e150f294f7848dc56b7686d9ac3""><br></div></div>"
"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;isovolumetric&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> processes are <i>also </i>called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isochoric</b></span> processes."	"In thermochemistry, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>isovolumetric</b></span> processes are <i>also </i>called <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isochoric</b></span> processes.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>4 different processes that describe how these variables interact with each other...</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isothermal</b>: system's temperature is constant (U = 0, so Q = W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Adiabatic</b>: system does not receive thermal energy from the surroundings (Q = 0, so U = -W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isobaric</b>: system's pressure is constant (no changes to U = Q - W)</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Isovolumetric&nbsp;(isochoric)</b>: volume is constant, so since the gas isn't expanding or contracting, no work is done on it (W=0, U=Q).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a28ba8d343016b0e06c1a88e800389ee.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""30l.GIF""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Boyle&#x27;s&#x20;law&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressure</u></span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> under a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">constant temperature</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Boyle's law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressure</u></span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> under a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">constant temperature</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>PV = nRT → nRT simplifies to some constant when temperature is constant, so the only things that can change are pressure and volume.</div><div>- <b>Isothermal process</b> in thermodynamics (since temperature is constant... Q=W).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8ff1ed999b992347379c2a92b39f5a0b.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""68eb2304f68e45d0d86c110de3af3baa.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Boyle's law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;volume&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> under a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">constant temperature</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Boyle's law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressure</u></span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> under a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">constant temperature</span>.

"<div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;partial&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a gas is the <u>pressure</u> exerted by <i>only 1</i> type of gas in a mixture.</div>"	"<div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>partial pressure</b></span> of a gas is the <u>pressure</u> exerted by <i>only 1</i> type of gas in a mixture.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The <b>total pressure (P<sub>T</sub>)</b> in the mixture is equal to the <u>sum</u> of all the partial pressures of different gases (P<sub>A</sub>, P<sub>B</sub>, etc.)</div><div><br></div><img src=""06afa6280e237db376ecdcff371c31c7.png""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Dalton's law</b></span> says the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;total&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;pressure&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;T&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of all the different gases in a mixture is equal to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;sum&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;of&#x20;the&#x20;partial&#x20;pressures&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of <i>each </i>different gas.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Dalton's law</b></span> says the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>total </i>pressure (P<sub>T</sub>)</span> of all the different gases in a mixture is equal to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u><i>sum</i>&nbsp;of the partial pressures</u></span> of <i>each </i>different gas.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b><u>Dalton's Law</u></b>...</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""06afa6280e237db376ecdcff371c31c7.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-da77b8223fea869a0f94f5dab0caf1e00630dd8d.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Dalton&#x27;s&#x20;law&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[law]</span> says the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>total </i>pressure (P<sub>T</sub>)</span> of all the different gases in a mixture is equal to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u><i>sum</i>&nbsp;of the partial pressures</u></span> of <i>each </i>different gas.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Dalton's law</b></span> says the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>total </i>pressure (P<sub>T</sub>)</span> of all the different gases in a mixture is equal to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u><i>sum</i>&nbsp;of the partial pressures</u></span> of <i>each </i>different gas.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The total pressure is the <u>sum</u> of all the partial pressures.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-da77b8223fea869a0f94f5dab0caf1e00630dd8d.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"A compound's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;mole&#x20;fraction&#x20;&#x28;X&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u style=""font-style: italic;"">fraction</u><u>&nbsp;of moles</u></span><i>&nbsp;</i>it contributes to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>total </i>number of moles</u></span> in the mixture (including other compounds)."	"A compound's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>mole fraction (X<sub>A</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u style=""font-style: italic;"">fraction</u><u>&nbsp;of moles</u></span><i>&nbsp;</i>it contributes to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>total </i>number of moles</u></span> in the mixture (including other compounds).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If there was 1 mole of N<sub>2</sub> in a mixture containng 5 total moles (of other compounds as well), the <b>mole fraction</b> would be 1/5 → 0.2.</div><div><br></div><img src=""f671897e5f1a8315731adfbc1d0f48c7.png""></div>"
"A compound's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>mole fraction (X<sub>A</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;font&#x2D;style&#x3A;&#x20;italic&#x3B;&quot;&gt;fraction&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;of&#x20;moles&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><i>&nbsp;</i>it contributes to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;total&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;number&#x20;of&#x20;moles&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in the mixture (including other compounds)."	"A compound's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>mole fraction (X<sub>A</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u style=""font-style: italic;"">fraction</u><u>&nbsp;of moles</u></span><i>&nbsp;</i>it contributes to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>total </i>number of moles</u></span> in the mixture (including other compounds).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- If there was 1 mole of N<sub>2</sub> in a mixture containng 5 total moles (of other compounds as well), the <b>mole fraction</b> would be 1/5 → 0.2.</div><div><br></div><img src=""f671897e5f1a8315731adfbc1d0f48c7.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;partial&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of one <i>single </i>gas species in a mixture is its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mole fraction</u> (X<sub style="""">A</sub><u>)</u></span>&nbsp;<i>multiplied </i>by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>total pressure</u> (P<sub>T</sub>)</span> of all the gas species."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>partial pressure</b></span> of one <i>single </i>gas species in a mixture is its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mole fraction</u> (X<sub style="""">A</sub><u>)</u></span>&nbsp;<i>multiplied </i>by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>total pressure</u> (P<sub>T</sub>)</span> of all the gas species.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Mole fraction (X<sub>A</sub>)</b>: the <u>fraction</u> of the moles that one gas species contributes to the entire mixture's moles.</div><div>- <b>Total pressure (P<sub>A</sub>)</b>: <u>sum</u> of all the partial pressures of each gas in a mixture.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2f316c69fe66ae71847d9f498bf225af.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>partial pressure</b></span> of one <i>single </i>gas species in a mixture is its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mole&#x20;fraction&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;X&lt;sub&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<i>multiplied </i>by the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;total&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;T&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of all the gas species."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>partial pressure</b></span> of one <i>single </i>gas species in a mixture is its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mole fraction</u> (X<sub style="""">A</sub><u>)</u></span>&nbsp;<i>multiplied </i>by the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>total pressure</u> (P<sub>T</sub>)</span> of all the gas species.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Mole fraction (X<sub>A</sub>)</b>: the <u>fraction</u> of the moles that one gas species contributes to the entire mixture's moles.</div><div>- <b>Total pressure (P<sub>A</sub>)</b>: <u>sum</u> of all the partial pressures of each gas in a mixture.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2f316c69fe66ae71847d9f498bf225af.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Henry&#x27;s&#x20;law&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[law]</span> says that for a liquid at a constant tempertature, an <i>increase </i>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>partial pressure</u></span> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i></span> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>concentration</u></span> of gas particles in the fluid over time."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span> says that for a liquid at a constant tempertature, an <i>increase </i>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>partial pressure</u></span> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i></span> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>concentration</u></span> of gas particles in the fluid over time.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>C</b>&nbsp;= concentration of the gas in the blood;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>H</sub></b>&nbsp;= solubility constant for the specific gas;&nbsp;<b>P<sub>gas</sub></b>&nbsp;= partial pressure.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Vapor pressure</b> is the pressure exerted <u>by</u> gas molecules <u>on</u> the surface of a liquid.</div><div>- When vapor pressure increases, more gas molecules are forced into the liquid phase... hence increased dissolved gas concentration.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""5e9240207b2e46d2dc05d20eb83abbde.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span> says that for a liquid at a constant tempertature, an <i>increase </i>in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;partial&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i></span> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;concentration&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of gas particles in the fluid over time."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span> says that for a liquid at a constant tempertature, an <i>increase </i>in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>partial pressure</u></span> will <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i></span> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>concentration</u></span> of gas particles in the fluid over time.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>C</b>&nbsp;= concentration of the gas in the blood;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>H</sub></b>&nbsp;= solubility constant for the specific gas;&nbsp;<b>P<sub>gas</sub></b>&nbsp;= partial pressure.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Vapor pressure</b> is the pressure exerted <u>by</u> gas molecules <u>on</u> the surface of a liquid.</div><div>- When vapor pressure increases, more gas molecules are forced into the liquid phase... hence increased dissolved gas concentration.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""5e9240207b2e46d2dc05d20eb83abbde.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span> says that for a liquid at a constant tempertature, an <i>increase </i>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>partial pressure</u></span> will <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increase&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[inc/dec]</span> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>concentration</u></span> of gas particles in the fluid over time."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span> says that for a liquid at a constant tempertature, an <i>increase </i>in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>partial pressure</u></span> will <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increase</i></span> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>concentration</u></span> of gas particles in the fluid over time.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>C</b>&nbsp;= concentration of the gas in the blood;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>H</sub></b>&nbsp;= solubility constant for the specific gas;&nbsp;<b>P<sub>gas</sub></b>&nbsp;= partial pressure.<br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Vapor pressure</b> is the pressure exerted <u>by</u> gas molecules <u>on</u> the surface of a liquid.</div><div>- When vapor pressure increases, more gas molecules are forced into the liquid phase... hence increased dissolved gas concentration.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""5e9240207b2e46d2dc05d20eb83abbde.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Henry&#x27;s&#x20;law&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[law]</span>, showing how an <i>increase</i> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>partial pressure of gases</u>&nbsp;<i>above</i>&nbsp;the liquid surface</span>&nbsp;results in <i>higher</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>gas concentrations</u></span> in liquids over time:</div><div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""paste-e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad.jpg""></span></div></div>"	"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span>, showing how an <i>increase</i> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>partial pressure of gases</u>&nbsp;<i>above</i>&nbsp;the liquid surface</span>&nbsp;results in <i>higher</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>gas concentrations</u></span> in liquids over time:</div><div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""paste-e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad.jpg""></span></div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>C</b>&nbsp;= concentration of the gas in the blood;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>H</sub></b>&nbsp;= solubility constant for the specific gas;&nbsp;<b>P<sub>gas</sub></b>&nbsp;= partial pressure.<br></div><div>- Henry's law says the higher the vapor pressure exerted by gas molecules above the surface of a liquid, the higher the concentration of dissolved gas particles in that fluid (since they re-enter the fluid state).</div><div><br></div><img src=""5e9240207b2e46d2dc05d20eb83abbde.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span>, showing how an <i>increase</i> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>partial pressure of gases</u>&nbsp;<i>above</i>&nbsp;the liquid surface</span>&nbsp;results in <i>higher</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>gas concentrations</u></span> in liquids over time:</div><div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div></div>"	"<div>Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Henry's law</b></span>, showing how an <i>increase</i> in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>partial pressure of gases</u>&nbsp;<i>above</i>&nbsp;the liquid surface</span>&nbsp;results in <i>higher</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>gas concentrations</u></span> in liquids over time:</div><div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""paste-e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad.jpg""></span></div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>C</b>&nbsp;= concentration of the gas in the blood;&nbsp;<b>k<sub>H</sub></b>&nbsp;= solubility constant for the specific gas;&nbsp;<b>P<sub>gas</sub></b>&nbsp;= partial pressure.<br></div><div>- Henry's law says the higher the vapor pressure exerted by gas molecules above the surface of a liquid, the higher the concentration of dissolved gas particles in that fluid (since they re-enter the fluid state).</div><div><br></div><img src=""5e9240207b2e46d2dc05d20eb83abbde.png""></div>"
"<div>The <b>solubility</b> of a <u>gas</u> in a liquid refers to its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;concentration&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in that liquid.</div>"	"<div>The <b>solubility</b> of a <u>gas</u> in a liquid refers to its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>concentration</i></span> in that liquid.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>C</b> = concentration of the gas in the blood; <b>k<sub>H</sub></b> = solubility constant for the specific gas; <b>P<sub>gas</sub></b> = partial pressure.</div><div>- <b>Henry's law</b> says the higher the vapor pressure exerted by gas molecules above the surface of a liquid, the higher the concentration of dissolved gas particles in that fluid (since they re-enter the fluid state).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-e460506c803e9f187ea146980036c52932bbc7ad.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-52f3f6686b62208c1ef7689ede6db848d3346e63.jpg""><br></div>"
"Equation that describes the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>average </i>kinetic energy</b></span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">a gas particle</span> using its <u>temperature</u>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation that describes the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>average </i>kinetic energy</b></span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">a gas particle</span> using its <u>temperature</u>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Describes the kinetic energy of the gas particles in terms of temperature.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>k<sub>B</sub></b> = Boltzmann constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup> J/K); <b>T</b> = temperature (Kelvin)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d6ef7d08c49204ea905ce445ec46df004a4bf8da.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Equation that describes the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&lt;i&gt;average&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""a&#x20;gas&#x20;particle"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> using its <u>temperature</u>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2.png""></span><br></div>"	"Equation that describes the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>average </i>kinetic energy</b></span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">a gas particle</span> using its <u>temperature</u>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Describes the kinetic energy of the gas particles in terms of temperature.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>k<sub>B</sub></b> = Boltzmann constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup> J/K); <b>T</b> = temperature (Kelvin)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d6ef7d08c49204ea905ce445ec46df004a4bf8da.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In the below equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>average </i>kinetic energy</u></span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>gas</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>particles, <b>k<sub>B</sub></b> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Boltzmann"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> constant, which <i>equals&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x2E;4&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;23&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;J&#x2F;K"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2.png""><br></div>"	"<div>In the below equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>average </i>kinetic energy</u></span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>gas</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>particles, <b>k<sub>B</sub></b> is the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Boltzmann</span> constant, which <i>equals&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup> J/K</span>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Note that this applies only to their kinetic energies - <u>not</u> specific speeds. Use the <b>root-mean-square speed</b> for that instead.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d6ef7d08c49204ea905ce445ec46df004a4bf8da.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;average&#x20;&lt;i&gt;speed&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;of&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;u&gt;gas&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;particles"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;in a mixture:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""3f61a41fc9c73818e527547e90c07f6d.png""></span><br></div>"	"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>average <i>speed</i>&nbsp;</b>of&nbsp;<u>gas</u>&nbsp;particles</span>&nbsp;in a mixture:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""3f61a41fc9c73818e527547e90c07f6d.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Note that this only describes the <b>average speed</b> of the gas particles - <u>not</u> their kinetic energies. Use KE = (3/2)k<sub>B</sub>T instead for that.</div><div>- <b>u<sub>rms</sub></b> = root-mean-square speed (what this equation is called); <b>R</b> = ideal gas constant (0.0821 or 8.21); <b>M</b> = molar mass.</div><div>- (3/2)K<sub>B</sub>T is the equation for the average <u>kinetic energy</u> of gas particles.</div></div>"
"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>average <i>speed</i>&nbsp;</b>of&nbsp;<u>gas</u>&nbsp;particles</span>&nbsp;in a mixture:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;3f61a41fc9c73818e527547e90c07f6d&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>average <i>speed</i>&nbsp;</b>of&nbsp;<u>gas</u>&nbsp;particles</span>&nbsp;in a mixture:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""3f61a41fc9c73818e527547e90c07f6d.png""></span><br></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b><u>Graham's law</u></b></div><div>- Just like diffusion!</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""82d22c126d33a671494b284656a18dd6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3625c272f92029618e664e1dc32201b7.png""><br></div></div>"
"High<i> </i>pressures cause <b>gases</b> to act more <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;real&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[ideal/real]</span>, since the gas molecules are forced into close proximity - leading to<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger </i>attractive<i>&nbsp;</i><u>intermolecular forces</u></span>."	"High<i> </i>pressures cause <b>gases</b> to act more <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>real</u></span>, since the gas molecules are forced into close proximity - leading to<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger </i>attractive<i>&nbsp;</i><u>intermolecular forces</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Leads to <b>condensation</b>.</div>"
"High<i> </i>pressures cause <b>gases</b> to act more <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>real</u></span>, since the gas molecules are forced into close proximity - leading to<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;stronger&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;attractive&lt;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;intermolecular&#x20;forces&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"High<i> </i>pressures cause <b>gases</b> to act more <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>real</u></span>, since the gas molecules are forced into close proximity - leading to<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger </i>attractive<i>&nbsp;</i><u>intermolecular forces</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Leads to <b>condensation</b>.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Real&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[real/ideal]</span> <b>gas molecules&nbsp;</b>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <u>volumes</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ideal"" data-ordinal=""1"">[real/ideal]</span> gases."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Real</b></span> <b>gas molecules&nbsp;</b>have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <u>volumes</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ideal</span> gases.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

...because <u>real gas molecules physically take up space</u> (which ideal gases are assumed to not take up any space). Extremely high pressures cause them to take up more space per volume - higher volume.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Real</b></span> <b>gas molecules&nbsp;</b>have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher]</span> <u>volumes</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ideal</span> gases."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Real</b></span> <b>gas molecules&nbsp;</b>have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <u>volumes</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ideal</span> gases.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

...because <u>real gas molecules physically take up space</u> (which ideal gases are assumed to not take up any space). Extremely high pressures cause them to take up more space per volume - higher volume.</div>"

<div><img src=""f54fcb77b4044730c191bcad5c35f6a0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""acd5de6b846682d7af16e39a0403ca77.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""definition-of-spectator-ion-and-examples-605675_FINAL2-9baec249a4034ca78349afd52961c35b.png""></div>"
"In redox reactions, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;net&#x20;ionic&#x20;equations&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are equations that <i>only </i>include the <u>reacting species</u> - <i>not </i>spectator ions."	"In redox reactions, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>net ionic equations</b></span> are equations that <i>only </i>include the <u>reacting species</u> - <i>not </i>spectator ions.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>SO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup> didn't participate in the reaction at all, so it's excluded from the net ionic equation.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f54fcb77b4044730c191bcad5c35f6a0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ca589a90a3c00698598c34f25a9b3a50.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""definition-of-spectator-ion-and-examples-605675_FINAL2-9baec249a4034ca78349afd52961c35b.png""></div>"
"In <u>net ionic equations</u>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;aqueous&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[solid/aqueous]</span> compounds should be <i>split apart</i> (Na<sup>+</sup> + Cl<sup>-</sup>), while <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;solid&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[solid/aqueous]</span> <b>salts </b>should be kept as <i>one </i>species."	"In <u>net ionic equations</u>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>aqueous</b></span> compounds should be <i>split apart</i> (Na<sup>+</sup> + Cl<sup>-</sup>), while <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>solid</b></span> <b>salts </b>should be kept as <i>one </i>species.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Net ionic equations</b> are redox equations where only reactive species (ones that part-take in the redox reaction) are included.</div><div>- SO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup> didn't participate in the reaction at all, so it's excluded from the net ionic equation.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f54fcb77b4044730c191bcad5c35f6a0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ca589a90a3c00698598c34f25a9b3a50.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""definition-of-spectator-ion-and-examples-605675_FINAL2-9baec249a4034ca78349afd52961c35b.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Disproportionation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>reactions </b>are those where <i>one single</i> substance gets both <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>oxidized</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reduced</u></span> <i>simultaneously</i>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Disproportionation</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are those where <i>one single</i> substance gets both <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>oxidized</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reduced</u></span> <i>simultaneously</i>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>Oxygen's oxidation state on the&nbsp;<b>left</b>: -1 (in a peroxide)</div><div>Oxygen's oxidation states on the&nbsp;<b>right</b>: (-2) &amp; (0)... (-1 → -2 = reduction) and (-1 → 0 = oxidation).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0153fe875393b6541522cc0b7d1f54be.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""78995ce5d0af6da34e2d25cd672efc0a.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Disproportionation</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are those where <i>one single</i> substance gets both <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;oxidized&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;reduced&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>simultaneously</i>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Disproportionation</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are those where <i>one single</i> substance gets both <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>oxidized</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reduced</u></span> <i>simultaneously</i>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><div>Oxygen's oxidation state on the&nbsp;<b>left</b>: -1 (in a peroxide)</div><div>Oxygen's oxidation states on the&nbsp;<b>right</b>: (-2) &amp; (0)... (-1 → -2 = reduction) and (-1 → 0 = oxidation).</div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0153fe875393b6541522cc0b7d1f54be.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""78995ce5d0af6da34e2d25cd672efc0a.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Superoxide dismutase</b></span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;+ <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> via a <u>disproportionation</u> reaction."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Superoxide dismutase</b></span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup></span> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub></span>&nbsp;+ <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub></span> via a <u>disproportionation</u> reaction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Disproportionation </b>reactions are those where one element (oxygen in this case) is simultaneously both <u>oxidized</u> &amp; <u>reduced</u>. (-1/2) signifies the electron was ""spread"" over 2 oxygen atoms...<br></div><div><br></div><div>Oxygen on the <b>left</b>: (-1/2 → -1... reduced)</div><div>Oxygen on the <b>right</b>: (-1/2 → 0... oxidized)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c3b9d606d8a77729a0e144ea9524f0d0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6dfaed82bb81c77d74757de676094d46.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Superoxide&#x20;dismutase&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[enzyme]</span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup></span> → <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub></span>&nbsp;+ <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub></span> via a <u>disproportionation</u> reaction."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Superoxide dismutase</b></span> catalyzes <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup></span> → <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub></span>&nbsp;+ <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">O<sub>2</sub></span> via a <u>disproportionation</u> reaction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Disproportionation </b>reactions are those where one element (oxygen in this case) is simultaneously both <u>oxidized</u> &amp; <u>reduced</u>. (-1/2) signifies the electron was ""spread"" over 2 oxygen atoms...<br></div><div><br></div><div>Oxygen on the <b>left</b>: (-1/2 → -1... reduced)</div><div>Oxygen on the <b>right</b>: (-1/2 → 0... oxidized)</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c3b9d606d8a77729a0e144ea9524f0d0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""6dfaed82bb81c77d74757de676094d46.png""><br></div></div>"
"Enzymes that catalyze <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;redox&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <b>reactions </b>typically have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>copper</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>zinc</u></span> atoms <i>stabilized</i> by proximal <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">histidine</span> residues."	"Enzymes that catalyze <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>redox</b></span> <b>reactions </b>typically have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>copper</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>zinc</u></span> atoms <i>stabilized</i> by proximal <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">histidine</span> residues.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6dfaed82bb81c77d74757de676094d46.png""><br></div></div>"
"Enzymes that catalyze <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>redox</b></span> <b>reactions </b>typically have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;copper&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;zinc&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> atoms <i>stabilized</i> by proximal <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">histidine</span> residues."	"Enzymes that catalyze <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>redox</b></span> <b>reactions </b>typically have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>copper</u></span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>zinc</u></span> atoms <i>stabilized</i> by proximal <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">histidine</span> residues.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6dfaed82bb81c77d74757de676094d46.png""><br></div></div>"
"Enzymes that catalyze <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>redox</b></span> <b>reactions </b>typically have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>copper</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>zinc</u></span> atoms <i>stabilized</i> by proximal <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""histidine"" data-ordinal=""3"">[a.a.]</span> residues."	"Enzymes that catalyze <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>redox</b></span> <b>reactions </b>typically have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>copper</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>zinc</u></span> atoms <i>stabilized</i> by proximal <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">histidine</span> residues.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""6dfaed82bb81c77d74757de676094d46.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Potentiometric&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b> titrations</b> are redox titrations that <i>do not</i> use color indicators - they instead use <u>voltmeters</u>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Potentiometric</b></span><b> titrations</b> are redox titrations that <i>do not</i> use color indicators - they instead use <u>voltmeters</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Analogous to using pH meters in acid-base titrations.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Solvation&#x2F;dissolution&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> happens when the solvent interacts <i>electrostatically </i>with the solute and breaks it into <u>separately charged ions</u>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Solvation/dissolution</b></span> happens when the solvent interacts <i>electrostatically </i>with the solute and breaks it into <u>separately charged ions</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Solvation</b></span> is <i>also </i>known as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;dissolution&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Solvation</b></span> is <i>also </i>known as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dissolution</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Solvation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is <i>also </i>known as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dissolution</b></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Solvation</b></span> is <i>also </i>known as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dissolution</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""></div>"
"<b>Solvation </b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;exo&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> than the original."	"<b>Solvation </b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> than the original.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: gases dissolve better at&nbsp;<u>low</u>&nbsp;temperatures since, according to LeChatlier's principle, decreasing temperature&nbsp;<u>shifts</u>&nbsp;the reaction towards the more exothermic side (dissolving gases in H<sub>2</sub>O is exothermic).<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""></div>"
"<b>Solvation </b>is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;stronger&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[stronger/weaker]</span> than the original."	"<b>Solvation </b>is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> than the original.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: gases dissolve better at&nbsp;<u>low</u>&nbsp;temperatures since, according to LeChatlier's principle, decreasing temperature&nbsp;<u>shifts</u>&nbsp;the reaction towards the more exothermic side (dissolving gases in H<sub>2</sub>O is exothermic).<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""></div>"
"<b>Dissolving</b> <u>gases</u> in water is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""exo"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>thermic &amp; easier at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> temperatures, since the new bonds are stronger due to gases only having <i>slight </i>intermolecular forces."	"<b>Dissolving</b> <u>gases</u> in water is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">exo</span>thermic &amp; easier at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> temperatures, since the new bonds are stronger due to gases only having <i>slight </i>intermolecular forces.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Gases dissolve better at <u>low</u> temperatures since, according to LeChatlier's principle, decreasing temperature <u>shifts</u> the reaction towards the more exothermic side (dissolving gases in H<sub>2</sub>O is exothermic).</div><div><br></div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""></div>"
"<div>For the <b>solvation/dissolution</b> of compounds that result in <u>exothermic</u> reactions (like gases in water), why do <i>low </i>temperatures get favored?</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""LeChatlier&#x27;s&#x20;principle&#x20;says&#x20;decreasing&#x20;the&#x20;temperature&#x20;shifts&#x20;the&#x20;reaction&#x20;toward&#x20;the&#x20;more&#x20;exothermic&#x20;side&#x20;&#x28;to&#x20;&quot;counterbalance&quot;&#x20;the&#x20;stress&#x29;&#x2E;&#x2E;&#x2E;favoring&#x20;this&#x20;side&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;reaction&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>"	"<div>For the <b>solvation/dissolution</b> of compounds that result in <u>exothermic</u> reactions (like gases in water), why do <i>low </i>temperatures get favored?</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">LeChatlier's principle says decreasing the temperature shifts the reaction toward the more exothermic side (to ""counterbalance"" the stress)...favoring this side of the reaction.</span>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Imagine that temperature acts as a reactant in the equation.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>For the <b>solvation/dissolution</b> of compounds that result in <u>endothermic</u> reactions (like NaCl in water), why do <i>high&nbsp;</i>temperatures get favored?</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""LeChatlier&#x27;s&#x20;principle&#x20;says&#x20;increasing&#x20;the&#x20;temperature&#x20;shifts&#x20;the&#x20;reaction&#x20;toward&#x20;the&#x20;more&#x20;&lt;u&gt;endothermic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;side&#x20;&#x28;to&#x20;&quot;counterbalance&quot;&#x20;the&#x20;stress&#x29;&#x2E;&#x2E;&#x2E;favoring&#x20;this&#x20;side&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;reaction&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>"	"<div>For the <b>solvation/dissolution</b> of compounds that result in <u>endothermic</u> reactions (like NaCl in water), why do <i>high&nbsp;</i>temperatures get favored?</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">LeChatlier's principle says increasing the temperature shifts the reaction toward the more <u>endothermic</u> side (to ""counterbalance"" the stress)...favoring this side of the reaction.</span>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Most</u> dissolutions are <b>endothermic </b>(meaning the new ""solvated"" interactions are weaker than the original bonds). This is why turning the heat up on reactions usually favors dissolving.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Solvation&nbsp;</b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;endo&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaker</i></span> than the original."	"<b>Solvation&nbsp;</b>is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaker</i></span> than the original.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Most</u> dissolutions are <b>endothermic </b>(meaning the new ""solvated"" interactions are weaker than the original bonds). This is why turning the heat up on reactions usually favors dissolving.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Solvation&nbsp;</b>is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weaker&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[stronger/weaker]</span> than the original."	"<b>Solvation&nbsp;</b>is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u>&nbsp;and favored at <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> temperatures when the strength of the new electrostatic interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weaker</i></span> than the original.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Most</u> dissolutions are <b>endothermic </b>(meaning the new ""solvated"" interactions are weaker than the original bonds). This is why turning the heat up on reactions usually favors dissolving.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Increasing the temperature usually <i>speeds up</i> <b>dissolution</b>, since <u>most</u> <b>dissolution </b>reactions form <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""weaker"" data-ordinal=""1"">[stronger/weaker]</span> bonds than the original (<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""endo"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>thermic).</div>"	"<div>Increasing the temperature usually <i>speeds up</i> <b>dissolution</b>, since <u>most</u> <b>dissolution </b>reactions form <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">weaker</span> bonds than the original (<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">endo</span>thermic).</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>According to LeChatlier's principle, increasing the temperature shifts the reaction towards the endothermic side of the equilibrium (to ""counterbalance"" the stress of increased stress)... hence favoring solvation/dissolution.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"The products of <i>most </i><b>solvation </b>(dissolving) reactions form <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""weaker"" data-ordinal=""1"">[stronger/weaker]</span> bonds than the original (i.e. NaCl vs. dissolved Na<sup>+</sup> &amp; Cl<sup>-</sup>)."	"The products of <i>most </i><b>solvation </b>(dissolving) reactions form <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">weaker</span> bonds than the original (i.e. NaCl vs. dissolved Na<sup>+</sup> &amp; Cl<sup>-</sup>).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This means most dissolving reactions are <u><b>endothermic</b></u> (hence why turning up the heat speeds it up).</div><div>- According to LeChatlier's principle, increasing the temperature shifts the reaction towards the endothermic side of the equilibrium (to ""counterbalance"" the stress of increased stress)... hence favoring solvation/dissolution.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Ideal&#x20;solutions&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are dissolved solutions where the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>strength of the bonds</u></span> in the products are <i>equal to</i>&nbsp;the reactants.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ideal solutions</b></span> are dissolved solutions where the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>strength of the bonds</u></span> in the products are <i>equal to</i>&nbsp;the reactants.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Solvation product's bonds <u>stronger</u>: <b>exothermic </b>(favored at low temperatures)</div><div>- Solvation product's bonds <u>weaker</u>: <b>endothermic </b>(favored at high temperatures).</div><div>- Solvation product's bonds equal in strength: <b>ideal solution</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ideal solutions</b></span> are dissolved solutions where the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;strength&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;bonds&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in the products are <i>equal to</i>&nbsp;the reactants.<br></div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ideal solutions</b></span> are dissolved solutions where the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>strength of the bonds</u></span> in the products are <i>equal to</i>&nbsp;the reactants.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Solvation product's bonds <u>stronger</u>: <b>exothermic </b>(favored at low temperatures)</div><div>- Solvation product's bonds <u>weaker</u>: <b>endothermic </b>(favored at high temperatures).</div><div>- Solvation product's bonds equal in strength: <b>ideal solution</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Solvation/dissolution</b> reactions <i>always </i>result in a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""increase"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increase/decrease]</span> in <u>entropy</u>&nbsp;(ΔS)."	"<b>Solvation/dissolution</b> reactions <i>always </i>result in a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">increase</span> in <u>entropy</u>&nbsp;(ΔS).
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Enthalpy may or may not be increased, which is the other factor that determines the spontaneity of the reaction.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <b>solvation/dissolution</b> of NaCl in water is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""endo"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>thermic and is favored at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[high/low]</span> temperatures."	"The <b>solvation/dissolution</b> of NaCl in water is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">endo</span>thermic and is favored at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> temperatures.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <u>Overall</u> interaction is endothermic (takes heat to break apart Na &amp; Cl) despite the fact that water forming dipole-dipole interactions with the ions is an exothermic reaction (net is endothermic though).</div><div>-&nbsp;<u>Most</u> solvation reactions are <b>endothermic </b>(hence why turning up the heat normally helps to dissolve). Exceptions are <u>gases</u> (exothermic).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""090e31e7da73739234713e09752e2f69.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In solubility, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;saturation&#x20;point&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is the equilibrium position where the amount of dissolved solute is <i>maximized</i>.<br></div>"	"<div>In solubility, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>saturation point</b></span> is the equilibrium position where the amount of dissolved solute is <i>maximized</i>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...can't get any more dissolved in its equilibrium.</div><div>- If you keep adding more, it'll for a <b>precipitate</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""les_matsci_unlimitedandlimitedsolubility_2.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Adding <i>even more</i> of a compound to a solution <i>after </i>reaching its solubility <b>saturation point</b> leads to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""precipitation"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>Adding <i>even more</i> of a compound to a solution <i>after </i>reaching its solubility <b>saturation point</b> leads to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">precipitation</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The&nbsp;<b>saturation point</b>&nbsp;is the equilibrium position where the amount of dissolved solute is&nbsp;<i>maximized</i>&nbsp;(can't get any more dissolved in its equilibrium).<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""les_matsci_unlimitedandlimitedsolubility_2.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>When a solvation reaction's ΔG is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;negative&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span>, a compound is said to be <b>soluble </b>in its solution.</div>"	"<div>When a solvation reaction's ΔG is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>negative</i></span>, a compound is said to be <b>soluble </b>in its solution.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Process occurs spontaneously &amp; it dissolves on its own.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""les_matsci_unlimitedandlimitedsolubility_2.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>When the ΔG for a solvation reaction is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;positive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[+/-]</span>, a compound is said to be <b>insoluble </b>in its solution.</div>"	"<div>When the ΔG for a solvation reaction is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>positive</i></span>, a compound is said to be <b>insoluble </b>in its solution.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Process occurs non-spontaneously &amp; it cannot dissolve on its own.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""les_matsci_unlimitedandlimitedsolubility_2.png""><br></div></div>"
"Solutes with a <b>molar solubility</b> <i>above </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""0&#x2E;1&#x20;M"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <u>soluble</u> in its solution."	"Solutes with a <b>molar solubility</b> <i>above </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">0.1 M</span> are <u>soluble</u> in its solution.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Over 0.1 M solubilty = soluble.</div><div>- Under 0.1 M solubility = sparingly soluble.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""les_matsci_unlimitedandlimitedsolubility_2.png""><br></div></div>"
"Solutes with a <b>molar solubility</b> <i>below&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""0&#x2E;1&#x20;M"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <u>sparingly&nbsp;soluble</u> in its solution."	"Solutes with a <b>molar solubility</b> <i>below&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">0.1 M</span> are <u>sparingly&nbsp;soluble</u> in its solution.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Over 0.1 M solubilty = soluble.</div><div>- Under 0.1 M solubility = sparingly soluble.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""les_matsci_unlimitedandlimitedsolubility_2.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Aqueous</b> solutions use <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""water"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> as their solvents."	"<b>Aqueous</b> solutions use <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">water</span> as their solvents.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""467faba6adb2203171795b19f27af417.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7818759d860c8f0d28e93e57d58a2c52.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup></b> is called a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hydronium"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> ion."	"<b>H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup></b> is called a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">hydronium</span> ion.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""467faba6adb2203171795b19f27af417.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Salts </b>formed by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;group&#x20;1&#x20;metals&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;nitrate&#x20;salts&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;ex&#x3A;&#x20;NaNO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>always</i></span> <u>water-soluble</u>." <hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""467faba6adb2203171795b19f27af417.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7818759d860c8f0d28e93e57d58a2c52.png""><br></div></div>"

"<b>Salts </b>formed by <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>group 1 metals</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>nitrate salts</u> (ex: NaNO<sub>3</sub>)</span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>always</i></span> <u>water-soluble</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""467faba6adb2203171795b19f27af417.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7818759d860c8f0d28e93e57d58a2c52.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Complex ions</b></span> are made of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;central&#x20;&lt;i&gt;cations&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> surrounded by <u>ligands</u> acting as electron pair donors."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Complex ions</b></span> are made of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>central <i>cations</i></u></span> surrounded by <u>ligands</u> acting as electron pair donors.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This complex formation is called a <b>coordinate complex/ionic complex</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""abb21c22909437f59e2666dccd759a3b.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Complex&#x20;ions&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> are made of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>central <i>cations</i></u></span> surrounded by <u>ligands</u> acting as electron pair donors."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Complex ions</b></span> are made of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>central <i>cations</i></u></span> surrounded by <u>ligands</u> acting as electron pair donors.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This complex formation is called a <b>coordinate complex/ionic complex</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""abb21c22909437f59e2666dccd759a3b.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Hemoglobin&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[protein]</span> uses a <u>coordinate complex (Fe)</u> in its central core that allows <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub></span>, or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO</span> to bind as ligands."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hemoglobin</b></span> uses a <u>coordinate complex (Fe)</u> in its central core that allows <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub></span>, or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO</span> to bind as ligands.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- O<sub>2</sub>, CO<sub>2</sub>, and CO are the <b>ligands </b>(electron donors) that coordinately bind to the central transition metal (Fe) for transport.</div><div>- Other molecules like H<sup>+</sup> and 2,3-BPG bind <u>allosterically</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""4de99bc65943a192c0ea17a4fa9a834f.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hemoglobin</b></span> uses a <u>coordinate complex (Fe)</u> in its central core that allows <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""CO&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""CO"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> to bind as ligands."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Hemoglobin</b></span> uses a <u>coordinate complex (Fe)</u> in its central core that allows <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">O<sub>2</sub></span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub></span>, or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO</span> to bind as ligands.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- O<sub>2</sub>, CO<sub>2</sub>, and CO are the <b>ligands </b>(electron donors) that coordinately bind to the central transition metal (Fe) for transport.</div><div>- Other molecules like H<sup>+</sup> and 2,3-BPG bind <u>allosterically</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""4de99bc65943a192c0ea17a4fa9a834f.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Vitamin&#x20;B&lt;sub&gt;12&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x28;cyanocobalamin&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[vitamin]</span>&nbsp;uses a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cobalt (Co<sup>+</sup>)</span> metal in its <u>coordinate complex</u>&nbsp;to aid in redox reactions."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Vitamin B<sub>12</sub> (cyanocobalamin)</b></span>&nbsp;uses a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cobalt (Co<sup>+</sup>)</span> metal in its <u>coordinate complex</u>&nbsp;to aid in redox reactions.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Lots of other coenzymes (vitamins) and cofactors use coordinate complexes to aid in electron transfer reactions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""49d278dc98e41ee71cdfa765a5b05b89.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Vitamin B<sub>12</sub> (cyanocobalamin)</b></span>&nbsp;uses a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cobalt&#x20;&#x28;Co&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> metal in its <u>coordinate complex</u>&nbsp;to aid in redox reactions." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Lots of other coenzymes (vitamins) and cofactors use coordinate complexes to aid in electron transfer reactions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""49d278dc98e41ee71cdfa765a5b05b89.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Chelation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is when a central <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">metal cation</span> in a <u>coordinate complex</u> binds to <i>multiple </i>ligands from different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">binding sites</span> on the <i>same </i>molecule."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Chelation</b></span> is when a central <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">metal cation</span> in a <u>coordinate complex</u> binds to <i>multiple </i>ligands from different <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">binding sites</span> on the <i>same </i>molecule.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Chelation</b></span> is when a central <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">metal cation</span> in a <u>coordinate complex</u> binds to <i>multiple </i>ligands from different <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">binding sites</span> on the <i>same </i>molecule.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The ligands that form this coordinate complex come from different binding sites of the same molecule.</div><div>- The second coordinate bond forms quicker than the first on bidendate ligands.</div><div><br></div><img src=""aa28e3ac9ad404afed97c65d59df6044.png""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since chelating complexes are formed when multiple different binding sites from the same molecule act as ligands on the same metal cation.</div><div><br></div><img src=""aa28e3ac9ad404afed97c65d59df6044.png""></div>"
