"The <u>sum</u> of a solution's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;pH&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;pOH&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> will <i>always </i>equal <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">14</span>."	"The <u>sum</u> of a solution's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pH</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pOH</b></span> will <i>always </i>equal <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">14</span>.

"The <u>sum</u> of a solution's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pH</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pOH</b></span> will <i>always </i>equal <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""14"" data-ordinal=""2"">[#]</span>."	"The <u>sum</u> of a solution's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pH</b></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>pOH</b></span> will <i>always </i>equal <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">14</span>.

<img src=""cf56bb19236af51bc46cea4648a7d401.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""1ca64da6c151f69ee2b158f7a3d5c8b7.png""><br></div></div>"
"<u>Shortcut</u> to determining <b>[H<sup>+</sup>]</b> <i>given</i> pH of strong acids/bases:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x5B;H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x5D;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;1&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;pH&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<u>Shortcut</u> to determining <b>[H<sup>+</sup>]</b> <i>given</i> pH of strong acids/bases:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">[H<sup>+</sup>] = 1 x 10<sup>-pH</sup></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""58d0854d34fcdea57cae74612cc48269.png""></div>"
"<u>Shortcut</u> to determining <b>[OH<sup>-</sup>]</b> <i>given</i> pOH:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x5B;OH&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x5D;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;1&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;pOH&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<u>Shortcut</u> to determining <b>[OH<sup>-</sup>]</b> <i>given</i> pOH:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">[OH<sup>-</sup>] = 1 x 10<sup>-pOH</sup></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Below shows the trick for acids, but it applies here as well.</div><div><br></div><img src=""58d0854d34fcdea57cae74612cc48269.png""></div>"
"<div></div><div><u>Shortcut</u> to calculate <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pK<sub>a</sub></b></span>&nbsp;when <i>given</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">K<sub>a</sub></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pK&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x27;s&#x20;exponent&#x29;&#x20;&#x2D;&#x20;0&#x2E;&#x5B;K&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x27;s&#x20;coefficient&#x5D;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div></div><div><u>Shortcut</u> to calculate <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pK<sub>a</sub></b></span>&nbsp;when <i>given</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">K<sub>a</sub></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pK<sub>a</sub> = (K<sub>a</sub>'s exponent) - 0.[K<sub>a</sub>'s coefficient]</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""c781dae35116abd5d502ae4988044f75.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2ac6c603a2dcef326402ba7ed30df2b2.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""81552a42d0ae6626a27ccec798213a31.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Strong acids</b> &amp; <b>strong bases</b> are compounds that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ionize&#x20;100&#x25;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> when placed in solution."	"<b>Strong acids</b> &amp; <b>strong bases</b> are compounds that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ionize 100%</span> when placed in solution.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>NaOH</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""base"" data-ordinal=""1"">[acid/base]</span>."	"<b>NaOH</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">base</span>.

"<b>Group </b><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>1</b></span> metals with <b>OH</b> tend to act as <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>bases</u></span>.

<div><b>Group 1 metals</b> tend to act as strong bases when bound to <b>OH</b>.</div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

The value of K<sub>a</sub> in solution will only change if temperature is changed.</div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;acid&#x20;dissociation&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""d9ffa396a3d411faa300076a56e653ae.png""></span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>acid dissociation constant (K<sub>a</sub>)</b></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""d9ffa396a3d411faa300076a56e653ae.png""></span><br></div>

The value of K<sub>a</sub> in solution will only change if temperature is changed.</div>"
"<u>Strong acids</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;large&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[small/large]</span> <b>K<sub>a</sub>'s</b>."	"<u>Strong acids</u> have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>large</i></span> <b>K<sub>a</sub>'s</b>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"Shortcut for calculating <b>pH/pOH</b> of a strong acid/base when given <u>molarity (M)</u>:<div><br></div><div>0.01 M → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;2&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;M&#x20;→&#x20;pH&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;exponent&#x20;&#x28;2&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Shortcut for calculating <b>pH/pOH</b> of a strong acid/base when given <u>molarity (M)</u>:<div><br></div><div>0.01 M → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1 x 10<sup>-2</sup> M → pH = exponent (2)</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Since strong acids/bases dissociate 100%, you can treat their molarities as their H<sup>+</sup>/OH<sup>-</sup> concentrations.</div>"
"<b>Metallic character</b> (more ""metal-like"") <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>down</u></span> &amp; to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>left</u></span>." "On the periodic table, the element's&nbsp;<u>density</u>&nbsp;tends to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decrease&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increase/decrease]</span> as you go to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">left</span>."	"On the periodic table, the element's&nbsp;<u>density</u>&nbsp;tends to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decrease</i></span> as you go to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">left</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>d = m/v</b></div><div>- As you move towards the left, mass decreases (smaller atomic weights) &amp; volume increases (larger atomic radii).</div><div><br></div><img src=""20140811155153658831.png""></div>"
"On the periodic table, the element's&nbsp;<u>density</u>&nbsp;tends to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decrease</i></span> as you go to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""left"" data-ordinal=""2"">[left/right]</span>."	"On the periodic table, the element's&nbsp;<u>density</u>&nbsp;tends to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decrease</i></span> as you go to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">left</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"On the periodic table, the element's&nbsp;<u>density</u>&nbsp;tends to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increase&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increase/decrease]</span> as you go to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">right</span>."	"On the periodic table, the element's&nbsp;<u>density</u>&nbsp;tends to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> as you go to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">right</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Nitrate salts are almost always soluble. Silver salts are almost always <u>insoluble</u>, but this is an exception.</div><div><br></div><img src=""43aa313b52e2cc0819a85338aa835c44.png""></div>"

"1 <b>atomic mass</b> <b>unit </b>(amu) is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1.7 x 10<sup>-24</sup></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">g</span>.

</div>"
"<div>In the below equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;average&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;gas&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><b>&nbsp;</b>particles, <b>k<sub>B</sub></b> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Boltzmann</span> constant, which <i>equals&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup> J/K</span>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2.png""><br></div>"	"<div>In the below equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>average </i>kinetic energy</u></span>&nbsp;of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>gas</b></span><b>&nbsp;</b>particles, <b>k<sub>B</sub></b> is the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Boltzmann</span> constant, which <i>equals&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup> J/K</span>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2e65656b708a3a8ecf550172a14930c2.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Note that this applies only to their kinetic energies - <u>not</u> specific speeds. Use the <b>root-mean-square speed</b> for that instead.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d6ef7d08c49204ea905ce445ec46df004a4bf8da.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div></div><div><u>Shortcut</u> to calculate <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;pK&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;when <i>given</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""K&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">pK<sub>a</sub> = (K<sub>a</sub>'s exponent) - 0.[K<sub>a</sub>'s coefficient]</span><br></div>"	"<div></div><div><u>Shortcut</u> to calculate <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pK<sub>a</sub></b></span>&nbsp;when <i>given</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">K<sub>a</sub></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">pK<sub>a</sub> = (K<sub>a</sub>'s exponent) - 0.[K<sub>a</sub>'s coefficient]</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""c781dae35116abd5d502ae4988044f75.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""2ac6c603a2dcef326402ba7ed30df2b2.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""81552a42d0ae6626a27ccec798213a31.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>For every 1 unit difference between the <b>pK<sub>a</sub></b>&nbsp;of a compound and the <b>pH</b> it's at&nbsp;(like 8.4 pK<sub>a</sub> → 7.4 pH), the ratio of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&#x5B;A&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x5D;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&#x5B;HA&#x5D;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> changes by a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">factor of 10</span> for <i>each</i> integer.</div>"	"<div>For every 1 unit difference between the <b>pK<sub>a</sub></b>&nbsp;of a compound and the <b>pH</b> it's at&nbsp;(like 8.4 pK<sub>a</sub> → 7.4 pH), the ratio of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>[A</u><sup>-</sup><u>]</u></span>:<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>[HA]</u></span> changes by a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">factor of 10</span> for <i>each</i> integer.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>8.4 → 7.4 changes it by a factor of 10.</div><div>8.4 → 6.4 changes it by a factor of 100.</div><div>8.4 → 5.4 chanegs it by a factor of 1,000.</div><div><br></div><div>- Based off of the <b>Henderson-Hasselbalch equation</b> (below).</div><div>- Can be used for questions like ""the pK<sub>a</sub> of the compound is 8.4. What's the ratio of depronation:protonation at a pH of 7.4?""</div><div><br></div><img src=""72e98b59d03d8cc0b84752751d67a6b1.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""af26ca2388f6bd7ec4bb22326645e6f4.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>For every 1 unit difference between the <b>pK<sub>a</sub></b>&nbsp;of a compound and the <b>pH</b> it's at&nbsp;(like 8.4 pK<sub>a</sub> → 7.4 pH), the ratio of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>[A</u><sup>-</sup><u>]</u></span>:<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>[HA]</u></span> changes by a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""factor&#x20;of&#x20;10"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> for <i>each</i> integer.</div>"	"<div>For every 1 unit difference between the <b>pK<sub>a</sub></b>&nbsp;of a compound and the <b>pH</b> it's at&nbsp;(like 8.4 pK<sub>a</sub> → 7.4 pH), the ratio of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>[A</u><sup>-</sup><u>]</u></span>:<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>[HA]</u></span> changes by a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">factor of 10</span> for <i>each</i> integer.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>8.4 → 7.4 changes it by a factor of 10.</div><div>8.4 → 6.4 changes it by a factor of 100.</div><div>8.4 → 5.4 chanegs it by a factor of 1,000.</div><div><br></div><div>- Based off of the <b>Henderson-Hasselbalch equation</b> (below).</div><div>- Can be used for questions like ""the pK<sub>a</sub> of the compound is 8.4. What's the ratio of depronation:protonation at a pH of 7.4?""</div><div><br></div><img src=""72e98b59d03d8cc0b84752751d67a6b1.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""af26ca2388f6bd7ec4bb22326645e6f4.png""><br></div></div>"
"Water forms a <i>highly</i>-ordered <b>solvation layer</b> around hydro<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""phobic"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> residues on proteins."	"Water forms a <i>highly</i>-ordered <b>solvation layer</b> around hydro<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">phobic</span> residues on proteins.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Since it can readily hydrogen bond with hydrophilic residues, there's no need to form a ""highly-ordered network.""</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ee59824d78dd90495ef211217d3ff8dc4764d62a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>empirical formula</b></span> when given <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>percent composition</u></span> of each element:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;8ff9a1b58ba40cc6376c3e84fac8f054&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>empirical formula</b></span> when given <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>percent composition</u></span> of each element:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""8ff9a1b58ba40cc6376c3e84fac8f054.png""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- These <u>aren't</u> being multiplied. This only shows how dividing <b>(%)</b> / <b>(molar mass)</b> gives its subscript for the empirical formula.</div><div><br></div><img src=""empirical-formula-lab-n.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;equilibrium&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is a <u>ratio</u> of the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">forward rate (k)</span> <i>divided</i> by its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">backwards rate (k<sub>-1</sub>)</span> - its magnitude can <i>only</i> be altered by changing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>pressure</u></span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is a <u>ratio</u> of the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">forward rate (k)</span> <i>divided</i> by its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">backwards rate (k<sub>-1</sub>)</span> - its magnitude can <i>only</i> be altered by changing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>pressure</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Shows two different&nbsp;<b>rate equations (k<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;&amp; k<sub>r</sub>)</b>. Dividing k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub>&nbsp;tells you the dynamic equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>).<br></div><div><div>- k<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;&amp; k<sub>r</sub>&nbsp;are set equal to each other because at equilibrium, the rates of forward and backward are the same.</div><div><br></div><img src=""be0964ed9732f1bf43ad4898519a05a2.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c602a260f1a06043a1aaea43d6198d8a.png""></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is a <u>ratio</u> of the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""forward&#x20;rate&#x20;&#x28;k&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[constant]</span> <i>divided</i> by its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""backwards&#x20;rate&#x20;&#x28;k&lt;sub&gt;&#x2D;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[constant]</span> - its magnitude can <i>only</i> be altered by changing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>pressure</u></span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is a <u>ratio</u> of the reaction's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">forward rate (k)</span> <i>divided</i> by its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">backwards rate (k<sub>-1</sub>)</span> - its magnitude can <i>only</i> be altered by changing <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>pressure</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Shows two different&nbsp;<b>rate equations (k<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;&amp; k<sub>r</sub>)</b>. Dividing k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub>&nbsp;tells you the dynamic equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>).<br></div><div><div>- k<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;&amp; k<sub>r</sub>&nbsp;are set equal to each other because at equilibrium, the rates of forward and backward are the same.</div><div><br></div><img src=""be0964ed9732f1bf43ad4898519a05a2.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c602a260f1a06043a1aaea43d6198d8a.png""></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is a <u>ratio</u> of the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">forward rate (k)</span> <i>divided</i> by its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">backwards rate (k<sub>-1</sub>)</span> - its magnitude can <i>only</i> be altered by changing <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;temperature&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;pressure&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b></span>&nbsp;is a <u>ratio</u> of the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">forward rate (k)</span> <i>divided</i> by its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">backwards rate (k<sub>-1</sub>)</span> - its magnitude can <i>only</i> be altered by changing <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>temperature</u></span> or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>pressure</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Shows two different&nbsp;<b>rate equations (k<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;&amp; k<sub>r</sub>)</b>. Dividing k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub>&nbsp;tells you the dynamic equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>).<br></div><div><div>- k<sub>f</sub>&nbsp;&amp; k<sub>r</sub>&nbsp;are set equal to each other because at equilibrium, the rates of forward and backward are the same.</div><div><br></div><img src=""be0964ed9732f1bf43ad4898519a05a2.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""c602a260f1a06043a1aaea43d6198d8a.png""></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Hypochlorite</b></span> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ClO&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, with a formal charge of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x2D;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Hypochlorite</b></span> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ClO<sup>-</sup></span>, with a formal charge of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">-1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- ClO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>&nbsp;→ Chlorate<div>- ClO<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup>&nbsp;→ Chlorite</div><div>- ClO<sup>-</sup>&nbsp;→ Hypochlorite</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Hypochlorite&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ClO<sup>-</sup></span>, with a formal charge of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">-1</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Hypochlorite</b></span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ClO<sup>-</sup></span>, with a formal charge of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">-1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- ClO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>&nbsp;→ Chlorate<div>- ClO<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup>&nbsp;→ Chlorite</div><div>- ClO<sup>-</sup>&nbsp;→ Hypochlorite</div></div>"
"<div>Equation that relates the <b>K<sub>a/b</sub></b> of an acid/base to <b>K<sub>a/b,conjugate</sub></b> (conjugate acid/base's dissociation constant):<br></div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;2675c3c6b8f0c23a6f9257a9c4ff6ab1b092f64a&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation that relates the <b>K<sub>a/b</sub></b> of an acid/base to <b>K<sub>a/b,conjugate</sub></b> (conjugate acid/base's dissociation constant):<br></div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-2675c3c6b8f0c23a6f9257a9c4ff6ab1b092f64a.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Multiplying the K<sub>a</sub> of an acid by the K<sub>b</sub> of its conjugate base will always give you the <b>water dissociation constant (K<sub>w</sub>)</b> (1 x 10<sup>-14</sup>).</div>"
"<div>A compound's <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;inversely&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[directly/inversely]</span> <u>proportional</u> to its <b>K<sub>b</sub></b>.</div>"	"<div>A compound's <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>inversely</i></span> <u>proportional</u> to its <b>K<sub>b</sub></b>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- If a compound has a high K<sub>a</sub> (good acid), it has a low K<sub>b</sub> (bad base).<div>- Weak acids/weak bases tend to produce conjugates that are <u>also weak</u>, though.</div></div>"
"The <b>conjugate acids/bases</b> of <u><i>strong</i> acids/bases</u> are called <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""inert"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> species, because they're <i>so</i> weak that they're <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""mostly&#x20;unreactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The <b>conjugate acids/bases</b> of <u><i>strong</i> acids/bases</u> are called <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">inert</span> species, because they're <i>so</i> weak that they're <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">mostly unreactive</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"The <i>reverse</i> of a <b>neutralization reaction</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hydrolysis"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> reaction."	"The <i>reverse</i> of a <b>neutralization reaction</b> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">hydrolysis</span> reaction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Hydrolysis because water is used (which is often a product of neutralization reactions) to cleave a salt into its constituent ionized species.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d37a41a0a74919ea02b38958a4035fa5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""267c0ec1563bca10d67a36ea78fc8429.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Polyvalent&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> acids or bases are compounds that have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>multiple</i> equivalences (H<sup>+</sup> or OH<sup>-</sup>)</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Polyvalent</b></span> acids or bases are compounds that have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>multiple</i> equivalences (H<sup>+</sup> or OH<sup>-</sup>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Could also be called <u>polyprotic</u> compounds.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0e8815734665afe4ab4077c9df02dc908c582a74.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Polyvalent</b></span> acids or bases are compounds that have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;multiple&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;equivalences&#x20;&#x28;H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;or&#x20;OH&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Polyvalent</b></span> acids or bases are compounds that have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>multiple</i> equivalences (H<sup>+</sup> or OH<sup>-</sup>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Could also be called <u>polyprotic</u> compounds.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0e8815734665afe4ab4077c9df02dc908c582a74.jpg""></div>"
"<div>On a titration curve, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;equivalence&#x20;point&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> indicates the point where 100% of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">starting acid/base</span> has <i>turned into</i> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conjugate form</u></span>.</div>"	"<div>On a titration curve, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equivalence point</b></span> indicates the point where 100% of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">starting acid/base</span> has <i>turned into</i> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conjugate form</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- As you continue to add H<sup>+</sup> equivalences (example below), the pH drops significantly, because the other OH<sup>-</sup> equivalences in solution have been ""sopped up"" at the equivalent point.</div><div>- The <b>half-equivalence</b> point indicates a <u>buffer region</u> - not the equivalence point.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""main-qimg-cf2f53b7dbf9c04434a2706f68221454.png""><br></div>"
"<div>On a titration curve, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equivalence point</b></span> indicates the point where 100% of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""starting&#x20;acid&#x2F;base"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> has <i>turned into</i> its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;conjugate&#x20;form&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>On a titration curve, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>equivalence point</b></span> indicates the point where 100% of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">starting acid/base</span> has <i>turned into</i> its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>conjugate form</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- As you continue to add H<sup>+</sup> equivalences (example below), the pH drops significantly, because the other OH<sup>-</sup> equivalences in solution have been ""sopped up"" at the equivalent point.</div><div>- The <b>half-equivalence</b> point indicates a <u>buffer region</u> - not the equivalence point.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""main-qimg-cf2f53b7dbf9c04434a2706f68221454.png""><br></div>"
"Titration curves with <b>equivalence points</b> <i>exactly</i> at a pH of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""7"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>acids/bases</u>."	"Titration curves with <b>equivalence points</b> <i>exactly</i> at a pH of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">7</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>acids/bases</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Having an equivalence point away from a pH of 7 indicates a <u>weak</u> acid/base.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Titration curves with <b>equivalence points</b> <i>exactly</i> at a pH of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">7</span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weak/strong]</span> <u>acids/bases</u>."	"Titration curves with <b>equivalence points</b> <i>exactly</i> at a pH of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">7</span> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>acids/bases</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Having an equivalence point away from a pH of 7 indicates a <u>weak</u> acid/base.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Titration curves with <b>equivalence points</b>&nbsp;above or below&nbsp;a pH of 7 indicate that you have at least one <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weak/strong]</span> <u>acid</u>&nbsp;or <u>base</u>."	"Titration curves with <b>equivalence points</b>&nbsp;above or below&nbsp;a pH of 7 indicate that you have at least one <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> <u>acid</u>&nbsp;or <u>base</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Having an equivalence point exactly at a pH of 7 indicates a strong acid/base though.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>moles </i>of acid or base</span> present at the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">equivalence point</span> in a <u>titration</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>moles </i>of acid or base</span> present at the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">equivalence point</span> in a <u>titration</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>N<sub>a/b</sub></b> = normality (Molarity x equivalences); <b>V</b> = volume (either added or started with).</div><div>- <b>Normality</b> is used in place of molarity to account for the number of equivalences it has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg"">&nbsp;<br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;moles&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;of&#x20;acid&#x20;or&#x20;base"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> present at the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""equivalence&#x20;point"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in a <u>titration</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""></span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation to find the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>moles </i>of acid or base</span> present at the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">equivalence point</span> in a <u>titration</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>N<sub>a/b</sub></b> = normality (Molarity x equivalences); <b>V</b> = volume (either added or started with).</div><div>- <b>Normality</b> is used in place of molarity to account for the number of equivalences it has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg"">&nbsp;<br></div></div>"
"According to <b>Charles' Law</b>, an&nbsp;<i>increase</i> in <u>temperature</u> <i>increases</i> <u>volume</u> by giving gas particles&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;greater&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - leading to more collisions &amp; expansion of the gas - when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pressure is constant</span>."	"According to <b>Charles' Law</b>, an&nbsp;<i>increase</i> in <u>temperature</u> <i>increases</i> <u>volume</u> by giving gas particles&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater&nbsp;</i>kinetic energy</span> - leading to more collisions &amp; expansion of the gas - when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pressure is constant</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Normally a greater number of collisions results in increased pressure, but Charles' Law works under the assumption that <u>pressure is held constant</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""fcc31c63994906d0c57a292eafc989c6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-53ab44ebc23161cba67f830bc3d12fb765c04935.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In the <u>ideal gas equation</u>, <b>T</b> should <i>always</i> be in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Kelvin&#x20;&#x28;K&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[units]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""08beb446d46233a83f3d5c0be17c7bd6.png"">"	"<div>In the <u>ideal gas equation</u>, <b>T</b> should <i>always</i> be in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Kelvin (K)</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""08beb446d46233a83f3d5c0be17c7bd6.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>P</b>&nbsp;= pressure (either atm or Pa)<div><b>V</b>&nbsp;= volume (either L or m<sup>2</sup>)</div><div><b>n</b>&nbsp;= number of moles</div><div><b>T</b>&nbsp;= temperature (K)</div><div><b>R</b>&nbsp;= either 8.3 J/mol or 0.083 Latm/mol</div></div>"
"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mole&#x20;fraction&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of O<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">0.8</span>.</div>"	"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mole fraction</u></span> of O<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">0.8</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Mole fraction of gases can be expressed as percentages. It represents the number of moles contributed by a gas to the total number of moles. Out of 100 moles of gas molecules, O<sub>2</sub> makes up 80% of them.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fc1b9dfcbdfa6b5beaac93117f43680cb157437f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mole fraction</u></span> of O<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""0&#x2E;8"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mole fraction</u></span> of O<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">0.8</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Mole fraction of gases can be expressed as percentages. It represents the number of moles contributed by a gas to the total number of moles. Out of 100 moles of gas molecules, O<sub>2</sub> makes up 80% of them.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fc1b9dfcbdfa6b5beaac93117f43680cb157437f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mole&#x20;fraction&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of N<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">0.2</span>.</div>"	"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mole fraction</u></span> of N<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">0.2</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Mole fraction of gases can be expressed as percentages. It represents the number of moles contributed by a gas to the total number of moles. Out of 100 moles of gas molecules, N<sub>2</sub> makes up 20% of them.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fc1b9dfcbdfa6b5beaac93117f43680cb157437f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mole fraction</u></span> of N<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""0&#x2E;2"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"A gas chamber is 80% O<sub>2</sub> and 20% N<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mole fraction</u></span> of N<sub>2</sub> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">0.2</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Mole fraction of gases can be expressed as percentages. It represents the number of moles contributed by a gas to the total number of moles. Out of 100 moles of gas molecules, N<sub>2</sub> makes up 20% of them.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fc1b9dfcbdfa6b5beaac93117f43680cb157437f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The <u>percent <i>concentration</i></u> of a gas molecule in a mixture of different gases refers to its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;mole&#x20;fraction&#x20;&#x28;X&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The <u>percent <i>concentration</i></u> of a gas molecule in a mixture of different gases refers to its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>mole fraction (X)</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- ""80% of the mixture is O<sub>2</sub>"" → O<sub>2</sub>'s mole fraction is 0.8.</div><div>- Mole fraction is the number of moles that a gas contributes to the total number of moles in the mixture.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-fc1b9dfcbdfa6b5beaac93117f43680cb157437f.jpg""></div></div>"
"On a free energy diagram, the reaction's <u>rate-determining step</u> is the step with the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;highest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;activation&#x20;energy"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"On a free energy diagram, the reaction's <u>rate-determining step</u> is the step with the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>highest</i> activation energy</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>If the reaction uses intermediates and the second-step is the one with the greatest E<sub>a</sub>, the second step is rate-limiting.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-aa4742fa46f33b33b34eed6156501be56c2e723a.jpg""><br></div>"
"In chemical kinetics, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;elementary&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[category]</span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions that occur in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>one</i> <i>single</i> step</u> (no intermediates)</span>."	"In chemical kinetics, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>elementary</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions that occur in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>one</i> <i>single</i> step</u> (no intermediates)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Rate law for elementary reactions → k[reactant][reactant].</div><div>- <u>Stoichiometric coefficients</u> are equal to the rate orders for elementary reactions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a7545c54135541013d2c5f0733b1b041237632b0.jpg""></div>"
"In chemical kinetics, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>elementary</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions that occur in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;one&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;i&gt;single&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;step&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;no&#x20;intermediates&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In chemical kinetics, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>elementary</b></span> <b>reactions</b> are reactions that occur in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>one</i> <i>single</i> step</u> (no intermediates)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Rate law for elementary reactions → k[reactant][reactant].</div><div>- <u>Stoichiometric coefficients</u> are equal to the rate orders for elementary reactions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a7545c54135541013d2c5f0733b1b041237632b0.jpg""></div>"
"<div>In chemical kinetics, the <b>rate law</b> for reactions with <i>more</i> <i>than one</i> step is based on the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rate&#x2D;limiting&#x20;&#x28;slowest&#x29;&#x20;step"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>In chemical kinetics, the <b>rate law</b> for reactions with <i>more</i> <i>than one</i> step is based on the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">rate-limiting (slowest) step</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- k[reactant1][reactant2]</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-60679f8e1bbcbf079fa893a34c2d0ae010d1b885.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a7545c54135541013d2c5f0733b1b041237632b0.jpg""></div>"
"In free energy diagrams, <u>reaction intermediates</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;do&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[do/do not]</span> factor into the <b>rate law</b> when the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span> step is the rate-limiting step."	"In free energy diagrams, <u>reaction intermediates</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> factor into the <b>rate law</b> when the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span> step is the rate-limiting step.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- When the <u>first</u> step is rate-limiting, the intermediate is formed as a ""product."" Rate laws only focus on reactants, so it is not factored in.</div><div>- When the <u>second</u> step is rate-limiting, the intermediate acts as a ""reactant."" Since rate laws only care about reactants, the intermediate <u>does</u> play a role in the rate law.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-aa4742fa46f33b33b34eed6156501be56c2e723a.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In free energy diagrams, <u>reaction intermediates</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> factor into the <b>rate law</b> when the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""first"" data-ordinal=""2"">[first/second]</span> step is the rate-limiting step."	"In free energy diagrams, <u>reaction intermediates</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> factor into the <b>rate law</b> when the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span> step is the rate-limiting step.
<hr>

"In chemical kinetics, <b>rate laws</b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span>&nbsp;include products - <i>only</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>reactants</u></span>.

"Reactions under <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;kinetic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/thermo.]</span> conditions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>irreversible</i></span>."	"Reactions under <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> conditions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>irreversible</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Kinetically favored reactions are irreversible because they occur at <u>very low temperatures</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"

"Reactions under <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> conditions are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>irreversible</i></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Kinetically favored reactions are irreversible because they occur at <u>very low temperatures</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"
"Reactions that are under <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;thermodynamic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/thermo.]</span> control are <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reversible</span>."	"Reactions that are under <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>thermodynamic</b></span> control are <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">reversible</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Thermodynamically favored reactions are reversible because they <u>occur at high temperatures</u> - the forward and backward reactions are able to take place.</div><div>- Under thermodynamic conditions, kinetic products can still form (as minor products). Thermodynamic product predominates in solution because the theramal energy available allows it to reverse and form the thermodynamic product instead.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"
"Reactions that are under <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>thermodynamic</b></span> control are <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reversible"" data-ordinal=""2"">[reversible/irreversible]</span>."	"Reactions that are under <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>thermodynamic</b></span> control are <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">reversible</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Thermodynamically favored reactions are reversible because they <u>occur at high temperatures</u> - the forward and backward reactions are able to take place.</div><div>- Under thermodynamic conditions, kinetic products can still form (as minor products). Thermodynamic product predominates in solution because the theramal energy available allows it to reverse and form the thermodynamic product instead.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"
"For reactions under <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;kinetic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/thermodynamic]</span> control, the <u><i>most</i> amount of products</u> will form under conditions that give it the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lowest </i><u>activation energy</u></span>."	"For reactions under <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> control, the <u><i>most</i> amount of products</u> will form under conditions that give it the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lowest </i><u>activation energy</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Even if there are different conditions available that have lower ΔG's, the one with the lowest activation energy will form the highest amount of products.</div><div>- For thermodynamic control, the conditions that give it the lowest ΔG will form the most products.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"
"For reactions under <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> control, the <u><i>most</i> amount of products</u> will form under conditions that give it the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lowest&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;activation&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"For reactions under <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>kinetic</b></span> control, the <u><i>most</i> amount of products</u> will form under conditions that give it the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lowest </i><u>activation energy</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Even if there are different conditions available that have lower ΔG's, the one with the lowest activation energy will form the highest amount of products.</div><div>- For thermodynamic control, the conditions that give it the lowest ΔG will form the most products.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"
"For reactions under <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;thermodynamic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[kinetic/thermodynamic]</span> control, the <i>most</i> amount of <u>products</u> will form under conditions that give it the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>lowest ΔG</u></span>."	"For reactions under <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>thermodynamic</b></span> control, the <i>most</i> amount of <u>products</u> will form under conditions that give it the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>lowest ΔG</u></span>.

<div><b>A</b> = frequency factor; <b>E<sub>a</sub></b> = activation energy; <b>R</b> = ideal gas constant; <b>T</b> = temperature (Kelvin).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Increasing</span> the <u>temperature</u> of an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;endo&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span><b>thermic</b> reaction shifts it to favor its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">products</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Increasing</span> the <u>temperature</u> of an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reaction shifts it to favor its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">products</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Endothermic reactions ""absorb"" heat (think of heat like a reactant). When you add heat, according to LeChateliers principle, the reaction is shifted forwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7640ed558a308e456d802bd63bb0eeaadf864ac4.jpg""></div>"
"A <b>solvent</b> is defined as having the ability to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;dissolve&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;a&#x20;solute"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"A <b>solvent</b> is defined as having the ability to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>dissolve</i> a solute</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-541e97e70195e64947c349ff877ab2a24a6b8418.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Colligative properties</b></span>&nbsp;are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""physical"" data-ordinal=""3"">[chemical/physical]</span> properties in solution that <i>only </i>rely on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>number&nbsp;</i>of dissolved particles</u></span> but not<i> </i>on the <i>specific</i> identities of those particles."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Colligative properties</b></span>&nbsp;are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">physical</span> properties in solution that <i>only </i>rely on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>number&nbsp;</i>of dissolved particles</u></span> but not<i> </i>on the <i>specific</i> identities of those particles.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Ex: osmotic pressure.<div>- The ability of a substance to boil, freeze, or vaporize has <u>nothing</u> to do with the chemical properties of the substance (like its reactivity).</div></div>"
"1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">atm</span>&nbsp;= <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">760</span> mmHg = <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">760</span> Torr = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x20;x&#x20;10&lt;sup&gt;5&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> Pa."	"1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">atm</span>&nbsp;= <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">760</span> mmHg = <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">760</span> Torr = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">1 x 10<sup>5</sup></span> Pa.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>Torr</b> is just another way to say ""mmHg.""</div>"
"<div>In the expression for the equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>), the <u>concentrations</u> of compounds ([A], [B], etc.) are expressed in units of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;molarity&#x20;&#x28;M&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[units]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""48bf4c5fa25222bde9718ed1e1fc5324.png"">"	"<div>In the expression for the equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>), the <u>concentrations</u> of compounds ([A], [B], etc.) are expressed in units of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>molarity (M)</b></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""48bf4c5fa25222bde9718ed1e1fc5324.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Sometimes referred to as <b>molar concentrations</b>.</div>"
"The <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub style="""">eq</sub>)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> have a <u>unit</u>."	"The <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub style="""">eq</sub>)</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> have a <u>unit</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- K<sub>eq</sub> is a unitless quantity.</div><div>- Units for the concentrations of the compounds ([A], [B], etc.) are molarity (M).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""48bf4c5fa25222bde9718ed1e1fc5324.png""><br></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;law&#x20;of&#x20;mass&#x20;action&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[law]</span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reaction quotient (Q)</u></span> will <i>change</i> until it eventually reaches its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equilibrium point (K</u><sub>eq</sub>)</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>law of mass action</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reaction quotient (Q)</u></span> will <i>change</i> until it eventually reaches its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equilibrium point (K</u><sub>eq</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-47c32ad784e21258abcd2ebc461b56832669af17.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>law of mass action</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;reaction&#x20;quotient&#x20;&#x28;Q&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> will <i>change</i> until it eventually reaches its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;equilibrium&#x20;point&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>law of mass action</b></span> says that the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reaction quotient (Q)</u></span> will <i>change</i> until it eventually reaches its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equilibrium point (K</u><sub>eq</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-47c32ad784e21258abcd2ebc461b56832669af17.jpg""></div>"
"For <b>single-step</b> <b>reactions</b>, reagents with <i>higher</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""stoichiometric&#x20;coefficients"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> play <i>larger</i> roles in the <u>reaction's rate</u>."	"For <b>single-step</b> <b>reactions</b>, reagents with <i>higher</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">stoichiometric coefficients</span> play <i>larger</i> roles in the <u>reaction's rate</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Elementary reactions are reactions that occur in only&nbsp;<u>one single step</u>&nbsp;(no intermediates).</div><div>- This is because stoichiometric coefficients are <u>equivalent to rate orders</u> for <b style="""">elementary reactions</b> <b>only</b>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-89f4243e8068fae728e08b2e6c91dde3d67de51a.jpg""></div>"
"Increasing the <u>surface area</u> for a <b>catalyst</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> have an effect on the reaction's rate."	"Increasing the <u>surface area</u> for a <b>catalyst</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does</i></span> have an effect on the reaction's rate.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Increasing the surface area increases the reaction's rate.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-272dd732e20d7c72e3be53cf8d4f34ed25fd53dd.jpg""></div>"
"Breaking a <b>catalyst</b> into <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;smaller&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[larger/smaller]</span> particles <i style="""">increases</i> its <u>surface area</u> moreso than leaving it as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;large&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[small/large]</span> particles."	"Breaking a <b>catalyst</b> into <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>smaller</i></span> particles <i style="""">increases</i> its <u>surface area</u> moreso than leaving it as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>large</i></span> particles.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Increasing the surface area increases the reaction's rate.</div><div>- Making it smaller increases its surface area.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-272dd732e20d7c72e3be53cf8d4f34ed25fd53dd.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Smooth</span> surfaces have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[lower/higher]</span> <u>surface areas</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">rough</span>&nbsp;surfaces."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Smooth</span> surfaces have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <u>surface areas</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">rough</span>&nbsp;surfaces.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-272dd732e20d7c72e3be53cf8d4f34ed25fd53dd.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"On a <u>free energy diagram</u>, the <b>enthalpy</b> of the reaction can be calculated by <i>subtracting</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""product&#x27;s&#x20;energy"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reactants&#x20;energy"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"On a <u>free energy diagram</u>, the <b>enthalpy</b> of the reaction can be calculated by <i>subtracting</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">product's energy</span> - <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">reactants energy</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is also how you calculate <b>free energy (ΔG)</b>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5bf1ca23965346e3b6eab0fd83438cb56488daa5.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a401da8d56426a022db6b5326c41f3f3e6af91d7.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Exo&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increase</i></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Exo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increase</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>As heat is being released, the system becomes hotter.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1665b0842198bd1c6c122dba04ef2e59e7b1776f.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Exo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increase&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increase/decrease]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Exo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increase</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>As heat is being released, the system becomes hotter.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1665b0842198bd1c6c122dba04ef2e59e7b1776f.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Endo&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decrease</i></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decrease</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>As heat is being absorbed, the system becomes cooler.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1665b0842198bd1c6c122dba04ef2e59e7b1776f.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decrease&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increase/decrease]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reactions cause the overall <u>temperature</u> of the system to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decrease</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>As heat is being absorbed, the system becomes cooler.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1665b0842198bd1c6c122dba04ef2e59e7b1776f.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Smooth"" data-ordinal=""2"">[rough/smooth]</span> surfaces have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <u>surface areas</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rough"" data-ordinal=""2"">[rough/smooth]</span>&nbsp;surfaces."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Smooth</span> surfaces have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <u>surface areas</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">rough</span>&nbsp;surfaces.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-272dd732e20d7c72e3be53cf8d4f34ed25fd53dd.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Kinetic</b> <b>products</b> can <i>reverse</i> back to their initial reactants to form the <u>thermodynamic product</u> instead <i>only</i> when temperature is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""high"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span>."	"<b>Kinetic</b> <b>products</b> can <i>reverse</i> back to their initial reactants to form the <u>thermodynamic product</u> instead <i>only</i> when temperature is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">high</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Both kinetic &amp; thermodynamic products can form under high temperatures. However, kinetic products can reverse themselves to form the thermodynamic product instead.<br></div><div>- When it's under ""kinetic conditions"" (i.e. low temperature), the reaction is irreversible.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c4c26a88447334abc6e953300fa809b4.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""0984787d7c7431963dcba6d0954a031d.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mixed order</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are those that&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;change&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;in&#x20;&lt;u&gt;rate&#x20;order&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;as the reaction progresses."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Mixed order</b></span> <b>reactions </b>are those that&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>change </i>in <u>rate order</u></span>&nbsp;as the reaction progresses.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Ex: reaction starts as a first order reaction but later progresses to second order.</div><div>- <b>A</b> = single reactant; <b>C</b> = catalyst</div><div><br></div><img src=""e1aedf524b550603c1ee4b34245e8165.png""></div>"
"According to the <b>Heisenberg uncertainity principle</b>, the <i>more</i> accurately we know an electron's <u>momentum</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[more/less]</span> accurately we know its <u>position</u>."	"According to the <b>Heisenberg uncertainity principle</b>, the <i>more</i> accurately we know an electron's <u>momentum</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> accurately we know its <u>position</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...and vice versa.</div><div>- Because of this, we can only describe an electron's location with a probability function.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9190c35526462964b8b8543d1a3d19d78b43b2d4.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Ferromagnetic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> materials are materials with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">iron</span> that exhibit <i>strong</i> <u>paramagnetic</u> attractions to magnetic fields."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ferromagnetic</b></span> materials are materials with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">iron</span> that exhibit <i>strong</i> <u>paramagnetic</u> attractions to magnetic fields.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is just a <u>special case</u> of paramagnetism that involves iron.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-90fff3e9909b1446271768aec5572d428404edac.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ferromagnetic</b></span> materials are materials with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""iron"" data-ordinal=""2"">[element]</span> that exhibit <i>strong</i> <u>paramagnetic</u> attractions to magnetic fields."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ferromagnetic</b></span> materials are materials with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">iron</span> that exhibit <i>strong</i> <u>paramagnetic</u> attractions to magnetic fields.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-88dce064636bcaf8a52b1839eaa90b6df9e45848.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Chelate formation</b> reactions occur with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>bidendate</u></span> ligands - ligands that have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""2"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> atoms on their structures that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;coordinately&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;donate&#x20;electrons"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to the cation."	"<b>Chelate formation</b> reactions occur with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>bidendate</u></span> ligands - ligands that have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">2</span> atoms on their structures that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>coordinately</i> donate electrons</span> to the cation.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The below compound has 2 NH<sub>2</sub> groups (which can both donate electrons to form coordinate complexes with the central cation) - making it bidendate.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-633bd2262f4e07970aa59813d34b6cabafeb171a.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-737ba282a5ece89552b4269df8b503dbb4cd6a07.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Chelate formation</b> reactions occur with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;bidendate&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> ligands - ligands that have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">2</span> atoms on their structures that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>coordinately</i> donate electrons</span> to the cation."	"<b>Chelate formation</b> reactions occur with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>bidendate</u></span> ligands - ligands that have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">2</span> atoms on their structures that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>coordinately</i> donate electrons</span> to the cation.

<div>The below compound has 2 NH<sub>2</sub> groups (which can both donate electrons to form coordinate complexes with the central cation) - making it bidendate.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-633bd2262f4e07970aa59813d34b6cabafeb171a.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-737ba282a5ece89552b4269df8b503dbb4cd6a07.jpg""></div>"
"To calculate an element's <b>electron affinity</b>, <i>subtract</i> its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;initial&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;without&#x20;electron&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;final&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;with&#x20;electron&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"To calculate an element's <b>electron affinity</b>, <i>subtract</i> its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>initial energy</u> (without electron)</span> - <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>final energy</u> (with electron)</span>.

"<b>Isotopes</b> exhibit <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">the same</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;chemical&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[physical/chemical]</span>&nbsp;properties."	"<b>Isotopes</b> exhibit <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i style="""">the same</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>chemical</u></span>&nbsp;properties.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since they still have the same number of valence electrons.</div><div>- Isotopes have different physical properties though, since they have different densities &amp; masses.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""fba0d4e607bc35af1f14d038629b5f9c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Isotopes</b>&nbsp;exhibit <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[the same/different]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>physical</u></span>&nbsp;properties."	"<b>Isotopes</b>&nbsp;exhibit <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>physical</u></span>&nbsp;properties.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- They have the same chemical properties, but they exhibit different physical properties (different density &amp; mass).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""fba0d4e607bc35af1f14d038629b5f9c.png""></div></div>"
"<b>Isotopes</b>&nbsp;exhibit <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;physical&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[physical/chemical]</span>&nbsp;properties."	"<b>Isotopes</b>&nbsp;exhibit <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>physical</u></span>&nbsp;properties.

<div>Aluminum (Al): +3</div><div>Silver (Ag): +1</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-611670e5fdce0831277fddeb7c29936e5e73f4cd.jpg""></div>"
"Steps to writing the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;empirical&#x20;formula&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a compound when given the <u>weight</u> of each element:<div><br></div><div>1. Divide the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>given element's weight</u></span> / <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>element's atomic mass</u></span> to get the moles for each.</div><div>2. Divide each by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">lowest</i> numbered mole</span>.</div><div>3. Compare mole ratios.</div>"	"Steps to writing the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>empirical formula</b></span> of a compound when given the <u>weight</u> of each element:<div><br></div><div>1. Divide the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>given element's weight</u></span> / <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>element's atomic mass</u></span> to get the moles for each.</div><div>2. Divide each by the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i style="""">lowest</i> numbered mole</span>.</div><div>3. Compare mole ratios.</div>

Divide each by the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;lowest&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;numbered&#x20;mole"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.</div><div>3. Compare mole ratios.</div>"	"Steps to writing the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>empirical formula</b></span> of a compound when given the <u>weight</u> of each element:<div><br></div><div>1.
