<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-c658c016074629023fba9a21180f30aa1872a927.jpg""></div>"
"<i>Exceptions</i> to the <b>ionization energy</b> trend:<div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Nitrogen"" data-ordinal=""1"">[O/N]</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""oxygen"" data-ordinal=""1"">[O/N]</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Berylium"" data-ordinal=""1"">[Be/B]</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""boron"" data-ordinal=""1"">[Be/B]</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Magnesium"" data-ordinal=""1"">[Al/Mg]</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Aluminum"" data-ordinal=""1"">[Al/Mg]</span></div>"	"<i>Exceptions</i> to the <b>ionization energy</b> trend:<div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Nitrogen</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">oxygen</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Berylium</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">boron</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Magnesium</span> &gt; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Aluminum</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Nitrogen has a 1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>3</sup> electron configuration, which is more stable than oxygen's (1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>4</sup>). It's therefore harder to pull off an electron from N than O. The same logic applies to Berylium &gt; Boron.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f38de24231072df639df5147db58f59a1f4dff1.jpg""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Nitrogen&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[O/N]</span> has a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <b>ionization energy</b> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;oxygen&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[O/N]</span> because its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">electron configuration is <i>more stable</i></span> - requiring <i>more</i> energy to pull off the more tightly bound electron.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Nitrogen</u></span> has a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <b>ionization energy</b> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>oxygen</u></span> because its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">electron configuration is <i>more stable</i></span> - requiring <i>more</i> energy to pull off the more tightly bound electron.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Two <u>exceptions</u> to the <b>ionization energy</b> trend: N &gt; O &amp; Be &gt; B.</div><div>- Nitrogen has a 1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>3</sup> electron configuration, which is more stable than oxygen's (1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>4</sup>). It's therefore harder to pull off an electron from N than O. The same logic applies to Berylium &gt; Boron.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f38de24231072df639df5147db58f59a1f4dff1.jpg""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Nitrogen</u></span> has a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher]</span> <b>ionization energy</b> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>oxygen</u></span> because its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">electron configuration is <i>more stable</i></span> - requiring <i>more</i> energy to pull off the more tightly bound electron.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Nitrogen</u></span> has a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <b>ionization energy</b> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>oxygen</u></span> because its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">electron configuration is <i>more stable</i></span> - requiring <i>more</i> energy to pull off the more tightly bound electron.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Two <u>exceptions</u> to the <b>ionization energy</b> trend: N &gt; O &amp; Be &gt; B.</div><div>- Nitrogen has a 1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>3</sup> electron configuration, which is more stable than oxygen's (1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>4</sup>). It's therefore harder to pull off an electron from N than O. The same logic applies to Berylium &gt; Boron.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f38de24231072df639df5147db58f59a1f4dff1.jpg""></div>"
"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Nitrogen</u></span> has a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <b>ionization energy</b> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>oxygen</u></span> because its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""electron&#x20;configuration&#x20;is&#x20;&lt;i&gt;more&#x20;stable&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> - requiring <i>more</i> energy to pull off the more tightly bound electron.</div>"	"<div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Nitrogen</u></span> has a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> <b>ionization energy</b> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>oxygen</u></span> because its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">electron configuration is <i>more stable</i></span> - requiring <i>more</i> energy to pull off the more tightly bound electron.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Two <u>exceptions</u> to the <b>ionization energy</b> trend: N &gt; O &amp; Be &gt; B.</div><div>- Nitrogen has a 1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>3</sup> electron configuration, which is more stable than oxygen's (1s<sup>2</sup>2s<sup>2</sup>2p<sup>4</sup>). It's therefore harder to pull off an electron from N than O. The same logic applies to Berylium &gt; Boron.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f38de24231072df639df5147db58f59a1f4dff1.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Gases with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;PV&#x2F;RT&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;ratio"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>above</i></span><i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1</span>&nbsp;exhibit <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermolecular repulsions</u></span> - causing it to behave more real.</div>"	"<div>Gases with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>PV/RT</b> ratio</span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>above</i></span><i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1</span>&nbsp;exhibit <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermolecular repulsions</u></span> - causing it to behave more real.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>Ethyl acetate</b>&nbsp;is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""polar"" data-ordinal=""1"">[polar/non-polar]</span> molecule that can act as a suitable solvent for the dissolution of <u>hydro</u><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;phobic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> compounds."	"<b>Ethyl acetate</b>&nbsp;is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">polar</span> molecule that can act as a suitable solvent for the dissolution of <u>hydro</u><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>phobic</u></span> compounds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Ethyl acetate is normally a <b>polar molecule</b>, but it can't <u>donate</u> hydogen atoms during H-bonding. Non-polar compounds therefore don't H-bond with the solvent (since they also lack O-H bonds capable of H-bonding).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b76d01d906dd52d99ced4b28491f4c7f9d545573.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Plating/galvanization</b> can <i>only</i> take place on the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;cathode&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[anode/cathode]</span>, since that's the <i>only</i> site where ions are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""reduced&#x20;into&#x20;solids"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Plating/galvanization</b> can <i>only</i> take place on the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>cathode</u></span>, since that's the <i>only</i> site where ions are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">reduced into solids</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Doesn't happen on the Zinc bar since that's the site of oxidation (ZnSO<sub>4</sub>&nbsp;leads to Zn<sup>2+</sup>&nbsp;instead of causing precipitation).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""098cf7ba35a1957a943cf3c8c111ff96.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8ebfa5a776631ba0a62e93d2dcfcd66a.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""9f5660936ee87c6cdbdff58c42d55525.png""></div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ion product (IP)</b></span> describes the <i>current </i>amount of ions&nbsp;<u>dissolved in solution</u> <i>before </i>they reach the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""solubility&#x20;equilibrium&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;sp&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ion product (IP)</b></span> describes the <i>current </i>amount of ions&nbsp;<u>dissolved in solution</u> <i>before </i>they reach the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">solubility equilibrium (K<sub>sp</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>Same exact equation as K<sub style="""">sp</sub></b> (multiplying the concentrations of the separated ionic species), except it only describes the solubility at that point in time relative to the equilibrium.</div><div><br></div><img src=""2c7d40a6e50860616ae77cf5f33e6672.png""></div>"
"Throughout the course of a reaction, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;intermediates&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are only <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>present</i></span>&nbsp;towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>middle</u></span> of the reaction - they're mostly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>absent</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">beginning</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">end</span>."	"Throughout the course of a reaction, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediates</b></span> are only <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>present</i></span>&nbsp;towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>middle</u></span> of the reaction - they're mostly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>absent</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">beginning</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">end</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In the case below, <b>[ES]</b> is the <u>intermediate</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-bceaf7e07cd2f9388ab26f82f401081637ce1e22.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7d61dfa7c81d2bc3d88c1af38ab76db7eb210f36.jpg""></div>"
"Throughout the course of a reaction, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>intermediates</b></span> are only <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>present</i></span>&nbsp;towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;middle&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/middle/end]</span> of the reaction - they're mostly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>absent</i></span> towards the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""beginning"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/middle/end]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""end"" data-ordinal=""2"">[beginning/middle/end]</span>." OH group <u>overpowers</u> the non-polar nature of the CH<sub>3</sub> group.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8287cc2e3b5276e3e17910238ceef10bc4dfa7c5.jpg""><br></div></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Can be used to dissolve fatty acids.</div><div><img src=""paste-5067e8643b3ad54ea1063d57bc471dbcce7aa00e.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;electrolytic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[galvanic/electro.]</span> cells, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>anodization</b></span> is the endothermic process of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>depositing</i> a metal layer</span> on the <i>surface</i> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">anode</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electrolytic</u></span> cells, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>anodization</b></span> is the endothermic process of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>depositing</i> a metal layer</span> on the <i>surface</i> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">anode</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- In order for a metal to be deposited on the anode (instead of the cathode), the direction of the current would have to be flowing from the cathode → anode (reversed).</div><div>- Endothermic because it's an electrolytic cell, so it requires an external source of energy to proceed.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d5df444aa46517ca0ac1b9b21adeb28ad4983092.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electrolytic</u></span> cells, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;anodization&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is the endothermic process of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>depositing</i> a metal layer</span> on the <i>surface</i> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">anode</span>."	"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electrolytic</u></span> cells, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>anodization</b></span> is the endothermic process of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>depositing</i> a metal layer</span> on the <i>surface</i> of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">anode</span>.

<div>- In order for a metal to be deposited on the anode (instead of the cathode), the direction of the current would have to be flowing from the cathode → anode (reversed).</div><div>- Endothermic because it's an electrolytic cell, so it requires an external source of energy to proceed.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d5df444aa46517ca0ac1b9b21adeb28ad4983092.jpg""><br></div></div>"

"In <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>electrolytic</u></span> cells, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>anodization</b></span> is the endothermic process of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>depositing</i> a metal layer</span> on the <i>surface</i> of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">anode</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- In order for a metal to be deposited on the anode (instead of the cathode), the direction of the current would have to be flowing from the cathode → anode (reversed).</div><div>- Endothermic because it's an electrolytic cell, so it requires an external source of energy to proceed.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d5df444aa46517ca0ac1b9b21adeb28ad4983092.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<b>DMSO</b> can be used as a <u>solvent</u> to dissolve <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;polar&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[polar/non-polar/amphi.]</span> molecules."	"<b>DMSO</b> can be used as a <u>solvent</u> to dissolve <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>polar</i></span> molecules.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

DMSO is polar &amp; aprotic.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-27c440fc6ed857b8e4b1310af20eb81bfbde041f.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dilution factor</b></span> by which a compound is diluted:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;ecdabf970ccda2f194d0df8bc91af1a58c6374bb&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>dilution factor</b></span> by which a compound is diluted:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""paste-ecdabf970ccda2f194d0df8bc91af1a58c6374bb.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>DF</b> = dilution factor; <b>V<sub>T1/2</sub></b> = volume transferred in step 1/2; <b>V<sub>F1/2</sub></b> = final volume after step 1/2.</div>"
"In a <b>lead-acid battery</b>, the <u>plates</u> when fully <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>charged</i></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Pb</span> (anode) &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">PbO<sub>2</sub></span> (cathode), with <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;SO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> in solution."	"In a <b>lead-acid battery</b>, the <u>plates</u> when fully <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>charged</i></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Pb</span> (anode) &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">PbO<sub>2</sub></span> (cathode), with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub></span> in solution.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub> </b>drives a reaction that causes both anode &amp; cathode to turn into PbSO<sub>4</sub> (H<sub>2</sub>O is a byproduct, explaining why the solution becomes dilute).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7b80d3ca2b902afab980b6c885a0023fd2cb8e45.jpg""><br></div>"
"<div>Steps to find the <b>net reaction</b>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Multiply&#x20;each&#x20;reactant&#x20;by&#x20;whatever&#x20;number&#x20;needed&#x20;to&#x20;&lt;u&gt;make&#x20;the&#x20;electrons&#x20;equal&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;5e&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;2e&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;→&#x20;10e&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Combine&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;reactants&#x20;into&#x20;one&#x20;large&#x20;equation&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Simplify&#x20;the&#x20;coefficients&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;by&#x20;dividing&#x20;by&#x20;a&#x20;common&#x20;denominator&#x20;&#x28;if&#x20;possible&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-438c86b8c66f7dd349a82207b78aaaf75788e892.jpg"">"	"<div>Steps to find the <b>net reaction</b>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Multiply each reactant by whatever number needed to <u>make the electrons equal</u> (5e<sup>-</sup> &amp; 2e<sup>-</sup>→ 10e<sup>-</sup>).</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Combine</u> the reactants into one large equation.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>Simplify the coefficients</u> by dividing by a common denominator (if possible).</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-438c86b8c66f7dd349a82207b78aaaf75788e892.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"Which ionic species <i>predominates</i> in solution, and why?<div><br></div><div><b>K<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub></b> <i>mixed</i> with <b>H<sub>2</sub>O</b> → <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""K&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;dominates&#x2C;&#x20;since&#x20;K&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;SO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;contributes&#x20;&lt;i&gt;twice&#x20;as&#x20;much&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;K&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;as&#x20;it&#x20;does&#x20;&lt;i&gt;anything&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;involving&#x20;SO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;2&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x2E;&#x20;For&#x20;every&#x20;molecule&#x20;of&#x20;SO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;2&#x2D;&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x28;or&#x20;anything&#x20;derived&#x20;from&#x20;SO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;2&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;&#x2C;&#x20;K&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;will&#x20;always&#x20;be&#x20;in&#x20;twice&#x20;as&#x20;high&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;concentration&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Which ionic species <i>predominates</i> in solution, and why?<div><br></div><div><b>K<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub></b> <i>mixed</i> with <b>H<sub>2</sub>O</b> → <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">K<sup>+</sup> dominates, since K<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub> contributes <i>twice as much</i> K<sup>+</sup> as it does <i>anything</i> involving SO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>. For every molecule of SO<sub>4</sub><sup>2- </sup>(or anything derived from SO<sub>4</sub><sup>2-</sup>), K<sup>+</sup> will always be in twice as high of a concentration.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"Real gases exert <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[lower/higher]</span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pressures</b></span> on the container walls<b>&nbsp;</b>than ideal gases due to their<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>slight&nbsp;</i>intermolecular forces</span>."	"Real gases exert <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>lower</i></span>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pressures</b></span> on the container walls<b>&nbsp;</b>than ideal gases due to their<i>&nbsp;</i><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>slight&nbsp;</i>intermolecular forces</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>Intermolecular forces</b> between real gases will cause them to be <u>attracted more</u> to each other and <u>collide less</u> with the wall.<div><br></div><div><img src=""68mG7d1JvjhhPv8sMLG6kD6vq49xhDMFtulIWJUqMn1SBXsMbtBu6AwSmYGF47SpPjooz6QPCyWuYTqcglplwRKpYQ.png""><br></div></div>"
"After titration, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">concentration of the <i>remaining</i> solution</span> is called the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;titer&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"After titration, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">concentration of the <i>remaining</i> solution</span> is called the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>titer</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"After titration, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""concentration&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;remaining&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;solution"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is called the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>titer</b></span>."	"After titration, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">concentration of the <i>remaining</i> solution</span> is called the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>titer</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>Salts</b> that contain <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""bromine&#x20;&#x28;Br&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[halogen]</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""iodine&#x20;&#x28;I&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[halogen]</span>, or <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""chlorine&#x20;&#x28;Cl&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[halogen]</span>&nbsp;are usually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>soluble</u></span>."	"<b>Salts</b> that contain <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">bromine (Br)</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">iodine (I)</span>, or <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">chlorine (Cl)</span>&nbsp;are usually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>soluble</u></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""43aa313b52e2cc0819a85338aa835c44.png""></div>"
"1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Dalton&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[unit]</span> is equivalent to 1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">g/mol</span> - they measure <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular weight</u> (MW)</span>."	"1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Dalton</b></span> is equivalent to 1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">g/mol</span> - they measure <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular weight</u> (MW)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Ex: 55,000 Da → MW = 55,000</div></div>"
"1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Dalton</b></span> is equivalent to 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""g&#x2F;mol"" data-ordinal=""2"">[unit]</span> - they measure <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;molecular&#x20;weight&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;MW&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"1 <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Dalton</b></span> is equivalent to 1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">g/mol</span> - they measure <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>molecular weight</u> (MW)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Ex: 55,000 Da → MW = 55,000</div></div>"
"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of an <i>added</i> acid/base</span> in a titration at its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equivalence point</u></span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Find&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;number&#x20;of&#x20;moles&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;original&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;acid&#x2F;base&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Number&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;original&#x20;solution&#x27;s&#x20;moles&#x20;is&#x20;&lt;i&gt;equal&#x20;to&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;the&#x20;number&#x20;of&#x20;titrant&#x27;s&#x20;moles&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of an <i>added</i> acid/base</span> in a titration at its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equivalence point</u></span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Find the <u>number of moles</u> of the <i>original</i> acid/base.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Number of the original solution's moles is <i>equal to </i>the number of titrant's moles.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The <b>equivalence point</b> is when the acid's concentration = the base's concentration. Therefore, they must be equal to each other at that point.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-147f6da7475622d265b5f77ac0baa0a62b4e1956.jpg""></div>"
"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""moles&#x20;of&#x20;an&#x20;&lt;i&gt;added&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;acid&#x2F;base"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in a titration at its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;equivalence&#x20;point&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Find the <u>number of moles</u> of the <i>original</i> acid/base.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Number of the original solution's moles is <i>equal to </i>the number of titrant's moles.</span></div>"	"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of an <i>added</i> acid/base</span> in a titration at its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equivalence point</u></span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Find the <u>number of moles</u> of the <i>original</i> acid/base.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Number of the original solution's moles is <i>equal to </i>the number of titrant's moles.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The <b>equivalence point</b> is when the acid's concentration = the base's concentration. Therefore, they must be equal to each other at that point.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-147f6da7475622d265b5f77ac0baa0a62b4e1956.jpg""></div>"
"<u>Van der Waal forces</u> can be exhibited by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;all&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[only some/all]</span> molecules."	"<u>Van der Waal forces</u> can be exhibited by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>all</i></span> molecules.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Includes London dispersion forces, which are instantaneous dipole moments between nearby atoms.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f5a5787bba6fed8f15843ce088f1384041b72b1.jpg""></div>"
"Ligands capable of forming <u>coordinate covalent bonds</u> in complex ions <i>must</i> have a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""lone&#x20;pair&#x20;of&#x20;electrons"" data-ordinal=""1"">[group]</span>."	"Ligands capable of forming <u>coordinate covalent bonds</u> in complex ions <i>must</i> have a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">lone pair of electrons</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Non-bonding electron pair allows them to donate electrons to the central cation.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-76dd1e0d3df95346edb76d8ae71d363890934170.jpg""></div>"

"Adding a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i> acid</span>&nbsp;to a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;carbonate&#x20;salt&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;Na&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;CO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;forms <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, and a salt." "Adding a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i> acid</span>&nbsp;to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carbonate salt</u> (Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>)</span>&nbsp;forms <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""CO&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, and a salt."	"Adding a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i> acid</span>&nbsp;to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carbonate salt</u> (Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>)</span>&nbsp;forms <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub></span>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, and a salt.

"Adding a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;acid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carbonate salt</u> (Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>)</span>&nbsp;forms <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, and a salt."	"Adding a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i> acid</span>&nbsp;to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>carbonate salt</u> (Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>)</span>&nbsp;forms <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">CO<sub>2</sub></span>, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">H<sub>2</sub>O</span>, and a salt.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> + HCl → CO<sub>2</sub> + H<sub>2</sub>O + NaCl</div>"
"<div>The compound on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>left</i></span> is more acidic, since its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">cis</span> orientation allows for better <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;hydrogen&#x20;bond&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;stabilization"" data-ordinal=""2"">[stabilization]</span> between OH &amp; COO<sup>-</sup>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f90c0ab23bd6f71be04ee6c43c44b548240d3383.jpg"">"	"<div>The compound on the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>left</i></span> is more acidic, since its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">cis</span> orientation allows for better <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>hydrogen bond</u> stabilization</span> between OH &amp; COO<sup>-</sup>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f90c0ab23bd6f71be04ee6c43c44b548240d3383.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Since they're both cis, they're better able to align their functional groups for bonding.</div>"
"<div>The compound on the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;left&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[left/right]</span> is more acidic, since its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cis"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cis/trans]</span> orientation allows for better <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>hydrogen bond</u> stabilization</span> between OH &amp; COO<sup>-</sup>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f90c0ab23bd6f71be04ee6c43c44b548240d3383.jpg"">"	"<div>The compound on the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>left</i></span> is more acidic, since its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">cis</span> orientation allows for better <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>hydrogen bond</u> stabilization</span> between OH &amp; COO<sup>-</sup>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-f90c0ab23bd6f71be04ee6c43c44b548240d3383.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Since they're both cis, they're better able to align their functional groups for bonding.</div>"
"Describe the <u>oxidation states</u> of the H and O atoms in <b>H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub></b>, and explain why.<div><br></div><div>Each H atom: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x2B;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>Each O atom: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x2D;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""This&#x20;is&#x20;because&#x20;oxygen&#x20;atoms&#x20;have&#x20;&#x2D;1&#x20;oxidation&#x20;states&#x20;in&#x20;peroxides&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reason]</span></div>"	"Describe the <u>oxidation states</u> of the H and O atoms in <b>H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub></b>, and explain why.<div><br></div><div>Each H atom: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">+1</span></div><div>Each O atom: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">-1</span></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">This is because oxygen atoms have -1 oxidation states in peroxides.</span></div>

</div>"
"When writing the <b>electron configuration</b> of a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>anion</u></span>, <i>add </i>electrons to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""shell&#x20;with&#x20;the&#x20;unfilled&#x20;orbital&#x20;&#x28;usually&#x20;on&#x20;the&#x20;end&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"When writing the <b>electron configuration</b> of a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>anion</u></span>, <i>add </i>electrons to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">shell with the unfilled orbital (usually on the end)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- An exception would be if it was more stable half-filling a previous subshell.</div><div>- If you have a -1 anion, add the electron to the subshell with the unfilled orbital (usually the last one).</div>-&nbsp;<a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uYftghZLEo"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9uYftghZLEo</a><div><br></div><div><img src=""692aa4520bc24af3aa5a1eeeb0c1116c.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Weak&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[strong/weak]</span> <b>acids</b> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> <u>conductors</u> of electricity."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Weak</b></span> <b>acids</b> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> <u>conductors</u> of electricity.

</div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Weak</b></span> <b>acids</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> <u>conductors</u> of electricity.

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Strong&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[strong/weak]</span> <b>acids</b> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>conductors</u> of electricity."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Strong</b></span> <b>acids</b> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>conductors</u> of electricity.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Strong</b></span> <b>acids</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weak/strong]</span> <u>conductors</u> of electricity."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Strong</b></span> <b>acids</b> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>conductors</u> of electricity.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>Strong acids</b> <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;dissociate&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[dissolve/dissociate]</span> 100% in solution, but they <i>may or may not </i><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""dissolve"" data-ordinal=""1"">[dissolve/dissociate]</span> 100% in solution."	"<b>Strong acids</b> <i>always</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>dissociate</u></span> 100% in solution, but they <i>may or may not </i><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">dissolve</span> 100% in solution.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>Dissociation</b>: ionic compounds splitting into smaller charged ions (which happens when they donate H<sup>+</sup> protons).<div>- <b>Dissolving</b>: compounds that get split apart by the solvent.</div></div>"
"A compound that <u>dissolves 100%</u> in solution but acts as a <u><i>weak</i> conductor of electricity</u> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;&lt;b&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> <b>acid</b>."	"A compound that <u>dissolves 100%</u> in solution but acts as a <u><i>weak</i> conductor of electricity</u> is a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i><b>weak</b></i></span> <b>acid</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Compounds can dissolve 100% <u>without dissociating 100%</u>... do not mix these up.</div><div><br></div>- <b>Dissociation</b>: ionic compounds splitting into smaller charged ions (which happens when they donate H<sup>+</sup> protons).<div>- <b>Dissolving</b>: compounds that get split apart by the solvent.</div></div>"
"<div>In fluid <b>barometers</b>, the liquid <i>inside</i> the tube rises until the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""total&#x20;&lt;u&gt;pressure&#x20;inside&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;liquid&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x2B;&#x20;P&lt;sub&gt;empty&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;sub&gt;air&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>equal </i>to</span> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""atmospheric&#x20;pressure&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;atm&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<i>outside</i> the tube.<br></div>"	"<div>In fluid <b>barometers</b>, the liquid <i>inside</i> the tube rises until the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">total <u>pressure inside</u> (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty</sub>&nbsp;<sub>air</sub>)</span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>equal </i>to</span> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">atmospheric pressure (P<sub>atm</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>outside</i> the tube.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- P<sub>atm</sub> is higher outside than the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid rises.</div><div>- P<sub>atm</sub>&nbsp;is lower outside than the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub>&nbsp;+ P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid drops in height.</div><div>- P<sub>atm</sub>&nbsp;is equal to the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub>&nbsp;+ P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid reaches equilibrium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a4bda460f722020c41fa96ea884c6c48dac98e15.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>In fluid <b>barometers</b>, the liquid <i>inside</i> the tube rises until the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">total <u>pressure inside</u> (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty</sub>&nbsp;<sub>air</sub>)</span> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;equal&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;to"" data-ordinal=""3"">[greater/less/equal to]</span> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">atmospheric pressure (P<sub>atm</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>outside</i> the tube.<br></div>"	"<div>In fluid <b>barometers</b>, the liquid <i>inside</i> the tube rises until the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">total <u>pressure inside</u> (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty</sub>&nbsp;<sub>air</sub>)</span> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>equal </i>to</span> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">atmospheric pressure (P<sub>atm</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>outside</i> the tube.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- P<sub>atm</sub> is higher outside than the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid rises.</div><div>- P<sub>atm</sub>&nbsp;is lower outside than the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub>&nbsp;+ P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid drops in height.</div><div>- P<sub>atm</sub>&nbsp;is equal to the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub>&nbsp;+ P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid reaches equilibrium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a4bda460f722020c41fa96ea884c6c48dac98e15.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"An atom's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>nucleon number</b></span> is <i>another name</i>&nbsp;for its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mass&#x20;number&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - the sum of its protons and neutrons."	"An atom's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>nucleon number</b></span> is <i>another name</i>&nbsp;for its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>mass number</u></span> - the sum of its protons and neutrons.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Heterogenous mixtures</b></span> are solutions where the <u>components</u> that make up the mixture are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""distributed&#x20;&lt;i&gt;unevenly&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Heterogenous mixtures</b></span> are solutions where the <u>components</u> that make up the mixture are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">distributed <i>unevenly</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>""Solutions"" are strictly considered homogenous mixtures.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9dde48ea99242a0b87cc1ec7602c3a96a72dfbf7.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Solutions</b> are <i>always</i> considered <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;homo&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><u>genous mixtures</u> - the mixture's components are <i>always</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""distributed&#x20;evenly"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Solutions</b> are <i>always</i> considered <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>homo</u></span><u>genous mixtures</u> - the mixture's components are <i>always</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">distributed evenly</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Homogenous mixtures: components of the mixture are distributed unevenly.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9dde48ea99242a0b87cc1ec7602c3a96a72dfbf7.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Rules for an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ideal-dilute solution</b></span> to be achieved:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;Solute&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;molecules&#x20;&lt;i&gt;never&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;interact&#x20;with&#x20;each&#x20;other&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Mole&#x20;fraction&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;solvent&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;molecules&#x20;comprises&#x20;close&#x20;to&#x20;100&#x25;&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;mixture&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Rules for an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ideal-dilute solution</b></span> to be achieved:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Solute</u> molecules <i>never</i> interact with each other.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Mole fraction of the <u>solvent</u> molecules comprises close to 100% of the mixture.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>These characteristics allow for a ""totally dilute solution.""</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-679e7492b758301a0d045c98f977ec1ddb49bf1f.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Rules for an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ideal&#x2D;dilute&#x20;solution&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> to be achieved:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Solute</u> molecules <i>never</i> interact with each other.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Mole fraction of the <u>solvent</u> molecules comprises close to 100% of the mixture.</span></div>"	"<div>Rules for an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ideal-dilute solution</b></span> to be achieved:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>Solute</u> molecules <i>never</i> interact with each other.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Mole fraction of the <u>solvent</u> molecules comprises close to 100% of the mixture.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>These characteristics allow for a ""totally dilute solution.""</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-679e7492b758301a0d045c98f977ec1ddb49bf1f.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Non&#x2D;polar"" data-ordinal=""1"">[polar/non-polar]</span> compounds <i>are not</i> <u>soluble</u> in polar solvents (like water) because the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">interactions between water molecules</span> are ultimately <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">more favorable</span> - <i>hindering</i> solvation."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Non-polar</span> compounds <i>are not</i> <u>soluble</u> in polar solvents (like water) because the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">interactions between water molecules</span> are ultimately <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">more favorable</span> - <i>hindering</i> solvation.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Water would rather stay bonded to other water molecules than bonding to the non-polar compound.</div><div><br></div><img src=""899de896a18cb42e581967914b65229c.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Non-polar</span> compounds <i>are not</i> <u>soluble</u> in polar solvents (like water) because the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""interactions&#x20;between&#x20;water&#x20;molecules"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> are ultimately <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""more&#x20;favorable"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - <i>hindering</i> solvation."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Non-polar</span> compounds <i>are not</i> <u>soluble</u> in polar solvents (like water) because the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">interactions between water molecules</span> are ultimately <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">more favorable</span> - <i>hindering</i> solvation.

</div>"
"Irreversible reactions <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;do&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[do/do not]</span> have <b><i>reverse</i> rate constants (k<sub>-1</sub>)</b>."	"Irreversible reactions <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> have <b><i>reverse</i> rate constants (k<sub>-1</sub>)</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>K<sub>eq</sub></b>&nbsp;is calculated as k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub>, or k<sub>1</sub>/k<sub>-1</sub>. Any reaction with a K<sub>eq</sub> value is <u>reversible</u>.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Acid&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x2F;&lt;b&gt;base&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[acidbase/nucophilicelectrophilic]</span>&nbsp;reactions favor the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>thermodynamic</u></span> products of a reaction - whichever product <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">is the <i>most stable</i></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Acid</b>/<b>base</b></span>&nbsp;reactions favor the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>thermodynamic</u></span> products of a reaction - whichever product <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">is the <i>most stable</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Nucleophilic/electrophilic</b>&nbsp;reactions → favor&nbsp;<u>kinetic</u>&nbsp;products.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Acid/base</b>&nbsp;reactions → favor&nbsp;<u>thermodynamic</u>&nbsp;products.</div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Acid</b>/<b>base</b></span>&nbsp;reactions favor the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;thermodynamic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[thermodynamic/kinetic]</span> products of a reaction - whichever product <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""is&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;most&#x20;stable&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>Acid</b>/<b>base</b></span>&nbsp;reactions favor the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>thermodynamic</u></span> products of a reaction - whichever product <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">is the <i>most stable</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>Nucleophilic/electrophilic</b>&nbsp;reactions → favor&nbsp;<u>kinetic</u>&nbsp;products.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Acid/base</b>&nbsp;reactions → favor&nbsp;<u>thermodynamic</u>&nbsp;products.</div></div>"
"In order for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Raoult</b></span><b>'s law</b> to remain true, the solution must be an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;ideal&#x20;solution&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - the solute-solvent bond strengths must be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;similar&#x20;to&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[different from/similar to]</span> the solute-solute bond strengths."	"In order for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Raoult</b></span><b>'s law</b> to remain true, the solution must be an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ideal solution</u></span> - the solute-solvent bond strengths must be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>similar to</i></span> the solute-solute bond strengths.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Ideal solution</b>: the bond strengths between the solvent and solute after dissolving must be roughly equal to the strength of the bonds between the solute molecules prior to dissolution.</div><div>- <b>Raoult's law</b> describes the <u>vapor pressure depression</u> due to the presence of impurities (solute).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"
"In order for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Raoult&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><b>'s law</b> to remain true, the solution must be an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ideal solution</u></span> - the solute-solvent bond strengths must be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>similar to</i></span> the solute-solute bond strengths."	"In order for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Raoult</b></span><b>'s law</b> to remain true, the solution must be an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>ideal solution</u></span> - the solute-solvent bond strengths must be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>similar to</i></span> the solute-solute bond strengths.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Ideal solution</b>: the bond strengths between the solvent and solute after dissolving must be roughly equal to the strength of the bonds between the solute molecules prior to dissolution.</div><div>- <b>Raoult's law</b> describes the <u>vapor pressure depression</u> due to the presence of impurities (solute).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"
"When the concentration of [H<sup>+</sup>] is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>smaller</i></span> than 1 x <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">10<sup>-7</sup></span>, we <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[can still/cannot]</span> use the <b>1 x 10<sup>-pH</sup></b> <b>trick</b>&nbsp;- you can predict that it will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;slightly&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;under&#x20;7"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"When the concentration of [H<sup>+</sup>] is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>smaller</i></span> than 1 x <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">10<sup>-7</sup></span>, we <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> use the <b>1 x 10<sup>-pH</sup></b> <b>trick</b>&nbsp;- you can predict that it will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>slightly</i> under 7</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Ex: 1 x 10<sup>-8</sup>&nbsp;HCl would make you think the pH is 8 (using the trick), but this is incorrect, since the presence of HCl (strong acid) should still make it more acidic.</div>"
"When the concentration of [H<sup>+</sup>] is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;smaller&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[smaller/larger]</span> than 1 x <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""10&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;7&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, we <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>cannot</i></span> use the <b>1 x 10<sup>-pH</sup></b> <b>trick</b>&nbsp;- you can predict that it will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>slightly</i> under 7</span>." Ex: 1 x 10<sup>-8</sup>&nbsp;HCl would make you think the pH is 8 (using the trick), but this is incorrect, since the presence of HCl (strong acid) should still make it more acidic.</div>"
"Equation that describes the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;temperature&#x20;at&#x20;&lt;i&gt;equilibrium&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""two&#x20;liquids&#x20;of&#x20;different&#x20;temperatures"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> are <i>mixed</i>:<div><br></div><div>Assuming liquid<sub>2</sub> is <i>hotter</i> than liquid<sub>1</sub>...<br><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q<sub>liquid2</sub> = q<sub>liquid1</sub></b></span><sub>&nbsp;</sub>...</div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">m<sub>liquid1</sub>c(ΔT<sub>liquid1</sub>) = m<sub>liquid2</sub>c(ΔT<sub>liquid2</sub>)</span></div></div>"	"Equation that describes the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>temperature at <i>equilibrium</i></u></span> when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">two liquids of different temperatures</span> are <i>mixed</i>:<div><br></div><div>Assuming liquid<sub>2</sub> is <i>hotter</i> than liquid<sub>1</sub>...<br><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q<sub>liquid2</sub> = q<sub>liquid1</sub></b></span><sub>&nbsp;</sub>...</div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">m<sub>liquid1</sub>c(ΔT<sub>liquid1</sub>) = m<sub>liquid2</sub>c(ΔT<sub>liquid2</sub>)</span></div></div>

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>liquid<sub>2</sub> is shown to have a <u>negative</u> temperature because it loses heat to liquid<sub>1</sub>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""></div>"
"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;stronger&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weaker/stronger/equal to]</span> than solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher&nbsp;</i>than</span> what the law predicts."	"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>stronger</i></span> than solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher&nbsp;</i>than</span> what the law predicts.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because the solute and solvent molecules will <u>prefer to not interact with each other at all</u>. Thus, the solute doesn't interfere with the solvent's ability to evaporate.</div><div>- Remember, Raoult's law requires an <b>ideal solution</b> to be accurate (where solute-solute/solvent-solvent interactions are <u>equal in strength</u> to solute-solvent interactions).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"
"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>stronger</i></span> than solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;than"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher/accurate]</span> what the law predicts."	"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>stronger</i></span> than solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher&nbsp;</i>than</span> what the law predicts.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because the solute and solvent molecules will <u>prefer to not interact with each other at all</u>. Thus, the solute doesn't interfere with the solvent's ability to evaporate.</div><div>- Remember, Raoult's law requires an <b>ideal solution</b> to be accurate (where solute-solute/solvent-solvent interactions are <u>equal in strength</u> to solute-solvent interactions).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"
"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>weaker</i></span> than solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lower&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;than"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher/accurate]</span> what the law predicts."	"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>weaker</i></span> than solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower&nbsp;</i>than</span> what the law predicts.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because the solute and solvent molecules will <u>prefer to interact far too much</u>. Thus, the solute interferes significantly with the solvent's ability to vaporize.</div><div>- Remember, Raoult's law requires an <b>ideal solution</b> to be accurate (where solute-solute/solvent-solvent interactions are <u>equal in strength</u> to solute-solvent interactions).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"

"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>weaker</i></span> than solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower&nbsp;</i>than</span> what the law predicts.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because the solute and solvent molecules will <u>prefer to interact far too much</u>. Thus, the solute interferes significantly with the solvent's ability to vaporize.</div><div>- Remember, Raoult's law requires an <b>ideal solution</b> to be accurate (where solute-solute/solvent-solvent interactions are <u>equal in strength</u> to solute-solvent interactions).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"

"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal to</i></span> solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>accurate </i>to</span> what the law predicts.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This <u>describes an ideal solution</u>, which is what Raoult's law depends on.</div><div>- Remember, Raoult's law requires an <b>ideal solution</b> to be accurate (where solute-solute/solvent-solvent interactions are <u>equal in strength</u> to solute-solvent interactions).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"

"In&nbsp;<b>Raoult's law</b>&nbsp;where solute-solute and solvent-solvent interactions are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal to</i></span> solute-solvent interactions, <u>vapor pressure</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>accurate </i>to</span> what the law predicts.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This <u>describes an ideal solution</u>, which is what Raoult's law depends on.</div><div>- Remember, Raoult's law requires an <b>ideal solution</b> to be accurate (where solute-solute/solvent-solvent interactions are <u>equal in strength</u> to solute-solvent interactions).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div></div>"
"As the <u>temperature</u> of an electrochemical cell <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>emf</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">non-linear</span> fashion."	"As the <u>temperature</u> of an electrochemical cell <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>emf</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">non-linear</span> fashion.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Directly proportional, but they don't follow a linear relationship.<div><br></div><div><img src=""00650e624ba59e7b3fc19ef931da5287.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d841fae27a7f49226b0b8e7bd834a91926f0fa12.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"As the <u>temperature</u> of an electrochemical cell <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>emf</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> in a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""non&#x2D;linear"" data-ordinal=""3"">[linear/non-linear]</span> fashion."	"As the <u>temperature</u> of an electrochemical cell <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>emf</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> in a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">non-linear</span> fashion.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Directly proportional, but they don't follow a linear relationship.<div><br></div><div><img src=""00650e624ba59e7b3fc19ef931da5287.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d841fae27a7f49226b0b8e7bd834a91926f0fa12.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"As the <u>temperature</u> of an electrochemical cell <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases]</span>, the <b>emf</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">non-linear</span> fashion."	"As the <u>temperature</u> of an electrochemical cell <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span>, the <b>emf</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increases</i></span> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">non-linear</span> fashion.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Directly proportional, but they don't follow a linear relationship.<div><br></div><div><img src=""00650e624ba59e7b3fc19ef931da5287.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d841fae27a7f49226b0b8e7bd834a91926f0fa12.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"A solid has reached its <b>melting point</b> when the mixture is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""50&#x25;&#x20;solid&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;50&#x25;&#x20;liquid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, where the temperature <i>does not</i> change until the mixture is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""100&#x25;&#x20;liquid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"A solid has reached its <b>melting point</b> when the mixture is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">50% solid &amp; 50% liquid</span>, where the temperature <i>does not</i> change until the mixture is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">100% liquid</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The amount of heat that gets added to transition phases (solid → liquid) without changing temperature is the&nbsp;<b>latent heat (L)</b>.</div><div>- Regions caught between phase transitions (freezing vs. melting point)&nbsp;<u>do not</u>&nbsp;increase in temperature.</div><div>- Once the entire sample transitions from being 50/50 liquid/ice → 100/0 liquid, temperature increases again.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""></div>"
"<div>The <i>product</i> of <b>N<sub>a</sub>V<sub>a</sub></b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""number&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;moles&#x20;of&#x20;acid&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> at the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""equivalence&#x20;point"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in a titration.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg"">"	"<div>The <i>product</i> of <b>N<sub>a</sub>V<sub>a</sub></b> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">number of <u>moles of acid</u></span> at the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">equivalence point</span> in a titration.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>N<sub>a/b</sub></b>&nbsp;= normality (Molarity x equivalences);&nbsp;<b>V</b>&nbsp;= volume (either added or started with).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Normality</b>&nbsp;is used in place of molarity to account for the number of equivalences it has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>The <i>product</i> of <b>N<sub>b</sub>V<sub>b</sub></b>&nbsp;in the below equation represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""number&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;moles&#x20;of&#x20;base&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> at the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""equivalence&#x20;point"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> in a titration.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg"">"	"<div>The <i>product</i> of <b>N<sub>b</sub>V<sub>b</sub></b>&nbsp;in the below equation represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">number of <u>moles of base</u></span> at the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">equivalence point</span> in a titration.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>N<sub>a/b</sub></b>&nbsp;= normality (Molarity x equivalences);&nbsp;<b>V</b>&nbsp;= volume (either added or started with).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Normality</b>&nbsp;is used in place of molarity to account for the number of equivalences it has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg""></div></div>"
"<div>Explain what this equation is used for.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""N&lt;sub&gt;a&#x20;or&#x20;b&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;V&lt;sub&gt;a&#x20;or&#x20;b&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;represents&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;moles&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;acid&#x20;or&#x20;base&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;at&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;equivalence&#x20;point&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;in&#x20;a&#x20;titration&#x2E;&#x20;This&#x20;describes&#x20;the&#x20;equivalence&#x20;point&#x2C;&#x20;since&#x20;the&#x20;equivalence&#x20;point&#x20;is&#x20;the&#x20;only&#x20;position&#x20;in&#x20;a&#x20;titration&#x20;when&#x20;the&#x20;moles&#x20;of&#x20;acid&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;moles&#x20;of&#x20;base&#x2E;&#x20;Normality&#x20;&#x28;N&#x29;&#x20;is&#x20;used&#x20;in&#x20;place&#x20;of&#x20;molarity&#x20;to&#x20;take&#x20;the&#x20;number&#x20;of&#x20;equivalences&#x20;into&#x20;account&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg"">"	"<div>Explain what this equation is used for.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">N<sub>a or b</sub>V<sub>a or b</sub> represents the <u><i>moles</i> of acid or base</u> at the <u>equivalence point</u> in a titration. This describes the equivalence point, since the equivalence point is the only position in a titration when the moles of acid = moles of base. Normality (N) is used in place of molarity to take the number of equivalences into account.</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-ff5adfdcf7e250f83b29101499a3e16b2dc1d339.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>N<sub>a/b</sub></b>&nbsp;= normality (Molarity x equivalences);&nbsp;<b>V</b>&nbsp;= volume (either added or started with).</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Normality</b>&nbsp;is used in place of molarity to account for the number of equivalences it has.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-8854c9235ab98264744765beb223298309ebc9f8.jpg""></div></div>"
"<b>N<sub>2</sub></b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> act as a <u>nucleophile</u> - it's an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""unreactive&#x2C;&#x20;inert&#x20;species"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>N<sub>2</sub></b>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> act as a <u>nucleophile</u> - it's an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">unreactive, inert species</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Despite the lone pairs, its triple bond makes it very stable (unreactive).</div><div>- Recognize the fact that N<sub>2</sub> makes up a significant portion of our <u>atmosphere</u>. If it was a good nucleophile, it'd be highly reactive with other elements in the atmosphere.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0b74f511b8cee2c5db2c9a4adb5001397d9cef71.jpg""></div>"
"<div>The reaction taking place on β-carotene is a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;oxidation&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;reaction"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reaction]</span>, because it's progressively <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;gaining&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;bonds&#x20;to&#x20;oxygen"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-bc3a4916187321c55c5422d19de56e14ca5e5bc1.jpg"">"	"<div>The reaction taking place on β-carotene is a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>oxidation</u> reaction</span>, because it's progressively <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>gaining</i> bonds to oxygen</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-bc3a4916187321c55c5422d19de56e14ca5e5bc1.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The pK<sub>a</sub> of the <u>indicator</u> used is ±1 unit from the endpoint's pH.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-a91dc8b3cbdf8b7e6bbef5a89fbbbc4d769eebc9.jpg""></div>"
"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;percent&#x20;dissociation&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;acid"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> when <i>given</i> the resulting pH:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Calculate the [H<sup>+</sup>] using the pH.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Calculate the <i>initial</i> concentration of the weak acid ([HA]) in solution.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Divide [H<sup>+</sup>]/[HA].</span></div>"	"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>percent dissociation</u></span> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i> acid</span> when <i>given</i> the resulting pH:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Calculate the [H<sup>+</sup>] using the pH.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Calculate the <i>initial</i> concentration of the weak acid ([HA]) in solution.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Divide [H<sup>+</sup>]/[HA].</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>percent dissociation</u></span> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i> acid</span> when <i>given</i> the resulting pH:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Calculate&#x20;the&#x20;&#x5B;H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x5D;&#x20;using&#x20;the&#x20;pH&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Calculate&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;initial&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;concentration&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;weak&#x20;acid&#x20;&#x28;&#x5B;HA&#x5D;&#x29;&#x20;in&#x20;solution&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Divide&#x20;&#x5B;H&lt;sup&gt;&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x5D;&#x2F;&#x5B;HA&#x5D;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>percent dissociation</u></span> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i> acid</span> when <i>given</i> the resulting pH:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Calculate the [H<sup>+</sup>] using the pH.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Calculate the <i>initial</i> concentration of the weak acid ([HA]) in solution.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Divide [H<sup>+</sup>]/[HA].</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"The <b>pH scale</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> go below 0 - typically seen for strong acids with a <u>molarity</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greater</i> than 1 M</span>."	"The <b>pH scale</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> go below 0 - typically seen for strong acids with a <u>molarity</u> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>greater</i> than 1 M</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 M of HCl will have a pH of 0 (1 x 10<sup>0</sup> → pH = 0).</div><div>- 10 M of HCl will have a pH of -1 (1 x 10<sup>1</sup> → [H<sup>+</sup>] = 1 → pH = -log(1) → pH = -1.</div><div><br></div><img src=""c9e024802013c9c367d8d200872f8a2e.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""1ca64da6c151f69ee2b158f7a3d5c8b7.png""></div></div>"

"Vaporizing a liquid requires <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <u>energy</u> than the energy needed to melt a solid, because liquids have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;stronger&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weaker/stronger]</span> intermolecular forces (like hydrogen bonding)."
