<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

It's easier to melt ice than it is to boil water.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"According to <b>Gay-Lussac's law</b>, a <u>temperature</u> increase from 150K to 250K will increase <u>pressure</u> by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""a&#x20;&lt;i&gt;factor&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;1&#x2E;67&#x20;&#x28;250&#x2F;150&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"According to <b>Gay-Lussac's law</b>, a <u>temperature</u> increase from 150K to 250K will increase <u>pressure</u> by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">a <i>factor</i> of 1.67 (250/150)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>None of these gas laws are actual&nbsp;equations</u>! Rather, they're proportionalities between the two variables. Therefore, they can only be described by factor increases or decreases.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""1420389031.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""f648bdd1826b47f37f5e586e8b48555f.png""></div></div>"
"Chemical <b>bonds</b> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <u>potential energies</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more]</span> stable bonds."	"Chemical <b>bonds</b> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <u>potential energies</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> stable bonds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Energy &amp; bond stability are <u>inversely related</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0f5b074a49b6a5227523c95f2da339a66277ec9f.jpg""></div>"
"Chemical <b>bonds</b> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> <u>potential energies</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more]</span> stable bonds."	"Chemical <b>bonds</b> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> <u>potential energies</u> are <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> stable bonds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Energy &amp; bond stability are <u>inversely related</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0f5b074a49b6a5227523c95f2da339a66277ec9f.jpg""></div>"
"<div>During phase changes, when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;latent&#x20;heat&#x20;&#x28;L&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is added <i>without</i> resulting in a change to temperature, that heat is <i>instead</i> being used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">break intermolecular forces</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>During phase changes, when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>latent heat (L)</b></span> is added <i>without</i> resulting in a change to temperature, that heat is <i>instead</i> being used to <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">break intermolecular forces</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This breaking of the intermolecular forces is what allows a substance to transition from one phase to the next.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""></div><div><br></div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""></div>"
"<div>During phase changes, when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>latent heat (L)</b></span> is added <i>without</i> resulting in a change to temperature, that heat is <i>instead</i> being used to <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""break&#x20;intermolecular&#x20;forces"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>During phase changes, when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>latent heat (L)</b></span> is added <i>without</i> resulting in a change to temperature, that heat is <i>instead</i> being used to <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">break intermolecular forces</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This breaking of the intermolecular forces is what allows a substance to transition from one phase to the next.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""></div><div><br></div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""></div>"
"<div>Two different gases in the same mixture (at the <i>same</i> temperature) have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;kinetic&#x20;energies&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[momentums/KEs]</span> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;momentums&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[momentums/KEs]</span>.</div>"	"<div>Two different gases in the same mixture (at the <i>same</i> temperature) have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energies</u></span> but <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>momentums</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>KE</b> is equal since temperature describes the average kinetic energy of the mixture, and the gas mixture has an even temperature.<div>- <b>Momentum (p)</b> is different because, in order to have the same KE, they must differ in their velocities (since they have different molecular weights). Since momentum is p = mv, they must have different momentums when their mass and velocity differ.<br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d6ef7d08c49204ea905ce445ec46df004a4bf8da.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"<div>Two different gases in the same mixture (at the <i>same</i> temperature) have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;equal&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[different/equal]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energies</u></span> but <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[different/equal]</span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>momentums</u></span>.</div>"	"<div>Two different gases in the same mixture (at the <i>same</i> temperature) have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energies</u></span> but <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>momentums</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <b>KE</b> is equal since temperature describes the average kinetic energy of the mixture, and the gas mixture has an even temperature.<div>- <b>Momentum (p)</b> is different because, in order to have the same KE, they must differ in their velocities (since they have different molecular weights). Since momentum is p = mv, they must have different momentums when their mass and velocity differ.<br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-d6ef7d08c49204ea905ce445ec46df004a4bf8da.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"Two different gases in the same mixture (at the&nbsp;<i>same</i>&nbsp;temperature) have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;momentums&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[momentums/KEs]</span> but roughly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;kinetic&#x20;energies&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[momentums/KEs]</span>."	"Two different gases in the same mixture (at the&nbsp;<i>same</i>&nbsp;temperature) have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>momentums</u></span> but roughly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energies</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>KE</b>&nbsp;is equal since temperature describes the average kinetic energy of the mixture, and the gas mixture has an even temperature.<div>-&nbsp;<b>Momentum (p)</b>&nbsp;is different because, in order to have the same KE, they must differ in their velocities (since they have different molecular weights). "Two different gases in the same mixture (at the&nbsp;<i>same</i>&nbsp;temperature) have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>different</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>momentums</u></span> but roughly <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energies</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>KE</b>&nbsp;is equal since temperature describes the average kinetic energy of the mixture, and the gas mixture has an even temperature.<div>-&nbsp;<b>Momentum (p)</b>&nbsp;is different because, in order to have the same KE, they must differ in their velocities (since they have different molecular weights). "<div>Different gases in the same mixture at the same temperature <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> differ in their <b>momentums (p)</b>, since they'll both differ in <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;mass&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;velocity&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[property]</span>.</div>"	"<div>Different gases in the same mixture at the same temperature <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> differ in their <b>momentums (p)</b>, since they'll both differ in <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>mass</u></span> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>velocity</u></span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>KE</b>&nbsp;is equal since temperature describes the average kinetic energy of the mixture, and the gas mixture has an even temperature.<div>-&nbsp;<b>Momentum (p)</b>&nbsp;is different because, in order to have the same KE, they must differ in their velocities (since they have different molecular weights). "At a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u></span>, the most favorable&nbsp;<b>phase</b> is whichever phase the substance is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;most&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;dense"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"At a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>pressure</u></span>, the most favorable&nbsp;<b>phase</b> is whichever phase the substance is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>most</i> dense</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Water → liquid phase is favored at a high pressure because the liquid phase is the most dense (hence why the solid-liquid line slants towards the left on the phase diagram).</div><div>- Carbon → solid phase is favored at a high pressure since the liquid phase is the least dense (hence why the solid-liquid line slants towards the right on the phase diagram).</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>At a high pressure, the phase that is the most favorable is the phase with the greatest density.</div><div><br></div><div>- Water → liquid phase is favored at a high pressure because the liquid phase is the most dense (hence why the solid-liquid line slants towards the left on the phase diagram).</div><div>- Carbon → solid phase is favored at a high pressure since the liquid phase is the least dense (hence why the solid-liquid line slants towards the right on the phase diagram).</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>At a high pressure, the phase that is the most favorable is the phase with the greatest density.</div><div><br></div><div>- Water → liquid phase is favored at a high pressure because the liquid phase is the most dense (hence why the solid-liquid line slants towards the left on the phase diagram).</div><div>- Carbon → solid phase is favored at a high pressure since the liquid phase is the least dense (hence why the solid-liquid line slants towards the right on the phase diagram).</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"

"When a gas <i>expands</i>, its <u>temperature</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increases/decreases]</span> due to a decrease in pressure." "<div>Which compound will be a <u>solid</u> and which will be a <u>liquid</u> at room temperature?</div><div><br></div><div>Top: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Liquid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[solid/liquid]</span> because it has <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""stronger&#x20;&lt;i&gt;intra&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;molecular&#x20;forces&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>Bottom: <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Solid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[solid/liquid]</span> because it has <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""stronger&#x20;&lt;i&gt;inter&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;molecular&#x20;forces&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d0f4c7d63297c1d90c2edca03b89112754edfafe.jpg"">"	"<div>Which compound will be a <u>solid</u> and which will be a <u>liquid</u> at room temperature?</div><div><br></div><div>Top: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Liquid</span> because it has <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">stronger <i>intra</i>molecular forces.</span></div><div>Bottom: <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Solid</span> because it has <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">stronger <i>inter</i>molecular forces.</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d0f4c7d63297c1d90c2edca03b89112754edfafe.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Solids have stronger intermolecular forces than liquids.</div><div>- The bottom compound's OH groups are far enough away to ensure that <i>inter</i>molecular interactions are more likely to occur than <i>intra</i>molecular interactions.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-cef09653d8dba02754e68e5fd4602b481d5de946.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"The force from <b>osmotic pressure</b> points in the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;opposite&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[same/opposite]</span> <u>direction</u> to the direction of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""osmosis"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The force from <b>osmotic pressure</b> points in the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>opposite</i></span> <u>direction</u> to the direction of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">osmosis</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Osmosis</b>: flow of water.</div><div>- <b>Osmotic pressure</b>: force that resists the influx of water.</div><div><br></div><img src=""imageedit_4_7763787191.jpg""></div>"
"Equation to calculate the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""moles&#x20;of&#x20;an&#x20;ion&#x20;&lt;i&gt;reduced&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;a&#x20;solid"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> when you're <i>given</i> the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""moles&#x20;of&#x20;electrons"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Moles<sub>reduced ion</sub> = (moles<sub>electrons <i>given</i></sub>) / (moles<sub>electrons you <i>would need</i></sub>)</span><br></div>"	"Equation to calculate the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of an ion <i>reduced</i> to a solid</span> when you're <i>given</i> the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of electrons</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Moles<sub>reduced ion</sub> = (moles<sub>electrons <i>given</i></sub>) / (moles<sub>electrons you <i>would need</i></sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 <b>Faraday</b> represents 1 mole of electrons.</div><div>- Al<sup>3+</sup> → Al(s) would require 3 moles of electrons. If you're given 0.1 F (0.1 moles of electrons), you'd have 0.1/3 → 0.033 moles of Al<sup>3+</sup>&nbsp;would be reduced.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d1cf2dfb0e8f1ca265609fd39be8d96b.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""294e30d032ab1837c1873567f00ce477.png""></div>"
"Equation to calculate the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of an ion <i>reduced</i> to a solid</span> when you're <i>given</i> the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of electrons</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Moles&lt;sub&gt;reduced&#x20;ion&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x28;moles&lt;sub&gt;electrons&#x20;&lt;i&gt;given&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&#x20;&#x2F;&#x20;&#x28;moles&lt;sub&gt;electrons&#x20;you&#x20;&lt;i&gt;would&#x20;need&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation to calculate the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of an ion <i>reduced</i> to a solid</span> when you're <i>given</i> the number of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">moles of electrons</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Moles<sub>reduced ion</sub> = (moles<sub>electrons <i>given</i></sub>) / (moles<sub>electrons you <i>would need</i></sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 <b>Faraday</b> represents 1 mole of electrons.</div><div>- Al<sup>3+</sup> → Al(s) would require 3 moles of electrons. If you're given 0.1 F (0.1 moles of electrons), you'd have 0.1/3 → 0.033 moles of Al<sup>3+</sup>&nbsp;would be reduced.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d1cf2dfb0e8f1ca265609fd39be8d96b.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""294e30d032ab1837c1873567f00ce477.png""></div>"
"<div>Steps to calculate the moles of ions of Al<sup>3+</sup>&nbsp;<i>reduced</i> to Al(s) when you're given 0.1 F:</div><div><br></div><div>Knowing that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""0&#x2E;1&#x20;F&#x20;corresponds&#x20;to&#x20;0&#x2E;1&#x20;moles&#x20;of&#x20;electrons"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>...</div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Moles&lt;sub&gt;ions&#x20;reduced&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;moles&lt;sub&gt;electrons&#x20;&lt;i&gt;given&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x2F;&#x20;moles&lt;sub&gt;electrons&#x20;you&#x20;would&#x20;&lt;i&gt;need&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Moles&lt;sub&gt;ions&#x20;reduced&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;0&#x2E;1&#x20;mol&#x20;&#x2F;&#x20;3&#x20;mol&#x20;→&#x20;0&#x2E;033&#x20;mol&#x20;Al&lt;sup&gt;3&#x2B;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x20;reduced"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>Steps to calculate the moles of ions of Al<sup>3+</sup>&nbsp;<i>reduced</i> to Al(s) when you're given 0.1 F:</div><div><br></div><div>Knowing that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">0.1 F corresponds to 0.1 moles of electrons</span>...</div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Moles<sub>ions reduced</sub> = moles<sub>electrons <i>given</i></sub> / moles<sub>electrons you would <i>need</i></sub></span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Moles<sub>ions reduced</sub> = 0.1 mol / 3 mol → 0.033 mol Al<sup>3+</sup> reduced</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- 1 <b>Faraday</b> represents 1 mole of electrons.</div><div>- Al<sup>3+</sup> → Al(s) would require 3 moles of electrons. If you're given 0.1 F (0.1 moles of electrons), you'd have 0.1/3 → 0.033 moles of Al<sup>3+</sup>&nbsp;would be reduced.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d1cf2dfb0e8f1ca265609fd39be8d96b.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""294e30d032ab1837c1873567f00ce477.png""></div>"
"To classify as <u>photon emission</u> as either <b>Lyman</b>, <b>Balmer</b>, or <b>Paschen</b>, the <i>initial</i> principle quantum number (n<sub>i</sub>) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> matter - <i>only</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;final&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;quantum&#x20;number&#x20;&#x28;n&lt;sub&gt;f&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&#x20;that&#x20;it&#x20;&lt;i&gt;lands&#x20;on&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> matters."	"To classify as <u>photon emission</u> as either <b>Lyman</b>, <b>Balmer</b>, or <b>Paschen</b>, the <i>initial</i> principle quantum number (n<sub>i</sub>) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> matter - <i>only</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>final</i> quantum number (n<sub>f</sub>) that it <i>lands on</i></span> matters.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Falling from n=3 → n=1 is Lyman.</div><div>- Falling from n=8 → n=2 is Balmer.</div><div>- Falling from n=22 → n=3 is Paschen.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""a4f18b49f5fe68eb40b74fd962f7c3b6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""80b27b2c3bce88d071f97cf63a01ca90.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><a href=""https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yv9NAo4oxLQ"">https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yv9NAo4oxLQ</a></div></div>"
"<div>This chiral center is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""R"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S/R]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9192d87bb4cea4d4cde50d8e235c480d99591018.jpg"">"	"<div>This chiral center is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">R</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9192d87bb4cea4d4cde50d8e235c480d99591018.jpg"">

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>You have to go out 1 carbon at a time. The side chain's first carbon is bound to 2 hydrogens and Selenium, where as the carbonyl carbon is bound to 2 oxygens. <u>Selenium has a higher total molecular weight</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d9bdcb6c26e9e9fe36de52ac629b2ae757592cbe.jpg""></div>"

"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""soluble"" data-ordinal=""2"">[soluble/insoluble]</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">lower</span> number."	"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">soluble</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">lower</span> number.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In order for a compound to be considered insoluble, it would have to have an ion product constant (IP) that exceeds K<sub>sp</sub>. If K<sub>sp</sub> is high, the majority of the time its IP will not be able to exceed K<sub>sp</sub>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""a8d2a8b39de3a1d0836e4fe577cd5f1b.png""></div>"
"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">soluble</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""lower"" data-ordinal=""1"">[lower/greater]</span> number."	"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">soluble</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">lower</span> number.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In order for a compound to be considered insoluble, it would have to have an ion product constant (IP) that exceeds K<sub>sp</sub>. If K<sub>sp</sub> is high, the majority of the time its IP will not be able to exceed K<sub>sp</sub>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""a8d2a8b39de3a1d0836e4fe577cd5f1b.png""></div>"
"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">insoluble</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""higher"" data-ordinal=""1"">[lower/greater]</span> number."	"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">insoluble</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">higher</span> number.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In order for a compound to be considered insoluble, it would have to have an ion product constant (IP) that exceeds Ksp. If K<sub>sp</sub> is low, the majority of the time its IP will exceed its K<sub>sp</sub>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""a8d2a8b39de3a1d0836e4fe577cd5f1b.png""></div>"
"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""insoluble"" data-ordinal=""2"">[soluble/insoluble]</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">higher</span> number."	"Compounds with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>low</i></span> <b>K<sub>sp</sub></b>&nbsp;are <i>mostly</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">insoluble</span> in a given solution, because its <u>ion product (IP)</u> likely will be a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">higher</span> number.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>In order for a compound to be considered insoluble, it would have to have an ion product constant (IP) that exceeds Ksp. If K<sub>sp</sub> is low, the majority of the time its IP will exceed its K<sub>sp</sub>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""a8d2a8b39de3a1d0836e4fe577cd5f1b.png""></div>"
"The <b>ionization energy</b> of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Boron&#x20;&#x28;B&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[boron/beryllium]</span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Beryllium&#x20;&#x28;Be&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[boron/beryllium]</span>, because <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""boron"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>'s valence electron has an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>unpaired</i>&nbsp;2p<sup>1&nbsp;</sup>electron</span>&nbsp;- it's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i></span> stable than the 2s<sup>2</sup> orbital.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7cf88ec86ea35211835de0d924d43a1c09a23f9d.jpg""><br></div>"	"The <b>ionization energy</b> of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Boron (B)</span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Beryllium (Be)</span>, because <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">boron</span>'s valence electron has an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>unpaired</i>&nbsp;2p<sup>1&nbsp;</sup>electron</span>&nbsp;- it's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i></span> stable than the 2s<sup>2</sup> orbital.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7cf88ec86ea35211835de0d924d43a1c09a23f9d.jpg""><br></div>

"To prepare a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;buffer&#x20;solution&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[solution]</span>, choose compounds with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a</sub> <i>within</i>&nbsp;±1</span>&nbsp;of the desired pH."	"To prepare a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>buffer solution</b></span>, choose compounds with a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a</sub> <i>within</i>&nbsp;±1</span>&nbsp;of the desired pH.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- To construct an acidic buffer, the compounds you choose will be acidic (since they need to have pK<sub>a&nbsp;</sub>values close to the desired pH).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5250efec74e53b215b01074d1978b1dab5428a52.jpg""></div>"
"To prepare a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>buffer solution</b></span>, choose compounds with a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pK&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&lt;i&gt;within&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#xB1;1"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of the desired pH."	"To prepare a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>buffer solution</b></span>, choose compounds with a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a</sub> <i>within</i>&nbsp;±1</span>&nbsp;of the desired pH.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"To prepare an <i>acidic</i> <b>buffer</b>, choose <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>acidic</u></span> compounds, because they'll be the <i>only</i> compounds with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pK<sub>a</sub>'s&nbsp;<i>within</i>&nbsp;±1</span> of the desired pH.

"To prepare an <i>basic</i> <b>buffer</b>, choose <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>basic</u></span> compounds, because they'll be the <i>only</i> compounds with <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pK<sub>a</sub>'s&nbsp;<i>within</i>&nbsp;±1</span> of the desired pH.

"<div>Equation to determine the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">rate order</span> a particular <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">reactant ([B])</span> has using experimental data:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;99b2641c90cae27c1fba7a8129aec6d5&#x2E;png&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation to determine the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">rate order</span> a particular <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">reactant ([B])</span> has using experimental data:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><img src=""99b2641c90cae27c1fba7a8129aec6d5.png""></span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""82b693fdde1f256fa671fb963eab9915.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Pseudo-first</b></span>&nbsp;<b>order&nbsp;</b>reactions are reactions that are <i>truly</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""second"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> order but <i>appear</i> to be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""first"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> order due to an <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;extreme&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;difference&#x20;in&#x20;concentrations"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> between the two reactants."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Pseudo-first</b></span>&nbsp;<b>order&nbsp;</b>reactions are reactions that are <i>truly</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">second</span> order but <i>appear</i> to be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span> order due to an <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>extreme</i> difference in concentrations</span> between the two reactants.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: <b>rate = k[1.25][0.005]</b> is truly second order, but since 1.25 is a significantly larger concentration than 0.005, the reaction falsely appears to be first order.</div><div><br></div><img src=""pseudo-first-order-reaction.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Pseudo&#x2D;first&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<b>order&nbsp;</b>reactions are reactions that are <i>truly</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">second</span> order but <i>appear</i> to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span> order due to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>extreme</i> difference in concentrations</span> between the two reactants."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Pseudo-first</b></span>&nbsp;<b>order&nbsp;</b>reactions are reactions that are <i>truly</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">second</span> order but <i>appear</i> to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">first</span> order due to an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>extreme</i> difference in concentrations</span> between the two reactants.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: <b>rate = k[1.25][0.005]</b> is truly second order, but since 1.25 is a significantly larger concentration than 0.005, the reaction falsely appears to be first order.</div><div><br></div><img src=""pseudo-first-order-reaction.png""></div>"
"Given rate = k[1.25 M][0.005 M]...<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;true&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[true/apparent]</span>&nbsp;<u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second</b></span> <b>order</b>, but the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;apparent&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[true/apparent]</span> <u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>first</b></span> <b>order</b>, due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;extreme&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;concentration&#x20;difference"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"Given rate = k[1.25 M][0.005 M]...<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>true</i></span>&nbsp;<u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second</b></span> <b>order</b>, but the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>apparent</i></span> <u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>first</b></span> <b>order</b>, due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>extreme</i> concentration difference</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is called a pseudo-first order reaction, where it appears to be first order due to an extreme imbalance in concentrations (even though it's truly second order).</div><div>- Ex: <b>rate = k[1.25][0.005]</b> is truly second order, but since 1.25 is a significantly larger concentration than 0.005, the reaction falsely appears to be first order.</div><div><br></div><img src=""pseudo-first-order-reaction.png""></div>"
"Given rate = k[1.25 M][0.005 M]...<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>true</i></span>&nbsp;<u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;second&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <b>order</b>, but the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>apparent</i></span> <u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;first&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <b>order</b>, due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>extreme</i> concentration difference</span>.</div>"	"Given rate = k[1.25 M][0.005 M]...<div><br></div><div>The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>true</i></span>&nbsp;<u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second</b></span> <b>order</b>, but the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>apparent</i></span> <u>rate order</u> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>first</b></span> <b>order</b>, due to the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>extreme</i> concentration difference</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is called a pseudo-first order reaction, where it appears to be first order due to an extreme imbalance in concentrations (even though it's truly second order).</div><div>- Ex: <b>rate = k[1.25][0.005]</b> is truly second order, but since 1.25 is a significantly larger concentration than 0.005, the reaction falsely appears to be first order.</div><div><br></div><img src=""pseudo-first-order-reaction.png""></div>"
"Using a <u><i>less</i> viscous solvent</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> influence the <b>rate</b> of a reaction by <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increasing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;collision&#x20;frequency&#x20;&#x28;A&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of the molecules."	"Using a <u><i>less</i> viscous solvent</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> influence the <b>rate</b> of a reaction by <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increasing</i> the collision frequency (A)</span> of the molecules.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""></div>"
"Adding <i>more</i> <u>solvent</u> to a reaction <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> influence the <b>rate</b> of the reaction, because it <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;the&#x20;concentration"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of the reacting species."	"Adding <i>more</i> <u>solvent</u> to a reaction <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> influence the <b>rate</b> of the reaction, because it <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreases</i> the concentration</span> of the reacting species.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>rate = k[][] → adding solvent decreases the reaction's rate because the concentration of the reacting species is decreased.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> says that after time, the concentration of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermediates</u></span> in a reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">becomes <i>stable</i></span> - the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rate&#x20;at&#x20;which&#x20;it&#x27;s&#x20;produced&#x20;&#x28;k&lt;sub&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;<i>equals</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rate&#x20;at&#x20;which&#x20;it&#x27;s&#x20;consumed&#x20;&#x28;k&lt;sub&gt;&#x2D;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x2B;&#x20;k&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> says that after time, the concentration of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermediates</u></span> in a reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">becomes <i>stable</i></span> - the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's produced (k<sub>1</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>equals</i> the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's consumed (k<sub>-1</sub> + k<sub>2</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The intermediate is produced by <b>k<sub>1</sub></b>, and it's consumed by both <b>k<sub>-1</sub></b> and <b>k<sub>2</sub></b>. Therefore, the <b>steady state approximation</b> is k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][].</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""image-9ccaebfdeae7d6e1ca94f7346574d53cc5729a7f.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;steady&#x20;state&#x20;approximation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> says that after time, the concentration of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermediates</u></span> in a reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">becomes <i>stable</i></span> - the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's produced (k<sub>1</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>equals</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's consumed (k<sub>-1</sub> + k<sub>2</sub>)</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> says that after time, the concentration of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermediates</u></span> in a reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">becomes <i>stable</i></span> - the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's produced (k<sub>1</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>equals</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's consumed (k<sub>-1</sub> + k<sub>2</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The intermediate is produced by <b>k<sub>1</sub></b>, and it's consumed by both <b>k<sub>-1</sub></b> and <b>k<sub>2</sub></b>. Therefore, the <b>steady state approximation</b> is k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][].</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""image-9ccaebfdeae7d6e1ca94f7346574d53cc5729a7f.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> says that after time, the concentration of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;intermediates&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in a reaction <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""becomes&#x20;&lt;i&gt;stable&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> - the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's produced (k<sub>1</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>equals</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's consumed (k<sub>-1</sub> + k<sub>2</sub>)</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> says that after time, the concentration of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>intermediates</u></span> in a reaction <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">becomes <i>stable</i></span> - the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's produced (k<sub>1</sub>)</span>&nbsp;<i>equals</i> the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">rate at which it's consumed (k<sub>-1</sub> + k<sub>2</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>The intermediate is produced by <b>k<sub>1</sub></b>, and it's consumed by both <b>k<sub>-1</sub></b> and <b>k<sub>2</sub></b>. Therefore, the <b>steady state approximation</b> is k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][].</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""image-9ccaebfdeae7d6e1ca94f7346574d53cc5729a7f.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Give an expression for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;steady&#x20;state&#x20;approximation&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> for the below reaction.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg"">"	"<div>Give an expression for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> for the below reaction.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The <b>steady state approximation</b> is a state in a reaction at which the concentration of the <u>intermediates eventually stabilize</u>.&nbsp;</div><div>- The intermediate is produced by <b>k<sub>1</sub></b>, and it's consumed by both <b>k<sub>-1</sub></b> and <b>k<sub>2</sub></b>. Therefore, the <u>steady state approximation</u> is k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][].</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""image-9ccaebfdeae7d6e1ca94f7346574d53cc5729a7f.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Give an expression for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> for the below reaction.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""k&lt;sub&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x5B;&#x5D;&#x5B;&#x5D;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;k&lt;sub&gt;&#x2D;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x5B;&#x5D;&#x5B;&#x5D;&#x20;&#x2B;&#x20;k&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x5B;&#x5D;&#x5B;&#x5D;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[expression]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg"">"	"<div>Give an expression for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>steady state approximation</b></span> for the below reaction.</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][]</span></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- The <b>steady state approximation</b> is a state in a reaction at which the concentration of the <u>intermediates eventually stabilize</u>.&nbsp;</div><div>- The intermediate is produced by <b>k<sub>1</sub></b>, and it's consumed by both <b>k<sub>-1</sub></b> and <b>k<sub>2</sub></b>. Therefore, the <u>steady state approximation</u> is k<sub>1</sub>[][] = k<sub>-1</sub>[][] + k<sub>2</sub>[][].</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-86781b5bfac063ffb73f84f7b5bb81dd09434217.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""image-9ccaebfdeae7d6e1ca94f7346574d53cc5729a7f.jpg""></div>"
"If a change in the conditions of a reaction resulted in a change to the reaction's <b>rate</b>, the <i>only</i> explanation is that it&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""directly&#x20;impacted&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;rate&#x2D;determining&#x20;step&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"If a change in the conditions of a reaction resulted in a change to the reaction's <b>rate</b>, the <i>only</i> explanation is that it&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">directly impacted the <u>rate-determining step</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Ex: if O<sub>2</sub> became more involved in the rate of a reaction once it was carried out at a higher temperature, the only inference you can make is that a higher temperature altered the rate's reaction in a way that made O<sub>2</sub> apart of the rate determining step.</div>"
"In a reaction, if an increase in temperature resulted in O<sub>2</sub> affecting the reaction's <b>rate</b>, what could you conclude about this?<br><br><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""You&#x20;can&#x20;&lt;i&gt;only&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;conclude&#x20;that&#x20;the&#x20;mechanism&#x20;changed&#x20;in&#x20;a&#x20;way&#x20;that&#x20;allowed&#x20;O&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;part&#x2D;take&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;rate&#x2D;determining&#x20;step&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;since&#x20;rates&#x20;are&#x20;only&#x20;affected&#x20;by&#x20;rate&#x2D;determining&#x20;steps&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"In a reaction, if an increase in temperature resulted in O<sub>2</sub> affecting the reaction's <b>rate</b>, what could you conclude about this?<br><br><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">You can <i>only</i> conclude that the mechanism changed in a way that allowed O<sub>2</sub> to part-take in the <u>rate-determining step</u> (since rates are only affected by rate-determining steps).</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Ex: if O<sub>2</sub> became more involved in the rate of a reaction once it was carried out at a higher temperature, the only inference you can make is that a higher temperature altered the rate's reaction in a way that made O<sub>2</sub> apart of the rate determining step.</div>"
"According to the <b>chelating effect</b>, the bidendate ligand's formation of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;second&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[first/second]</span> <u>coordinate bond</u> happens <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>faster</i></span> than the formation of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""first"" data-ordinal=""1"">[first/second]</span> <u>coordinate bond</u>." <hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- First order → rate depends only on 1 product (hence why the line of product appearance/reactant disappearance is less exaggerated).</div><div>- Second order → rate depends only 2 products (hence why the line of product appearance/reactant disappearance is more exaggerated).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c62415e35786dcda17ffb406b16c8cc0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""d736a1e5ecc54db1fde1dafe8b08e20a.png""><br></div>"
"On a graph of reactant disappearance or product appearance for a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>second</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction, the <u>slope</u> of the line is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more]</span> exaggerated when compared to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>first</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction."	"On a graph of reactant disappearance or product appearance for a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>second</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction, the <u>slope</u> of the line is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> exaggerated when compared to a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>first</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- First order → rate depends only on 1 product (hence why the line of product appearance/reactant disappearance is less exaggerated).</div><div>- Second order → rate depends only 2 products (hence why the line of product appearance/reactant disappearance is more exaggerated).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""c62415e35786dcda17ffb406b16c8cc0.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""d736a1e5ecc54db1fde1dafe8b08e20a.png""><br></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><u>Sublimation</u>: solid → gas.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-79f4e545381ecff3134b24008ea6394d82d50f48.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"<div>How do you calculate the&nbsp;<u>ionization energy</u> of an atom with <i>multiple</i> ionization energies (Ca → Ca<sup>2+</sup>)?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Take&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;sum&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;both&#x20;the&#x20;first&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;second&#x20;ionization&#x20;energies&#x20;&#x28;IE&lt;sub&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x2B;&#x20;IE&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;total&#x20;IE&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>How do you calculate the&nbsp;<u>ionization energy</u> of an atom with <i>multiple</i> ionization energies (Ca → Ca<sup>2+</sup>)?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Take the <i>sum</i> of both the first &amp; second ionization energies (IE<sub>1</sub> + IE<sub>2</sub> = total IE).</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Lithium only has 1 ionization energy, so you don't need to do that for this. For something like calcium though, add up both its first and second ionization energy.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-dcce877cb91502bd37c762b32717efc4ff3f3ee7.jpg""></div>"
"Converting a heterogeneous mixture into a <i>pure</i> homogenous solution is accompanied by a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decrease&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increase/decrease]</span> in <u>entropy</u>.&nbsp;"	"Converting a heterogeneous mixture into a <i>pure</i> homogenous solution is accompanied by a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decrease</i></span> in <u>entropy</u>.&nbsp;
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because you're going from a more disordered state to a more ordered state.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-9dde48ea99242a0b87cc1ec7602c3a96a72dfbf7.jpg""></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-standard</i> <b>free energy (ΔG)</b></span> of a reaction that takes <i>both</i>&nbsp;the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reaction quotient</u> (Q)</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equilibrium constant</u> (K<sub>eq</sub>)</span> into account:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ΔG&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;RTln&#x28;Q&#x2F;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-standard</i> <b>free energy (ΔG)</b></span> of a reaction that takes <i>both</i>&nbsp;the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reaction quotient</u> (Q)</span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equilibrium constant</u> (K<sub>eq</sub>)</span> into account:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ΔG = RTln(Q/K<sub>eq</sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>ΔG</b> = non-standard free energy; <b>Q</b> = reaction quotient; <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> = equilibrium constant.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5fb30301a269c9153ba97c255a35895ca20bdb8d.jpg""></div>"
"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;non&#x2D;standard&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;b&gt;free&#x20;energy&#x20;&#x28;ΔG&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of a reaction that takes <i>both</i>&nbsp;the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;reaction&#x20;quotient&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;Q&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;equilibrium&#x20;constant&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> into account:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ΔG = RTln(Q/K<sub>eq</sub>)</span><br></div>"	"Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>non-standard</i> <b>free energy (ΔG)</b></span> of a reaction that takes <i>both</i>&nbsp;the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>reaction quotient</u> (Q)</span> and <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>equilibrium constant</u> (K<sub>eq</sub>)</span> into account:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ΔG = RTln(Q/K<sub>eq</sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>-&nbsp;<b>ΔG</b> = non-standard free energy; <b>Q</b> = reaction quotient; <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b> = equilibrium constant.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5fb30301a269c9153ba97c255a35895ca20bdb8d.jpg""></div>"
"The <u>standard state</u> of <b>carbon</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""graphite"" data-ordinal=""1"">[diamond/graphite]</span>."	"The <u>standard state</u> of <b>carbon</b> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">graphite</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>second</b></span> <b>law of thermodynamics</b> says that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;entropy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;is&#x20;&lt;i&gt;always&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;increasing"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;&amp; heat flows from <i>higher</i> temperature to <i>lower</i> temperature objects."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>second</b></span> <b>law of thermodynamics</b> says that <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>entropy</u> is <i>always </i>increasing</span>&nbsp;&amp; heat flows from <i>higher</i> temperature to <i>lower</i> temperature objects.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>As a system becomes more ordered (-S), the entropy of the surroundings becomes even more disordered (++S).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""58778a4aa58bd4923326aa451e51380c.png""><br></div></div>"
"As a <u>system</u> becomes <i>more</i> ordered (-S), the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;surroundings&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> become <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;even&#x20;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;disordered&#x20;&#x28;&#x2B;&#x2B;S&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> - resulting in the net <i>increase</i> in the universe's entropy associated with the 2<sup>nd</sup> law of thermodynaimcs."	"As a <u>system</u> becomes <i>more</i> ordered (-S), the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>surroundings</u></span> become <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>even more</i> disordered (++S)</span> - resulting in the net <i>increase</i> in the universe's entropy associated with the 2<sup>nd</sup> law of thermodynaimcs.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This explains how we're able to have highly organized things like cells. Even though the cells are highly ordered, there was an even greater increase in the entropy of the universe, so it was able to ""off set"" the cell's orderedness.</div><div>- As a system becomes more ordered (-S), the entropy of the surroundings becomes even more disordered (++S).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""58778a4aa58bd4923326aa451e51380c.png""></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Weak&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> <b>acids/bases</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>do not</i></span> easily undergo <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>hydrolysis</u></span> reactions, since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">hydrolysis</span> reactions result in the formation of H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> &amp; OH<sup>-</sup> ions.&nbsp;"	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Weak</b></span> <b>acids/bases</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>do not</i></span> easily undergo <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>hydrolysis</u></span> reactions, since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">hydrolysis</span> reactions result in the formation of H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> &amp; OH<sup>-</sup> ions.&nbsp;

"For an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction, <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span> as temperature is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increased</i></span>.

<div>- <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> is a ratio of the products to the reactants.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since heat for an exothermic reaction behaves like a product of the reaction, as temperature is increased, product formation decreases.</div><div><br></div><img src=""d9ffa396a3d411faa300076a56e653ae.png""></div>"

"For an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction, <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> as temperature is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decreased</i></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> is a ratio of the products to the reactants.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since heat for an exothermic reaction behaves like a product of the reaction, as temperature is decreased, product formation increases.</div><div><br></div><img src=""d9ffa396a3d411faa300076a56e653ae.png""></div>"
"For an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction, <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increases/decreases/same]</span> as temperature is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increased</i></span>."	"For an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction, <b>K<sub>a</sub></b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> as temperature is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increased</i></span>.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>4</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>3</sub></b></span>, since the extra oxygen <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""provides&#x20;&lt;u&gt;resonance&#x20;stabilization&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> for the resulting <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""conjugate&#x20;base"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>4</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>3</sub></b></span>, since the extra oxygen <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">provides <u>resonance stabilization</u></span> for the resulting <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">conjugate base</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This trend is generally true for most oxyacids (acids with multiple oxygen atoms).</div><div><br></div><img src=""maxresdefault-c7bbf2abb0a81c0368ff320410fe988274651e1c.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;HBrO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[HBrO<sub>3</sub>/HBrO<sub>4</sub>]</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;HBrO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[HBrO<sub>3</sub>/HBrO<sub>4</sub>]</span>, since the extra oxygen <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">provides <u>resonance stabilization</u></span> for the resulting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">conjugate base</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>4</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>3</sub></b></span>, since the extra oxygen <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">provides <u>resonance stabilization</u></span> for the resulting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">conjugate base</span>.

<div>This trend is generally true for most oxyacids (acids with multiple oxygen atoms).</div><div><br></div><img src=""maxresdefault-c7bbf2abb0a81c0368ff320410fe988274651e1c.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;HBrO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[HBrO<sub>3</sub>/HBrO<sub>4</sub>]</span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;HBrO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[HBrO<sub>3</sub>/HBrO<sub>4</sub>]</span>, since having 1 less oxygen results in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i>&nbsp;resonance stabilization</span> for the resulting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">conjugate base</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>3</sub></b></span>&nbsp;is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>HBrO<sub>4</sub></b></span>, since having 1 less oxygen results in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>less</i>&nbsp;resonance stabilization</span> for the resulting <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">conjugate base</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This trend is generally true for most oxyacids (acids with multiple oxygen atoms).</div><div><br></div><img src=""maxresdefault-c7bbf2abb0a81c0368ff320410fe988274651e1c.jpg""></div>"
"Shortcut to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;pH&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;acid&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> when <i>given</i> its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pK&lt;sub&gt;a&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x28;between&#x20;2&#x20;and&#x20;12&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>...<div><br></div><div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">pH = 0.5(pK<sub>a</sub>) - (0.5(log([HA]))</span></div></div>"	"Shortcut to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pH</b> of a <u><i>weak</i> acid</u></span> when <i>given</i> its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a&nbsp;</sub>(between 2 and 12)</span>...<div><br></div><div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">pH = 0.5(pK<sub>a</sub>) - (0.5(log([HA]))</span></div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- This can ONLY be done if the pK<sub>a</sub> is between 2 and 12.<div><br></div><div>- Ex: Find the pH of a solution of 0.1 M HClO with a pK<sub>a</sub> of 7.2.</div><div>&nbsp; &nbsp;1. Plug into pH = 0.5(pKa) - (0.5(log([HA]).</div><div>&nbsp; &nbsp;2. pH = 3.6 - (0.5(log(0.1)) → pH = 3.6 - (0.5(-1)) → pH = 3.6 - (-0.5) → pH = 4.1.</div></div>"
"Shortcut to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pH</b> of a <u><i>weak</i> acid</u></span> when <i>given</i> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a&nbsp;</sub>(between 2 and 12)</span>...<div><br></div><div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pH&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;0&#x2E;5&#x28;pK&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&#x20;&#x2D;&#x20;&#x28;0&#x2E;5&#x28;log&#x28;&#x5B;HA&#x5D;&#x29;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div></div>"	"Shortcut to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>pH</b> of a <u><i>weak</i> acid</u></span> when <i>given</i> its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a&nbsp;</sub>(between 2 and 12)</span>...<div><br></div><div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">pH = 0.5(pK<sub>a</sub>) - (0.5(log([HA]))</span></div></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- This can ONLY be done if the pK<sub>a</sub> is between 2 and 12.<div><br></div><div>- Ex: Find the pH of a solution of 0.1 M HClO with a pK<sub>a</sub> of 7.2.</div><div>&nbsp; &nbsp;1. Plug into pH = 0.5(pKa) - (0.5(log([HA]).</div><div>&nbsp; &nbsp;2. pH = 3.6 - (0.5(log(0.1)) → pH = 3.6 - (0.5(-1)) → pH = 3.6 - (-0.5) → pH = 4.1.</div></div>"
"<b>Oxyacids</b> (i.e. HClO<sub>4</sub>) become <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[less/more]</span> <u>acidic</u> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativity</span> of the halide <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>."	"<b>Oxyacids</b> (i.e. HClO<sub>4</sub>) become <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativity</span> of the halide <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Don't confuse this with how the acidity of acid halides (like HCl) increases with increased halide size.<div>- A more electronegative halide is able to further weaken the bond between the O-H - making it more acidic.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7601-90-3.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Oxyacids</b> (i.e. HClO<sub>4</sub>) become <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativity</span> of the halide <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[increases/decreases]</span>."	"<b>Oxyacids</b> (i.e. HClO<sub>4</sub>) become <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativity</span> of the halide <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>increases</i></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Don't confuse this with how the acidity of acid halides (like HCl) increases with increased halide size.<div>- A more electronegative halide is able to further weaken the bond between the O-H - making it more acidic.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7601-90-3.png""><br></div></div>"
"<b>Oxyacids</b> (i.e. HClO<sub>4</sub>) become <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>acidic</u> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativity</span> of the halide <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreases&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[increases/decreases]</span>."	"<b>Oxyacids</b> (i.e. HClO<sub>4</sub>) become <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>less</i></span> <u>acidic</u> as the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">electronegativity</span> of the halide <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>decreases</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Don't confuse this with how the acidity of acid halides (like HCl) increases with increased halide size.<div>- A more electronegative halide is able to further weaken the bond between the O-H - making it more acidic.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""7601-90-3.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Thiols&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[alcohols/thiols]</span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;alcohols&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[alcohols/thiols]</span> because <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">sulfur is <i>larger</i>&nbsp;in size</span> than oxygen."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thiols</b></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>alcohols</b></span> because <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">sulfur is <i>larger</i>&nbsp;in size</span> than oxygen.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Since sulfur is larger than oxygen, it more easily loses its H<sup>+</sup> proton.</div><div>- As you move down the perioidic table, a larger atom results in greater acidity.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-147fcbd6dc88f2213d4b3d38884aefac99963a64.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""2-acidity-increases-going-down-the-periodic-table-alcohol-pkas-about-16-thiol-pkas-about-10-11-and-selenol-pka-abou.gif""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thiols</b></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>alcohols</b></span> because <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""sulfur&#x20;is&#x20;&lt;i&gt;larger&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;in&#x20;size"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> than oxygen."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Thiols</b></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>more</i></span> <u>acidic</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>alcohols</b></span> because <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">sulfur is <i>larger</i>&nbsp;in size</span> than oxygen.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<b>Halogens</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> have <u><i>positive</i> oxidation states</u> when they're bound to other <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""negatively&#x20;charged"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> compounds."	"<b>Halogens</b> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> have <u><i>positive</i> oxidation states</u> when they're bound to other <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">negatively charged</span> compounds.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: OHCl (OH<sup>-</sup> &amp; Cl<sup>+</sup>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""aspects-of-halogens-15-638.jpg""></div>"
"The <u>oxidation state</u> of <b>chlorine</b> in OHCl is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x2B;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The <u>oxidation state</u> of <b>chlorine</b> in OHCl is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">+1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Even though halogens would typically prefer to gain an electron, they <u>can</u> have positive oxidation states when they're bound to other negatively charged groups.</div><div>- Ex: OHCl (OH<sup>-</sup> &amp; Cl<sup>+</sup>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""aspects-of-halogens-15-638.jpg""></div>"
"In redox reactions, you <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;cannot&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> designate <i>only 1</i> atom of a compound to be an <u>oxidizing or reducing agent</u> - you must designate <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;entire&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;compound"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> as being the agent."	"In redox reactions, you <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>cannot</i></span> designate <i>only 1</i> atom of a compound to be an <u>oxidizing or reducing agent</u> - you must designate <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">the <i>entire</i> compound</span> as being the agent.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: K(s) is oxidized into K<sup>+</sup>, and the H<sub>2</sub> in H<sub>2</sub>O is reduced to H<sub>2</sub>(g). K(s) is the reducing agent and <u>H</u><sub style="""">2</sub><u>O is the oxidizing agent</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-d298576b6eb947bf41bc0af9325979bd95522a51.jpg""></div>"
"Changing the <u><i>type</i> of solvent</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;can&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[can/cannot]</span> influence the <b>pH</b> of a solution, since the type of solvent influences the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ability&#x20;of&#x20;an&#x20;acid&#x20;to&#x20;&lt;i&gt;dissociate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"Changing the <u><i>type</i> of solvent</u> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>can</i></span> influence the <b>pH</b> of a solution, since the type of solvent influences the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ability of an acid to <i>dissociate</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Some acids are capable of dissociating better in certain solvents over others.</div>"
"Plugging a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> value of [H<sup>+</sup>] into -log([H<sup>+</sup>]) yields a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[low/high]</span> <b>pH</b> value."	"Plugging a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> value of [H<sup>+</sup>] into -log([H<sup>+</sup>]) yields a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <b>pH</b> value.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- -log(1 x 10<sup>-1</sup>) → pH of 1. Large H<sup>+</sup> gives a smaller pH.<div>- -log(1 x 10<sup>-5</sup>) → pH of 5. Small H<sup>+</sup> gives a larger pH.</div></div>"
"Plugging a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[low/high]</span> value of [H<sup>+</sup>] into -log([H<sup>+</sup>]) yields a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <b>pH</b> value."	"Plugging a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>high</i></span> value of [H<sup>+</sup>] into -log([H<sup>+</sup>]) yields a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <b>pH</b> value.
<hr>

- -log(1 x 10<sup>-1</sup>) → pH of 1. Large H<sup>+</sup> gives a smaller pH.<div>- -log(1 x 10<sup>-5</sup>) → pH of 5. Small H<sup>+</sup> gives a larger pH.</div></div>"
