<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- This is predicted by the Nernst equation (below). This equation describes the <b>potential (E<sub>cell</sub>)</b> in a galvanic cell at any point in time under non-standard concentrations.<div>- Since the voltage decay is <u>logarithmic</u>, it's stable for majority of the time.<br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-905983e0caecef6d40e809098c412f4d1b52e82e.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"<div>Graph depicting the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>voltage <i>decay</i></b></span> over time in a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>galvanic</u></span> cell:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;e63994402a98f9fb50a5a993ea9935a8be057d66&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Graph depicting the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>voltage <i>decay</i></b></span> over time in a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>galvanic</u></span> cell:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-e63994402a98f9fb50a5a993ea9935a8be057d66.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- This is predicted by the Nernst equation (below). This equation describes the <b>potential (E<sub>cell</sub>)</b> in a galvanic cell at any point in time under non-standard concentrations.<div>- Since the voltage decay is <u>logarithmic</u>, it's stable for majority of the time.<br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-905983e0caecef6d40e809098c412f4d1b52e82e.jpg""><br></div></div></div>"
"<div>In an electrolytic cell using ZnCl<sub>2</sub>, to determine the <u>moles of zinc</u> <i>deposited</i> on the cathode, plug <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">2</span> in for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;n&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[variable]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""5ebfa538e263e94cf00c2bb5a0800b4e.png"">"	"<div>In an electrolytic cell using ZnCl<sub>2</sub>, to determine the <u>moles of zinc</u> <i>deposited</i> on the cathode, plug <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">2</span> in for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>n</b></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""5ebfa538e263e94cf00c2bb5a0800b4e.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Zn in ZnCl<sub>2</sub> has an oxidation state of +2. For zinc to be reduced to its solid form, it would need to accept 2 electrons (hence why 'n' is 2).</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>mol M</b>&nbsp;= amout of metal being deposited;&nbsp;<b>I</b>&nbsp;= current;&nbsp;<b>t&nbsp;</b>= time;&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= number of electron <u>equivalents</u> of the metal;&nbsp;<b>F</b>&nbsp;= faraday's constant (10<sup>5</sup>)</div><div>- In&nbsp;<u>electrolysis</u>, this is stated as saying the amount of electrons a compound loses after its redox reaction is equivalent to the number of electrons in the current.<br></div><div>- Below shows 'n' moles of electrons are needed to produce 1 mole of M (s).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d1cf2dfb0e8f1ca265609fd39be8d96b.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""294e30d032ab1837c1873567f00ce477.png""></div>"
"<div>In an electrolytic cell using ZnCl<sub>2</sub>, to determine the <u>moles of zinc</u> <i>deposited</i> on the cathode, plug <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""2"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> in for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>n</b></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""5ebfa538e263e94cf00c2bb5a0800b4e.png"">"	"<div>In an electrolytic cell using ZnCl<sub>2</sub>, to determine the <u>moles of zinc</u> <i>deposited</i> on the cathode, plug <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">2</span> in for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>n</b></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""5ebfa538e263e94cf00c2bb5a0800b4e.png"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Zn in ZnCl<sub>2</sub> has an oxidation state of +2. For zinc to be reduced to its solid form, it would need to accept 2 electrons (hence why 'n' is 2).</div><div><br></div><div>-&nbsp;<b>mol M</b>&nbsp;= amout of metal being deposited;&nbsp;<b>I</b>&nbsp;= current;&nbsp;<b>t&nbsp;</b>= time;&nbsp;<b>n</b>&nbsp;= number of electron <u>equivalents</u> of the metal;&nbsp;<b>F</b>&nbsp;= faraday's constant (10<sup>5</sup>)</div><div>- In&nbsp;<u>electrolysis</u>, this is stated as saying the amount of electrons a compound loses after its redox reaction is equivalent to the number of electrons in the current.<br></div><div>- Below shows 'n' moles of electrons are needed to produce 1 mole of M (s).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""d1cf2dfb0e8f1ca265609fd39be8d96b.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""294e30d032ab1837c1873567f00ce477.png""></div>"
"<div>The Nernst equation predicts that the <b>voltage</b> in a galvanic cell will <i>decay</i> according to the following graph, due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""logarithmic&#x20;&lt;i&gt;consumption&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;reactant&#x20;&#x28;↑log&#x28;Q&#x29;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e63994402a98f9fb50a5a993ea9935a8be057d66.jpg"">"	"<div>The Nernst equation predicts that the <b>voltage</b> in a galvanic cell will <i>decay</i> according to the following graph, due to the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">logarithmic <i>consumption</i> of the reactant (↑log(Q))</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e63994402a98f9fb50a5a993ea9935a8be057d66.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Recall that the Nernst equation factors in the reactant quotient Q (products/reactants), which gets subtracted from E°<sub>cell</sub>. A larger log(Q) results in a larger number being subtracted.</div>- The Nernst equation describes the&nbsp;<b>potential (E<sub>cell</sub>)</b>&nbsp;in a galvanic cell at any point in time under non-standard concentrations.<div>- Since the voltage decay is&nbsp;<u>logarithmic</u>, it's stable for majority of the time.<br><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-905983e0caecef6d40e809098c412f4d1b52e82e.jpg""></div></div></div>"
"If a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> in temperature results in a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increase&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[increase/decrease]</span> in a reaction's <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b>, the reaction is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u>."	"If a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increase</i></span> in temperature results in a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increase</i></span> in a reaction's <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b>, the reaction is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because an increase in temperature results in the formation of more product.</div><div><br></div><div>- Increasing temperature results in increased product formation → endothermic.</div><div>- Increasing temperature results in decreased product formation → exothermic.&nbsp;</div><div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7640ed558a308e456d802bd63bb0eeaadf864ac4.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1cb0eb2b2b5ba06806afccd5be4effdce74644b9.jpg""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...because an increase in temperature results in the formation of more product.</div><div><br></div><div>- Increasing temperature results in increased product formation → endothermic.</div><div>- Increasing temperature results in decreased product formation → exothermic.&nbsp;</div><div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7640ed558a308e456d802bd63bb0eeaadf864ac4.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1cb0eb2b2b5ba06806afccd5be4effdce74644b9.jpg""></div>"
"If a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decrease&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increase/decrease]</span> in temperature results in a(n) <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increase</i></span> in a reaction's <b>K<sub>eq</sub></b>, the reaction is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u>." If you add 1 atm of CO<sub>2</sub>, you <u>cannot</u> assume that 100% of that CO<sub>2</sub> converts to a mixture of CO and O<sub>2</sub>. You need to add up the partial pressures of both the reactants and products to find the total pressure at equilibrium.</div>"

"<div>Steps to determining the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&lt;i&gt;total&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of a gaseous system at <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;equilibrium&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Set up an ICE table.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Determine the ""equilibrium expressions"" of each component of the reaction.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Plug those equilibrium expressions into equation P<sub>total</sub> = (P<sub>reactants</sub>) + (P<sub>products</sub>).</span></div>"	"<div>Steps to determining the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b><i>total</i> pressure</b></span> of a gaseous system at <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>equilibrium</u></span>:<br></div><div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Set up an ICE table.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Determine the ""equilibrium expressions"" of each component of the reaction.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Plug those equilibrium expressions into equation P<sub>total</sub> = (P<sub>reactants</sub>) + (P<sub>products</sub>).</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- If you add 1 atm of CO<sub>2</sub>, you <u>cannot</u> assume that 100% of that CO<sub>2</sub> converts to a mixture of CO and O<sub>2</sub>. You need to add up the partial pressures of both the reactants and products to find the total pressure at equilibrium.<div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-4b84c44096919f67f538ddc6e65a85989a1704db.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-334100b498d001c3d1446151424cc9b7040fb30e.jpg""><br></div><div><img src=""paste-f6a67333db4867d6e5b877866aa5497b8934c382.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"When writing a reaction's <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;pure&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;solids&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;liquids&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>excluded</i></span>."	"When writing a reaction's <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b>, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>pure</i> <u>solids</u> &amp; <u>liquids</u></span> should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>excluded</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This does NOT apply to gases or aqueous compounds.</div><div>- Ex: Adding or removig Br<sub>2</sub>(l) to the reaction mixture <u>has no effect</u> on the equilibrium ratios of each product.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg""></div>"

<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This does NOT apply to gases or aqueous compounds.</div><div>- Ex: Adding or removig Br<sub>2</sub>(l) to the reaction mixture <u>has no effect</u> on the equilibrium ratios of each product.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Write the <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b> for the below reaction:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;7404c9c3c4d8fd60eb1ea75044017dc30004ea4a&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg"">"	"<div>Write the <b>equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b> for the below reaction:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-7404c9c3c4d8fd60eb1ea75044017dc30004ea4a.jpg""></span><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg"">
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Pure solids and liquids should always be <u>excluded</u> from equilibrium expressions.</div><div>- Ex: Adding or removig Br<sub>2</sub>(l) to the reaction mixture <u>has no effect</u> on the equilibrium ratios of each product.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg""></div>"
"<div>Despite <b>Br<sub>2</sub></b> being a <u>pure liquid</u> (meaning it <i>doesn't</i> affect the equilibrium), the reaction cannot proceed when it's missing. Why?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Br&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;could&#x20;&lt;i&gt;still&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;be&#x20;a&#x20;limiting&#x20;reagent&#x2E;&#x20;Even&#x20;though&#x20;it&#x20;&lt;i&gt;doesn&#x27;t&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;influence&#x20;the&#x20;equilibrium&#x2C;&#x20;it&#x27;s&#x20;presence&#x20;is&#x20;still&#x20;needed&#x20;for&#x20;the&#x20;reaction&#x20;to&#x20;take&#x20;place&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg""><br></div>"	"<div>Despite <b>Br<sub>2</sub></b> being a <u>pure liquid</u> (meaning it <i>doesn't</i> affect the equilibrium), the reaction cannot proceed when it's missing. Why?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Br<sub>2</sub> could <i>still</i> be a limiting reagent. Even though it <i>doesn't</i> influence the equilibrium, it's presence is still needed for the reaction to take place.</span></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-b41ac8b017da97968fec8d72c80a45fc442cb97e.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7404c9c3c4d8fd60eb1ea75044017dc30004ea4a.jpg""></div>"
"Steps to determining the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>direction</i> a reaction shifts to <u>reach equilibrium</u></span>&nbsp;after you're <i>given</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">starting concentrations</span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Determine&#x20;the&#x20;reaction&#x20;quotient&#x20;&#x28;Q&#x29;&#x20;using&#x20;the&#x20;starting&#x20;concentrations&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Compare&#x20;the&#x20;size&#x20;of&#x20;Q&#x20;to&#x20;the&#x20;value&#x20;of&#x20;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;and&#x20;determing&#x20;the&#x20;direction&#x20;accordingly&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"Steps to determining the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>direction</i> a reaction shifts to <u>reach equilibrium</u></span>&nbsp;after you're <i>given</i> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">starting concentrations</span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Determine the reaction quotient (Q) using the starting concentrations.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Compare the size of Q to the value of K<sub>eq</sub>, and determing the direction accordingly.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>According to&nbsp;<b><u>LeChatlier's principle</u></b>...</div><div>- Q &lt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows there's currently more reactants... will proceed forward.</div><div>- Q = K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows that the reaction is at equilibrium.</div><div>- Q &gt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows there's currently more product... will proceed backwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e03652c8b9b055b32df188b471e11c38.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""></div></div>"
"Steps to determining the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;direction&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;a&#x20;reaction&#x20;shifts&#x20;to&#x20;&lt;u&gt;reach&#x20;equilibrium&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;after you're <i>given</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""starting&#x20;concentrations"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Determine the reaction quotient (Q) using the starting concentrations.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Compare the size of Q to the value of K<sub>eq</sub>, and determing the direction accordingly.</span></div>"	"Steps to determining the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>direction</i> a reaction shifts to <u>reach equilibrium</u></span>&nbsp;after you're <i>given</i> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">starting concentrations</span>:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Determine the reaction quotient (Q) using the starting concentrations.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Compare the size of Q to the value of K<sub>eq</sub>, and determing the direction accordingly.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>According to&nbsp;<b><u>LeChatlier's principle</u></b>...</div><div>- Q &lt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows there's currently more reactants... will proceed forward.</div><div>- Q = K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows that the reaction is at equilibrium.</div><div>- Q &gt; K<sub>eq</sub>&nbsp;shows there's currently more product... will proceed backwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""e03652c8b9b055b32df188b471e11c38.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""></div></div>"
"<div>Graph depicting how the forward rate constant (k<sub>f</sub>) and backwards rate constant (k<sub>b</sub>) <i>change</i> over time:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;a9962f41360538780716e3e20cdbdf39e91e3bb1&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br>"	"<div>Graph depicting how the forward rate constant (k<sub>f</sub>) and backwards rate constant (k<sub>b</sub>) <i>change</i> over time:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-a9962f41360538780716e3e20cdbdf39e91e3bb1.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Forward rate begins at a positive value because it can't start from zero.</div>"
"<div>The <b>equilibrium position</b> of reactions with an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;moles&#x20;of&#x20;gas&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> on either side <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>cannot</i></span> shift with a change in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>pressure</u></span>.</div>"	"<div>The <b>equilibrium position</b> of reactions with an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>moles of gas</u></span> on either side <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>cannot</i></span> shift with a change in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4""><u>pressure</u></span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- When pressure changes, volume changes. A decrease in volume favors the side with less moles of gas, while an increase in volume favors the side with more moles of gas.</div><div>- Since reactions 1 and 3 have a different number of moles on each side, these two reactions *can* respond to pressure changes.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-2e25a9ae3b960f7f707ad8f351c7b41b5af49f01.jpg""></div>"

Since there is an <u>unequal number of moles of gas on either side</u>, an <i>increase</i> in volume will favor the side with more moles &amp; a decrease in volume will favor the side with less moles. If they happened to have the same number of moles of gas on either side, this wouldn't make any difference.</span><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-713e0e03793ce1e4b0e992b067d9ab1ca4b620be.jpg""><br></div>

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Weak</b></span> <b>acids/bases</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>do not</i></span> easily undergo <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;hydrolysis&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> reactions, since <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hydrolysis"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> reactions result in the formation of H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> &amp; OH<sup>-</sup> ions.&nbsp;"	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Weak</b></span> <b>acids/bases</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>do not</i></span> easily undergo <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>hydrolysis</u></span> reactions, since <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">hydrolysis</span> reactions result in the formation of H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> &amp; OH<sup>-</sup> ions.&nbsp;

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- A compound that undergoes 20% hydrolysis in solution is likely a good acid or base.</div><div>- A compound that undergoes 5% hydrolysis is likely a weak acid or base.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6a89bb10f90cd04121128e6951fa8494.png""></div>"

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Strong</b></span> <b>acids/bases</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>do</i></span> easily undergo <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;hydrolysis&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> reactions, since <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""hydrolysis"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> reactions result in the formation of H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> &amp; OH<sup>-</sup> ions.&nbsp;"	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Strong</b></span> <b>acids/bases</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>do</i></span> easily undergo <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>hydrolysis</u></span> reactions, since <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">hydrolysis</span> reactions result in the formation of H<sub>3</sub>O<sup>+</sup> &amp; OH<sup>-</sup> ions.&nbsp;
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- A compound that undergoes 20% hydrolysis in solution is likely a good acid or base.</div><div>- A compound that undergoes 5% hydrolysis is likely a weak acid or base.</div><div><br></div><img src=""6a89bb10f90cd04121128e6951fa8494.png""></div>"
"For a <b>buffer</b> composed of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""diprotic&#x20;acid"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;conjugate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;base&#x20;&#x28;i&#x2E;e&#x2E;&#x20;H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;CO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;HCO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>, the <i>first </i>buffer region's <u>pH</u> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i> to its pK<sub>a1</sub></span>."	"For a <b>buffer</b> composed of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, the <i>first </i>buffer region's <u>pH</u> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i> to its pK<sub>a1</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"For a <b>buffer</b> composed of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, the <i>first </i>buffer region's <u>pH</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;equal&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;its&#x20;pK&lt;sub&gt;a1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"For a <b>buffer</b> composed of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, the <i>first </i>buffer region's <u>pH</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i> to its pK<sub>a1</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"<div>For a <b>buffer solution</b> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pK&lt;sub&gt;a1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[pK<sub>a1</sub>/pK<sub>a2</sub>]</span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>first&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u> ([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.</div>"	"<div>For a <b>buffer solution</b> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a1</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>first&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u> ([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the first proton is the only proton being given away to generate the buffer.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"<div>For a <b>buffer solution</b> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""diprotic&#x20;acid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;conjugate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;base&#x20;&#x28;i&#x2E;e&#x2E;&#x20;H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;CO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;HCO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a1</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>first&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u> ([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.</div>"	"<div>For a <b>buffer solution</b> of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a1</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>first&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u> ([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the first proton is the only proton being given away to generate the buffer.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"<div>For a <b>buffer solution</b> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a1</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;first&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;buffer&#x20;region&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;&#x5B;HA&#x5D;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x5B;A&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x5D;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>.</div>"	"<div>For a <b>buffer solution</b> of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i> base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> &amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a1</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>first&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u> ([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the first proton is the only proton being given away to generate the buffer.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Buffer solutions</b> are <i>always</i> made by mixing a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""weak&#x20;acid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> with its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""conjugate&#x20;base"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"<b>Buffer solutions</b> are <i>always</i> made by mixing a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">weak acid</span> with its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">conjugate base</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Sometimes you might see a weak acid being mixed with a strong base, or a weak base being mixed with a strong acid. The strong acid/base in that case is only used (in small enough concentratons) to protonate/deprotonate half of the weak acid/base to <u>create its conjugate form</u>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-5250efec74e53b215b01074d1978b1dab5428a52.jpg""></div>"
"If <b>buffer solutions</b> are <i>only</i> made of weak acids with their conjugate bases, why do some buffer solutions use strong acids/bases in small concentrations?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""In&#x20;a&#x20;small&#x20;enough&#x20;concentration&#x2C;&#x20;a&#x20;strong&#x20;acid&#x2F;base&#x20;can&#x20;be&#x20;used&#x20;to&#x20;protonate&#x2F;deprotonate&#x20;&lt;i&gt;half&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;weak&#x20;acid&#x2F;base&#x20;to&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;create&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;its&#x20;conjugate&#x20;form&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x2E;&#x20;The&#x20;strong&#x20;acid&#x2F;base&#x20;itself&#x20;&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;participate&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;buffer&#x20;&#x2D;&#x20;it&#x20;&lt;i&gt;only&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;generates&#x20;the&#x20;other&#x20;conjugate&#x20;component&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"If <b>buffer solutions</b> are <i>only</i> made of weak acids with their conjugate bases, why do some buffer solutions use strong acids/bases in small concentrations?<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">In a small enough concentration, a strong acid/base can be used to protonate/deprotonate <i>half</i> of the weak acid/base to&nbsp;<u><i>create</i> its conjugate form</u>. The strong acid/base itself <i>does not</i> participate in the buffer - it <i>only</i> generates the other conjugate component.</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: a <u>benzoic acid buffer</u> can be generated by mixing benzoic acid with a small concentration of a strong base (to deprotonate 50% of benzoic acid to form benzoate).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5250efec74e53b215b01074d1978b1dab5428a52.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>To make a <b>buffer solution</b> using a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weak/strong]</span> <u>acid</u> &amp; a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[weak/strong]</span> <u>base</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">2:1</span>.</div>"	"<div>To make a <b>buffer solution</b> using a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> <u>acid</u> &amp; a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>base</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">2:1</span>.</div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- There should be 2x the moles of weak acid to that of the strong base.</div>- The strong base in this case is added in order to <u>generate the conjugate form of the weak acid</u>. The buffer should be composed of equal concentrations of [HA] to [A-], so only 50% of the moles of strong base should be added.</div>"
"<div>To make a <b>buffer solution</b> using a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> <u>acid</u> &amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>base</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""2&#x3A;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[ratio]</span>.</div>"	"<div>To make a <b>buffer solution</b> using a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span> <u>acid</u> &amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span> <u>base</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">2:1</span>.</div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

The buffer should be composed of equal concentrations of [HA] to [A<sup>-</sup>], so only 50% of the moles of strong acid should be added.</div>"
"To make a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;using a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span>&nbsp;<u>acid</u>&nbsp;&amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span>&nbsp;<u>base</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""molar&#x20;ratio&#x20;of&#x20;acid&#x3A;base"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1:2</span>."	"To make a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;using a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span>&nbsp;<u>acid</u>&nbsp;&amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span>&nbsp;<u>base</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1:2</span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

"To make a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;using a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>strong</i></span>&nbsp;<u>acid</u>&nbsp;&amp; a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>weak</i></span>&nbsp;<u>base</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1:2</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- There should be 2x the moles of weak base to that of the strong acid.</div>- The strong acid in this case is added in order to <u>generate the conjugate form of the weak base</u>. The buffer should be composed of equal concentrations of [HA] to [A<sup>-</sup>], so only 50% of the moles of strong acid should be added.</div>"
"To make a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;using a <i>weak</i>&nbsp;<u>acid</u>&nbsp;&amp; its <u>conjugate&nbsp;base</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""1&#x3A;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[ratio]</span>."	"To make a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;using a <i>weak</i>&nbsp;<u>acid</u>&nbsp;&amp; its <u>conjugate&nbsp;base</u>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">1:1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Since a buffer is 50% weak acid and 50% conjugate base, they should be added in equal ratios.<div></div></div>"
"To make a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;using a <i>weak</i>&nbsp;<u>acid</u>&nbsp;&amp; its <u>conjugate&nbsp;base</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""molar&#x20;ratio&#x20;of&#x20;acid&#x3A;base"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1:1</span>."	"To make a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;using a <i>weak</i>&nbsp;<u>acid</u>&nbsp;&amp; its <u>conjugate&nbsp;base</u>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">molar ratio of acid:base</span>&nbsp;should be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">1:1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Since a buffer is 50% weak acid and 50% conjugate base, they should be added in equal ratios.<div></div></div>"
"In a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <i>ratio</i> of the&nbsp;<u>weak acid/base</u> to its <u>conjugate form</u>&nbsp;<i>cannot</i> exceed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">10:1</span>, because exceeding that would <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cause&#x20;the&#x20;pH&#x20;to&#x20;be&#x20;&lt;i&gt;outside&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&#xB1;1&#x20;of&#x20;its&#x20;pK&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <i>ratio</i> of the&nbsp;<u>weak acid/base</u> to its <u>conjugate form</u>&nbsp;<i>cannot</i> exceed <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">10:1</span>, because exceeding that would <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">cause the pH to be <i>outside</i> ±1 of its pK<sub>a</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Remember, the pH scale is logarithmic. Each change in a pH unit represents a change by a multiple of 10.</div>"
"In a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <i>ratio</i> of the&nbsp;<u>weak acid/base</u> to its <u>conjugate form</u>&nbsp;<i>cannot</i> exceed <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""10&#x3A;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[ratio]</span>, because exceeding that would <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cause the pH to be <i>outside</i> ±1 of its pK<sub>a</sub></span>."	"In a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <i>ratio</i> of the&nbsp;<u>weak acid/base</u> to its <u>conjugate form</u>&nbsp;<i>cannot</i> exceed <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">10:1</span>, because exceeding that would <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">cause the pH to be <i>outside</i> ±1 of its pK<sub>a</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Remember, the pH scale is logarithmic. Each change in a pH unit represents a change by a multiple of 10.</div>"
"When the <b>pH</b> <i>exactly</i> equals an acid's pK<sub>a</sub>, the [HA] to [A<sup>-</sup>] ratio is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;exactly&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;1&#x3A;1"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"When the <b>pH</b> <i>exactly</i> equals an acid's pK<sub>a</sub>, the [HA] to [A<sup>-</sup>] ratio is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>exactly</i> 1:1</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Half of the acid is protonated, the other half is deprotonated.</div>"
"When there's slightly <i>more</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>weak acid [HA]</u></span>&nbsp;than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>conjugate base [A</u><sup style="""">-</sup><u>]</u></span> in a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pH"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">slightly <i>below</i> its pK<sub>a</sub></span>."	"When there's slightly <i>more</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>weak acid [HA]</u></span>&nbsp;than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>conjugate base [A</u><sup style="""">-</sup><u>]</u></span> in a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pH</span> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">slightly <i>below</i> its pK<sub>a</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- When [HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>] exactly, the pH exactly equals its pK<sub>a</sub>.<div>- When [HA] &gt; [A-], pH is below its pK<sub>a</sub>.</div><div>- When [HA] &lt; [A-], pH is above its pK<sub>a</sub>.</div></div>"
"When there's slightly <i>more</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;weak&#x20;acid&#x20;&#x5B;HA&#x5D;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[weak acid/conjugate]</span>&nbsp;than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;conjugate&#x20;base&#x20;&#x5B;A&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&lt;sup&#x20;style&#x3D;&quot;&quot;&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&lt;u&gt;&#x5D;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[weak acid/conjugate]</span> in a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pH</span> will be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">slightly <i>below</i> its pK<sub>a</sub></span>." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- When [HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>] exactly, the pH exactly equals its pK<sub>a</sub>.<div>- When [HA] &gt; [A-], pH is below its pK<sub>a</sub>.</div><div>- When [HA] &lt; [A-], pH is above its pK<sub>a</sub>.</div></div>"
"When there's slightly <i>more</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>weak acid [HA]</u></span>&nbsp;than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>conjugate base [A</u><sup style="""">-</sup><u>]</u></span> in a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pH</span> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""slightly&#x20;&lt;i&gt;below&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;its&#x20;pK&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"When there's slightly <i>more</i>&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>weak acid [HA]</u></span>&nbsp;than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>conjugate base [A</u><sup style="""">-</sup><u>]</u></span> in a <b>buffer solution</b>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pH</span> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">slightly <i>below</i> its pK<sub>a</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- When [HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>] exactly, the pH exactly equals its pK<sub>a</sub>.<div>- When [HA] &gt; [A-], pH is below its pK<sub>a</sub>.</div><div>- When [HA] &lt; [A-], pH is above its pK<sub>a</sub>.</div></div>"

<div>The triple point is where the 3 phases meet.</div><div><br></div><img src=""phasediagram.gif""></div>"
"From a phase change diagram, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>slope</u> of the line (ΔT/q)</span> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;inversely&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[directly/inversely]</span> proportional to the phase's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span>.<div><br></div><div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""><br></div>"	"From a phase change diagram, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>slope</u> of the line (ΔT/q)</span> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the phase's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span>.<div><br></div><div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <u>Slope</u> represents the amount of heat being added in each phase.<div>- <u>Heat capacity</u> (<b>C</b>=mc) is represented as the flattened line, since this is where heat is being added without resulting in a temperature change. (q = mcΔT → q/ΔT = mc → ΔT/q = 1/mc).</div></div>"
"From a phase change diagram, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;slope&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;line&#x20;&#x28;ΔT&#x2F;q&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the phase's <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;heat&#x20;capacity&#x20;&#x28;C&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.<div><br></div><div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""><br></div>"	"From a phase change diagram, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>slope</u> of the line (ΔT/q)</span> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional to the phase's <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>heat capacity (C)</b></span>.<div><br></div><div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- <u>Slope</u> represents the amount of heat being added in each phase.<div>- <u>Heat capacity</u> (<b>C</b>=mc) is represented as the flattened line, since this is where heat is being added without resulting in a temperature change. (q = mcΔT → q/ΔT = mc → ΔT/q = 1/mc).</div></div>"
"<div>In Raoult's law, which describes the vapor pressure of a compound in a mixture, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>P<sub>A</sub>°</b></span>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""vapor&#x20;pressure&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;&lt;i&gt;pure&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;solution&#x20;of&#x20;compound&#x20;A"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png"">"	"<div>In Raoult's law, which describes the vapor pressure of a compound in a mixture, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>P<sub>A</sub>°</b></span>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">vapor pressure of a <i>pure</i> solution of compound A</span>.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png"">
<hr>

<div>- <b>P<sub>A</sub></b> = vapor pressure of compound A; <b>X<sub>A</sub></b> = mole fraction of compound A in solution; <b>P<sub>A</sub>°</b> = pressure of a pure solution of compound A.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div>"
"<div>In Raoult's law, which describes the vapor pressure of a compound in a mixture, <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;P&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#xB0;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[variable]</span>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">vapor pressure of a <i>pure</i> solution of compound A</span>.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png"">"	"<div>In Raoult's law, which describes the vapor pressure of a compound in a mixture, <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>P<sub>A</sub>°</b></span>&nbsp;represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">vapor pressure of a <i>pure</i> solution of compound A</span>.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png"">

"Steps to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b><i>total</i> vapor pressure</b></span>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">mixture of two <i>different</i> compounds</span>...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Use&#x20;Raoult&#x27;s&#x20;law&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;X&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;P&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#xB0;&#x29;&#x20;to&#x20;calculate&#x20;the&#x20;vapor&#x20;pressure&#x20;&lt;i&gt;each&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;compound&#x20;is&#x20;contributing&#x20;&#x28;P&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;P&lt;sub&gt;B&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Take&#x20;the&#x20;sum&#x20;of&#x20;P&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;P&lt;sub&gt;B&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"Steps to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b><i>total</i> vapor pressure</b></span>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">mixture of two <i>different</i> compounds</span>...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Use Raoult's law (P<sub>A</sub> = X<sub>A</sub>P<sub>A</sub>°) to calculate the vapor pressure <i>each</i> compound is contributing (P<sub>A</sub> &amp; P<sub>B</sub>).</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Take the sum of P<sub>A</sub> &amp; P<sub>B</sub>.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>P<sub>A</sub></b> = vapor pressure of compound A; <b>X<sub>A</sub></b> = mole fraction of compound A in solution; <b>P<sub>A</sub>°</b> = pressure of a pure solution of compound A.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div>"
"Steps to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&lt;i&gt;total&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;vapor&#x20;pressure&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""mixture&#x20;of&#x20;two&#x20;&lt;i&gt;different&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;compounds"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Use Raoult's law (P<sub>A</sub> = X<sub>A</sub>P<sub>A</sub>°) to calculate the vapor pressure <i>each</i> compound is contributing (P<sub>A</sub> &amp; P<sub>B</sub>).</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Take the sum of P<sub>A</sub> &amp; P<sub>B</sub>.</span></div>"	"Steps to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b><i>total</i> vapor pressure</b></span>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">mixture of two <i>different</i> compounds</span>...<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Use Raoult's law (P<sub>A</sub> = X<sub>A</sub>P<sub>A</sub>°) to calculate the vapor pressure <i>each</i> compound is contributing (P<sub>A</sub> &amp; P<sub>B</sub>).</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Take the sum of P<sub>A</sub> &amp; P<sub>B</sub>.</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>P<sub>A</sub></b> = vapor pressure of compound A; <b>X<sub>A</sub></b> = mole fraction of compound A in solution; <b>P<sub>A</sub>°</b> = pressure of a pure solution of compound A.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""e801c1d58f7c022e2a484076011c7666.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Good"" data-ordinal=""1"">[good/bad]</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> Van't Hoff factors."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Good</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> Van't Hoff factors.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Good conductors are compounds that produce a <u>high number of ions in solution</u>. An increased number in the number of ions in solution would make the Van't Hoff factor (i) lager - increasing osmotic pressure.</div><div><br></div><img src=""1a3cf71e76086fb3b401ee5a4de9f559.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Good</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;high&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[low/high]</span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher]</span> Van't Hoff factors."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Good</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>high</i></span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>higher</i></span> Van't Hoff factors.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Good conductors are compounds that produce a <u>high number of ions in solution</u>. An increased number in the number of ions in solution would make the Van't Hoff factor (i) lager - increasing osmotic pressure.</div><div><br></div><img src=""1a3cf71e76086fb3b401ee5a4de9f559.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Bad"" data-ordinal=""1"">[good/bad]</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower</i></span> Van't Hoff factors."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Bad</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower</i></span> Van't Hoff factors.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Good conductors are compounds that produce a <u>high number of ions in solution</u>. An increased number in the number of ions in solution would make the Van't Hoff factor (i) lager - increasing osmotic pressure.</div><div><br></div><img src=""1a3cf71e76086fb3b401ee5a4de9f559.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Bad</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;low&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[low/high]</span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;lower&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[lower/higher]</span> Van't Hoff factors."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Bad</span> <b>conductors</b> in solution also happen to exert <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>low</i></span> <u>osmotic pressures</u>, since they have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>lower</i></span> Van't Hoff factors.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Good conductors are compounds that produce a <u>high number of ions in solution</u>. An increased number in the number of ions in solution would make the Van't Hoff factor (i) lager - increasing osmotic pressure.</div><div><br></div><img src=""1a3cf71e76086fb3b401ee5a4de9f559.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>vapor pressure</b></span>&nbsp;exerted on the surface of a liquid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">increases</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;exponentially&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[linearly/exponentially]</span> as <u>temperature</u> is <i>increased</i>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>vapor pressure</b></span>&nbsp;exerted on the surface of a liquid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">increases</span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>exponentially</i></span> as <u>temperature</u> is <i>increased</i>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-1a113e8f13d33a37a949080270ac78e9198d1994.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""05370f44800dca1ddf77c6cb9613accb.png""></div>"
"The observed <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;rate&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> over time, since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">heat is being <i>added</i></span> to the solution."	"The observed <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>rate</b></span>&nbsp;of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>exo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>increases</i></span> over time, since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">heat is being <i>added</i></span> to the solution.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>An increase in temperature always results in an increased reaction rate, according to the Arrhenius equation.</div><div><br></div><div>- <b>Endothermic</b> → reaction progressively <u>decreases</u> in rate.</div><div>- <b>Exothermic</b> → reaction progressively <u>increases</u> in rate.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7640ed558a308e456d802bd63bb0eeaadf864ac4.jpg""></div>"
"The observed <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;rate&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>&nbsp;of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span> over time, since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">heat is being <i>removed</i></span> from the solution."	"The observed <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>rate</b></span>&nbsp;of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>endo</u></span><u>thermic</u> reaction <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>decreases</i></span> over time, since <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">heat is being <i>removed</i></span> from the solution.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>A decrease in temperature always results in a decreased reaction rate, according to the Arrhenius equation.<br></div><div><br></div><div>- <b>Endothermic</b> → reaction progressively <u>decreases</u> in rate.</div><div>- <b>Exothermic</b> → reaction progressively <u>increases</u> in rate.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7640ed558a308e456d802bd63bb0eeaadf864ac4.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Hot"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cold/hot]</span> air is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <u>dense</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""cold"" data-ordinal=""1"">[cold/hot]</span> air, since its molecules are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">more spread apart</span> due to their higher kinetic energies."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Hot</span> air is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>less</i></span> <u>dense</u> than <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">cold</span> air, since its molecules are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">more spread apart</span> due to their higher kinetic energies.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

In order for hot and cold air to have the same density, there needs to be a higher volume of hot air than cold air.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Hot</span> air is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;less&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more]</span> <u>dense</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">cold</span> air, since its molecules are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""more&#x20;spread&#x20;apart"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> due to their higher kinetic energies." <div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

In order for hot and cold air to have the same density, there needs to be a higher volume of hot air than cold air.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Cold</span> air is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;more&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[less/more]</span> <u>dense</u> than <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">hot</span> air, since its molecules are <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""less&#x20;spread&#x20;apart"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> due to their lower kinetic energies." In order for hot and cold air to have the same density, there needs to be a higher volume of hot air than cold air.</div>"
"In calorimetry, the <b>specific heat (c)</b> of the surrounding solution <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> have <u>any effect</u> on whether or not the reaction can proceed - it <i>only</i> determines the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""resulting&#x20;temperature&#x20;change"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> <i>after</i> the reaction."	"In calorimetry, the <b>specific heat (c)</b> of the surrounding solution <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does not</i></span> have <u>any effect</u> on whether or not the reaction can proceed - it <i>only</i> determines the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">resulting temperature change</span> <i>after</i> the reaction.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- q = mcΔT.</div><div>- Calorimeters measure the amount of heat evolved in a reaction by the resulting temperature change in the water. There is <u>no rule</u> that dictates what the specific heat of the solution must be!</div><div><br></div><img src=""5047b4653a4f636b2de270dee1eb0d36.jpg""></div>"
"In the<b> equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b>, the <u>solvent</u> (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>O) is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""always&#x20;&lt;i&gt;excluded&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[included/excluded]</span> - it <i>doesn't</i> meaningfully participate in the reaction."	"In the<b> equilibrium constant (K<sub>eq</sub>)</b>, the <u>solvent</u> (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>O) is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">always <i>excluded</i></span> - it <i>doesn't</i> meaningfully participate in the reaction.

</div>"
