<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;initial&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;b&gt;&#x20;stock&#x20;solution&#x27;s&#x20;concentration&#x20;&#x5B;C&lt;sub&gt;initial&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x5D;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> <i>before</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;dilution&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[C<sub>inintial</sub>] = [C<sub>final</sub>](V<sub>f</sub>/V<sub>i</sub>)</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>initial</i><b> stock solution's concentration [C<sub>initial</sub>]</b></span> <i>before</i> a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>dilution</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">[C<sub>inintial</sub>] = [C<sub>final</sub>](V<sub>f</sub>/V<sub>i</sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>[C<sub>initial</sub>]</b> = compound's initial concentration; <b>[C<sub>final</sub>]</b> = compound's final concentration; <b>(V<sub>f</sub>/V<sub>i</sub>)</b> = factor by which the volume was increased.</div><div><br></div>- Ex: you have a 0.1 mL stock solution and dilute it to 25 mL ((25 mL) / (0.1 mL) → 250x volume increase)...<div><br></div><div>[C<sub>initial</sub>] = [C<sub>final</sub>] ((25mL) / (0.1 mL)). → [Cinitial] = [C<sub>final</sub>](250).</div></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>initial</i><b> stock solution's concentration [C<sub>initial</sub>]</b></span> <i>before</i> a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>dilution</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x5B;C&lt;sub&gt;inintial&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x5D;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x5B;C&lt;sub&gt;final&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x5D;&#x28;V&lt;sub&gt;f&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x2F;V&lt;sub&gt;i&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>initial</i><b> stock solution's concentration [C<sub>initial</sub>]</b></span> <i>before</i> a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>dilution</u></span>:</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">[C<sub>inintial</sub>] = [C<sub>final</sub>](V<sub>f</sub>/V<sub>i</sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>[C<sub>initial</sub>]</b> = compound's initial concentration; <b>[C<sub>final</sub>]</b> = compound's final concentration; <b>(V<sub>f</sub>/V<sub>i</sub>)</b> = factor by which the volume was increased.</div><div><br></div>- Ex: you have a 0.1 mL stock solution and dilute it to 25 mL ((25 mL) / (0.1 mL) → 250x volume increase)...<div><br></div><div>[C<sub>initial</sub>] = [C<sub>final</sub>] ((25mL) / (0.1 mL)). → [Cinitial] = [C<sub>final</sub>](250).</div></div>"
"For a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i>&nbsp;base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;&amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a2</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;second&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;u&gt;buffer&#x20;region&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;&#x5B;HA&#x5D;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x5B;A&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x5D;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span>."	"For a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i>&nbsp;base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;&amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a2</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>second&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u>&nbsp;([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the first proton is the only proton being given away to generate the buffer.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"For a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i>&nbsp;base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;&amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pK&lt;sub&gt;a2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[pK<sub>a1</sub>/pK<sub>a2</sub>]</span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>second&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u>&nbsp;([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>."	"For a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i>&nbsp;base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;&amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a2</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>second&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u>&nbsp;([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the first proton is the only proton being given away to generate the buffer.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"For a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""diprotic&#x20;acid"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;conjugate&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;base&#x20;&#x28;i&#x2E;e&#x2E;&#x20;H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;CO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;HCO&lt;sub&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a2</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>second&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u>&nbsp;([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>."	"For a&nbsp;<b>buffer solution</b>&nbsp;of a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">diprotic acid</span> &amp; its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>conjugate</i>&nbsp;base (i.e. H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;&amp; HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>)</span>, its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a2</sub></span> is the pK<sub>a</sub>&nbsp;&amp; pH of its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>second&nbsp;</i><u>buffer region</u>&nbsp;([HA] = [A<sup>-</sup>])</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...since the first proton is the only proton being given away to generate the buffer.&nbsp;</div><div>- Since H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;is diprotic, it has 2 equivalence points. In order for a buffer to exist between H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>&nbsp;and HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup>, the only proton that's being given away its its first proton (corresponding to its pK<sub>a1</sub>, as opposed to its pK<sub>a2</sub>).&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e14710e672aee227a6049ad622ecd98e33e80e34.jpg""></div>"
"In a titration, the <b>equivalence point</b> is when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""N&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;V&lt;sub&gt;A&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;= <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""N&lt;sub&gt;B&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;V&lt;sub&gt;B&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"In a titration, the <b>equivalence point</b> is when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">N<sub>A</sub>V<sub>A</sub></span>&nbsp;= <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">N<sub>B</sub>V<sub>B</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<img src=""paste-87926758de6d56b2202dd60c7c4161821b68c59e.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Solids</b> are <i>typically</i> held together by strong <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;inter&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><u>molecular</u> forces."	"<b>Solids</b> are <i>typically</i> held together by strong <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>inter</u></span><u>molecular</u> forces.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Ex: NaCl is a solid held together by ionic bonds (i.e. <u>inter</u>molecular forces).</div><div><br></div><img src=""34f37c271c720951d3e80fbde6c6680d.png""></div>"
"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;b&gt;energy&#x20;density&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;of&#x20;a&#x20;battery"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-799a480b686ad5deda29ffb6d19100c01a312c8f.jpg""></span><br>"	"<div>Equation for the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><b>energy density</b></u>&nbsp;of a battery</span>:</div><div><br></div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><img src=""paste-799a480b686ad5deda29ffb6d19100c01a312c8f.jpg""></span><br>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<b>Energy</b> (Watts); <b>mass</b> (kg).</div>"
"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;molar&#x20;concentration&#x20;&#x28;M&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of an ion after <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;mixing&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;2&#x20;compounds&#x20;&#x28;containing&#x20;that&#x20;ion&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;equal&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[equal/different]</span> <u>volumes</u> together:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Find the <u>normality</u> (Molarity(# of&nbsp; equivalences)) of compound 1.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Find the <u>normality</u> (Molarity(# of equivalences)) of compound 2.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Take the <i>average</i> of the two normalities.</span></div>"	"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>molar concentration (M)</b></span> of an ion after <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>mixing</i> 2 compounds (containing that ion)</span> of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <u>volumes</u> together:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Find the <u>normality</u> (Molarity(# of&nbsp; equivalences)) of compound 1.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Find the <u>normality</u> (Molarity(# of equivalences)) of compound 2.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Take the <i>average</i> of the two normalities.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>N</b> = M(n) → Normality = Molarity(# of equivalences)</div>- Normality is like molarity, except it takes the number of equivalences into account.<div><br></div><div>Ex: What is the molar concentration of Na<sup>+</sup> in a solution of 10 mL of 0.01 M NaHCO<sub>3</sub> &amp; 10 mL of 0.01 M Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>?</div><div><br></div><div>1. N = (0.01)(1) → 0.01</div><div>2. N = 0.01(2) → 0.02</div><div>3. (0.01 + 0.02) / 2 → 0.015</div></div>"
"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>molar concentration (M)</b></span> of an ion after <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>mixing</i> 2 compounds (containing that ion)</span> of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <u>volumes</u> together:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Find&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;normality&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;Molarity&#x28;&#x23;&#x20;of&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x20;equivalences&#x29;&#x29;&#x20;of&#x20;compound&#x20;1&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Find&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;u&gt;normality&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;&#x28;Molarity&#x28;&#x23;&#x20;of&#x20;equivalences&#x29;&#x29;&#x20;of&#x20;compound&#x20;2&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Take&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;average&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;two&#x20;normalities&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"Steps to finding the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>molar concentration (M)</b></span> of an ion after <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>mixing</i> 2 compounds (containing that ion)</span> of <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>equal</i></span> <u>volumes</u> together:<div><br></div><div>1. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Find the <u>normality</u> (Molarity(# of&nbsp; equivalences)) of compound 1.</span></div><div>2. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Find the <u>normality</u> (Molarity(# of equivalences)) of compound 2.</span></div><div>3. <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Take the <i>average</i> of the two normalities.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>N</b> = M(n) → Normality = Molarity(# of equivalences)</div>- Normality is like molarity, except it takes the number of equivalences into account.<div><br></div><div>Ex: What is the molar concentration of Na<sup>+</sup> in a solution of 10 mL of 0.01 M NaHCO<sub>3</sub> &amp; 10 mL of 0.01 M Na<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>?</div><div><br></div><div>1. N = (0.01)(1) → 0.01</div><div>2. N = 0.01(2) → 0.02</div><div>3. (0.01 + 0.02) / 2 → 0.015</div></div>"
"The <u>reactivity</u> of a metal is <i>primarily</i> determined by its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ionization&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"The <u>reactivity</u> of a metal is <i>primarily</i> determined by its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ionization energy</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Metals with a low ionization energy are very reactive (easily lose electrons).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7f38de24231072df639df5147db58f59a1f4dff1.jpg""></div>"
"<b>Phosphorus (P)</b> is <i>most</i> stable when it has <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""4"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> bonds with a total of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""8"" data-ordinal=""1"">[#]</span> valence electrons - it <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""follows"" data-ordinal=""1"">[follows/violates]</span> the octet rule."	"<b>Phosphorus (P)</b> is <i>most</i> stable when it has <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">4</span> bonds with a total of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">8</span> valence electrons - it <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">follows</span> the octet rule.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Follows the octet rule.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-1cfa081f961f8c6acd9f9594c9e2dca213f670c6.jpg""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;acid&#x20;dissociation&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> is <i>also</i> called the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ionization constant (K<sub>a</sub>)</b></span>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>acid dissociation constant (K<sub>a</sub>)</b></span> is <i>also</i> called the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ionization constant (K<sub>a</sub>)</b></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This will only change with a change in temperature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""d9ffa396a3d411faa300076a56e653ae.png""></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>acid dissociation constant (K<sub>a</sub>)</b></span> is <i>also</i> called the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ionization&#x20;constant&#x20;&#x28;K&lt;sub&gt;a&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"The <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>acid dissociation constant (K<sub>a</sub>)</b></span> is <i>also</i> called the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ionization constant (K<sub>a</sub>)</b></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This will only change with a change in temperature.</div><div><br></div><img src=""d9ffa396a3d411faa300076a56e653ae.png""></div>"
"Write the expression for the <b>solubility product constant (K<sub>sp</sub>)</b>, assuming that M(X)<sub>2</sub> = S mol/L.<div><br></div><div>K<sub>sp</sub> = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x5B;S&#x5D;&#x5B;2S&#x5D;&lt;sup&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Write the expression for the <b>solubility product constant (K<sub>sp</sub>)</b>, assuming that M(X)<sub>2</sub> = S mol/L.<div><br></div><div>K<sub>sp</sub> = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S][2S]<sup>2</sup></span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- For every 1 mole of M, you have 2 moles of X. It therefore has twice as large of a K<sub>sp</sub> as an ion (that's where the coefficient comes in).</div><div><br></div><img src=""a8d2a8b39de3a1d0836e4fe577cd5f1b.png""></div>"
"Write the expression for the <b>solubility product constant (K<sub>sp</sub>)</b>, assuming that M(X)&nbsp;= S mol/L.<div><br></div><div>K<sub>sp</sub> = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x5B;S&#x5D;&#x5B;S&#x5D;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Write the expression for the <b>solubility product constant (K<sub>sp</sub>)</b>, assuming that M(X)&nbsp;= S mol/L.<div><br></div><div>K<sub>sp</sub> = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S][S]</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- For every 1 mole of M, you have 1 moles of X. It therefore has the same K<sub>sp</sub> as an ion (that's where the coefficient comes in).</div><div><br></div><img src=""a8d2a8b39de3a1d0836e4fe577cd5f1b.png""></div>"
"Write the expression for the <b>solubility product constant (K<sub>sp</sub>)</b>, assuming that M(X)<sub>3</sub> = S mol/L.<div><br></div><div>K<sub>sp</sub> = <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&#x5B;S&#x5D;&#x5B;3S&#x5D;&lt;sup&gt;3&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Write the expression for the <b>solubility product constant (K<sub>sp</sub>)</b>, assuming that M(X)<sub>3</sub> = S mol/L.<div><br></div><div>K<sub>sp</sub> = <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">[S][3S]<sup>3</sup></span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- For every 1 mole of M, you have 3 moles of X. It therefore has three times as large of a K<sub>sp</sub> as an ion (that's where the coefficient comes in).</div><div><br></div><img src=""a8d2a8b39de3a1d0836e4fe577cd5f1b.png""></div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;weak&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smaller</i>&nbsp;than</span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA]."	"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>weak</i></span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smaller</i>&nbsp;than</span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA].
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Shows that it only partially ionizes.</div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>weak</i></span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;smaller&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;than"" data-ordinal=""2"">[smaller/larger/equal]</span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA]."	"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>weak</i></span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>smaller</i>&nbsp;than</span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA].
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Shows that it only partially ionizes.</div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;strong&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[weak/strong]</span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>equal to</i></span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA]."	"When a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i></span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>equal to</i></span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA].
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Shows that it completely ionizes.</div>"
"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i></span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;equal&#x20;to&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[smaller/larger/equal]</span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA]."	"When a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>strong</i></span> acid is added to a solution, the concentration of H<sup>+</sup> ([H<sup>+</sup>]) is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>equal to</i></span> the <i>initial</i> concentration of the acid [HA].
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Shows that it completely ionizes.</div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Basic&#x20;anions&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;&#x28;NO&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x2C;&#x20;CN&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;are usually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>insoluble</i></span> in <u>water</u> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>soluble</i></span>&nbsp;in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>acidic solutions</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Basic anions</b> (NO<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup>, CN<sup>-</sup>)</span>&nbsp;are usually <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>insoluble</i></span> in <u>water</u> &amp; <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>soluble</i></span>&nbsp;in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>acidic solutions</u></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- These are the anions that form from weak acids (HF → H<sup>+</sup> &amp; F<sup>-</sup>).</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-56133fe1f90e39cee69ffd49992cb435e11a0e05.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Basic anions</b> (NO<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup>, CN<sup>-</sup>)</span>&nbsp;are usually <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;insoluble&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[soluble/insoluble]</span> in <u>water</u> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;soluble&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[soluble/insoluble]</span>&nbsp;in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>acidic solutions</u></span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Basic anions</b> (NO<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup>, CN<sup>-</sup>)</span>&nbsp;are usually <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>insoluble</i></span> in <u>water</u> &amp; <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>soluble</i></span>&nbsp;in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>acidic solutions</u></span>.
<hr>

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Basic anions</b> (NO<sub>2</sub><sup>-</sup>, CN<sup>-</sup>, etc)</span> are the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">anionic</span> components of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>weak acids</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>These are mostly insoluble in water &amp; soluble in acidic solutions.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-56133fe1f90e39cee69ffd49992cb435e11a0e05.jpg""></div>"
"In fluid <b>barometers</b>, when the P<sub>atm</sub> is <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[lower/higher/equal]</span> <i>outside</i> the tube than the total pressure (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty air</sub>)&nbsp;<i>inside</i> the tube, the height of the fluid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">rises</span> to reach equilibrium."	"In fluid <b>barometers</b>, when the P<sub>atm</sub> is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <i>outside</i> the tube than the total pressure (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty air</sub>)&nbsp;<i>inside</i> the tube, the height of the fluid <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">rises</span> to reach equilibrium.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Think of this similar to how hot air balloons work. When the density of the air is low (low pressure) in a hot air balloon, it rises.</div><div><br></div><div>- P<sub>atm</sub>&nbsp;is higher outside than the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub>&nbsp;+ P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid rises.</div><div>- P<sub>atm</sub>&nbsp;is lower outside than the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub>&nbsp;+ P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid drops in height.</div><div>- P<sub>atm</sub>&nbsp;is equal to the total pressure inside (P<sub>liquid</sub>&nbsp;+ P<sub>empty air</sub>) → fluid reaches equilibrium.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a4bda460f722020c41fa96ea884c6c48dac98e15.jpg""></div></div>"
"In fluid <b>barometers</b>, when the P<sub>atm</sub> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <i>outside</i> the tube than the total pressure (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty air</sub>)&nbsp;<i>inside</i> the tube, the height of the fluid <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""rises"" data-ordinal=""2"">[rises/drops]</span> to reach equilibrium."	"In fluid <b>barometers</b>, when the P<sub>atm</sub> is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>higher</i></span> <i>outside</i> the tube than the total pressure (P<sub>liquid</sub> + P<sub>empty air</sub>)&nbsp;<i>inside</i> the tube, the height of the fluid <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">rises</span> to reach equilibrium.

"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Graham's law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;larger&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;gas&#x20;particles&#x20;diffuse&#x2F;effuse&#x20;at&#x20;&lt;i&gt;slower&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;rates"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;than <u>smaller</u> gas particles." <hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>R</b>&nbsp;=&nbsp;<u>rates</u>&nbsp;of diffusion/effusion (gas 1 &amp; gas 2);&nbsp;<b>M</b>&nbsp;=&nbsp;<u>molar mass</u>&nbsp;of each gas.<br></div><div><br></div><img src=""9160ba5b4e25c9234897de077a10a373.png""><div><div><br></div><div><img src=""3625c272f92029618e664e1dc32201b7.png""></div></div></div>"
"When the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""substrate&#x20;concentration"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> is <i>increased</i> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction, the reaction's <u>rate</u> increases <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>exponentially</i></span>."	"When the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">substrate concentration</span> is <i>increased</i> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>second</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction, the reaction's <u>rate</u> increases <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>exponentially</i></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>...assuming that the <u>substrate concentration is not at a saturating level</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7eaaff926e51dc2dc6615d029a9cfb2a2b0cbc5c.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"When the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""substrate&#x20;concentration"" data-ordinal=""3"">[...]</span> is <i>increased</i> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>first</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction (non-saturating conditions), the reaction's <u>rate</u> increases <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>linearly</i></span>."	"When the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3"">substrate concentration</span> is <i>increased</i> in a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>first</b></span> <b>order</b> reaction (non-saturating conditions), the reaction's <u>rate</u> increases <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>linearly</i></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is assuming that the <u>substrate concentration is not at a saturating level</u>.</div><div>- Don't confuse this with how product concentrations eventually level off (in second image). This is describing the [S] vs. the rate, NOT [S] vs. [product].</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-37a0aceded3adedfa0dc482d1cff173eec17320d.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-59b7ea7adf869c969bb8d91fb07c0484ce9259d1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>Should you ever <i>estimate</i> the <b>pH</b> of a <u>buffer solution</u> when you're <i>given</i> the concentrations &amp; pK<sub>a</sub>?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""NO&#x21;&#x20;Always&#x20;plug&#x20;it&#x20;into&#x20;the&#x20;Hendersson&#x20;Hasselbalch&#x20;equation&#x20;to&#x20;find&#x20;the&#x20;exact&#x20;pH&#x2E;&#x20;Don&#x27;t&#x20;estimate&#x21;&lt;div&gt;&lt;br&gt;&lt;&#x2F;div&gt;&lt;img&#x20;src&#x3D;&quot;paste&#x2D;ae336d8f5b93c4a4d0356e8b40891f237f2d87ea&#x2E;jpg&quot;&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"<div>Should you ever <i>estimate</i> the <b>pH</b> of a <u>buffer solution</u> when you're <i>given</i> the concentrations &amp; pK<sub>a</sub>?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">NO! Always plug it into the Hendersson Hasselbalch equation to find the exact pH. Don't estimate!<div><br></div><img src=""paste-ae336d8f5b93c4a4d0356e8b40891f237f2d87ea.jpg""></span><br></div>

</div>"
"Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ideal gases</b></span> in terms of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">number of <u>molecules</u></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""PV&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;Nk&lt;sub&gt;b&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;T"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ideal gases</b></span> in terms of the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">number of <u>molecules</u></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">PV = Nk<sub>b</sub>T</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>P</b> = pressure; <b>V</b> = volume; <b>N</b> = number of molecules (not moles); <b>k<sub>b</sub></b> = Boltzmann's constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup>); <b>T</b> = temperature (K).<div>- This equation is derived from Boltzmann's constant itself.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5fcae094778b5567b7a9b6ecd6b033100ecd8734.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ideal&#x20;gases&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> in terms of the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""number&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;molecules&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">PV = Nk<sub>b</sub>T</span><br></div>"	"Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>ideal gases</b></span> in terms of the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">number of <u>molecules</u></span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">PV = Nk<sub>b</sub>T</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

-&nbsp;<b>P</b> = pressure; <b>V</b> = volume; <b>N</b> = number of molecules (not moles); <b>k<sub>b</sub></b> = Boltzmann's constant (1.4 x 10<sup>-23</sup>); <b>T</b> = temperature (K).<div>- This equation is derived from Boltzmann's constant itself.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-5fcae094778b5567b7a9b6ecd6b033100ecd8734.jpg""><br></div></div>"

Releases heat (also has a large increase in entropy).</div>"
"Conversion between <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ppm</b></span>&nbsp;and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>molarity</u> (M)</span>:<div><br></div><div>1 <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;ppm&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>&nbsp;= 1 gram in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">10<sup>6</sup> grams of water</span>.</div>"	"Conversion between <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>ppm</b></span>&nbsp;and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>molarity</u> (M)</span>:<div><br></div><div>1 <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>ppm</b></span>&nbsp;= 1 gram in <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">10<sup>6</sup> grams of water</span>.</div>

"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;Ionic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[polar cov./ionic]</span> <b>bonds</b> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>larger</i></span> <u>atomic radii</u> have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>greater</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">electric <u>potential energies</u></span>, since they're oppositely charged particles spaced far apart."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Ionic</b></span> <b>bonds</b> with <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>larger</i></span> <u>atomic radii</u> have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>greater</i></span> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""4"">electric <u>potential energies</u></span>, since they're oppositely charged particles spaced far apart.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is the same as how oppositely charged compounds have greater potential energy when they're far apart.</div><div><br></div><div>- Larger atomic radii → greater electric potential energy.</div><div>- Small atomic radii → smaller electric potential energy.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0941b7e32500a4fa913c14f52e44f9c6f96995ea.jpg""></div>"

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is the same as how oppositely charged compounds have greater potential energy when they're far apart.</div><div><br></div><div>- Larger atomic radii → greater electric potential energy.</div><div>- Small atomic radii → smaller electric potential energy.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-0941b7e32500a4fa913c14f52e44f9c6f96995ea.jpg""></div>"
"Avogadro's number is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">6 x 10<sup style="""">23</sup></span> - representing the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""molecules&#x20;in&#x20;1&#x20;mole"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> of something."	"Avogadro's number is <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">6 x 10<sup style="""">23</sup></span> - representing the number of <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">molecules in 1 mole</span> of something.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>osmolarity (O)</b></span>, which represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>amount</i> of <u>solute</u> in a solution</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""O&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;iM"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"Equation for <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>osmolarity (O)</b></span>, which represents the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>amount</i> of <u>solute</u> in a solution</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">O = iM</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>O</b> = osmolarity; <b>i</b> = Van't Hoff factor (number of separate particles it breaks into in solution); <b>M</b> = molarity (mol/L).</div>- Don't confuse this with osmotic pressure!<div>- A solution with a high osmolarity has a high amount of solute relative to the amount of the solvent.</div></div>"
"Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;osmolarity&#x20;&#x28;O&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>, which represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;amount&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;solute&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;in&#x20;a&#x20;solution"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O = iM</span></div>"	"Equation for <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>osmolarity (O)</b></span>, which represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>amount</i> of <u>solute</u> in a solution</span>:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">O = iM</span></div>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>O</b> = osmolarity; <b>i</b> = Van't Hoff factor (number of separate particles it breaks into in solution); <b>M</b> = molarity (mol/L).</div>- Don't confuse this with osmotic pressure!<div>- A solution with a high osmolarity has a high amount of solute relative to the amount of the solvent.</div></div>"
"An electrochemical cell that utilizes a <b>salt bridge</b> is a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;galvanic&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[galvanic/electrolytic]</span> cell."	"An electrochemical cell that utilizes a <b>salt bridge</b> is a(n) <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>galvanic</u></span> cell.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

- Electrolytic cells have no need for salt bridges.<div><br></div><div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Anode</b>&nbsp;side slowly accumulates a&nbsp;<u>positive</u>&nbsp;charge (since it's losing electrons) - neutralized by the Cl<sup>-</sup>&nbsp;in the KCl ions in the salt bridge.</div><div>-&nbsp;<b>Cathode</b>&nbsp;side slowly accumulates a&nbsp;<u>negative</u>&nbsp;charge (since it's gaining electrons) - neutralized by the K<sup>+</sup>&nbsp;in the KCl ions in the salt bridge.<br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""098cf7ba35a1957a943cf3c8c111ff96.png""></div></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""9f5660936ee87c6cdbdff58c42d55525.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>What's the <i>difference</i> between these two equations?</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q = mL</b></span>&nbsp;→ heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""it&#x20;&lt;i&gt;is&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;middle&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;&lt;u&gt;phase&#x20;change&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q = mcΔT</b></span> → heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""it&#x27;s&#x20;&lt;i&gt;NOT&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;in&#x20;the&#x20;middle&#x20;of&#x20;a&#x20;&lt;u&gt;phase&#x20;change&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""><br></div>"	"<div>What's the <i>difference</i> between these two equations?</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q = mL</b></span>&nbsp;→ heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">it <i>is</i> in the middle of a <u>phase change</u></span>.</div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q = mcΔT</b></span> → heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">it's <i>NOT</i> in the middle of a <u>phase change</u></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""><br></div>

"The <b>heat of combustion</b> of a compound <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;does&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[does/does not]</span> indicate its stability."	"The <b>heat of combustion</b> of a compound <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>does</i></span> indicate its stability.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- <b>Heat of combustion</b>: the amount of heat given off when the compound is burned.</div><div><br></div>- More stable → releases less energy upon combustion.&nbsp;<div>- Less stable → releases more energy upon combustion.</div></div>"

"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>net</i> ΔG°</b> for two <u>half reactions</u></span>&nbsp;written in the <i>forward</i> direction:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""ΔG&#xB0;&lt;sub&gt;net&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x3D;&#x20;&#x28;ΔG&#xB0;&lt;sub&gt;1&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&#x20;&#x2B;&#x20;&#x28;ΔG&#xB0;&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span><br></div>"	"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>net</i> ΔG°</b> for two <u>half reactions</u></span>&nbsp;written in the <i>forward</i> direction:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">ΔG°<sub>net </sub>= (ΔG°<sub>1</sub>) + (ΔG°<sub>2</sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Recognize that a -ΔG indicates a release of energy, while a +ΔG indicates the consumption of energy. If you're giving off 146 J but you're consuming 30.5, then you must only be giving off a net of 115 kJ.</div><div>- Ex: (-146 kJ/mol) + (30.5) = -115.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-cdc3ef0d433fedf5b250ea4c7c420755c47f5568.jpg""><div><img src=""paste-d86969ee4248d452a2a378310c564301c9e53b73.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;&lt;i&gt;net&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;ΔG&#xB0;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;for&#x20;two&#x20;&lt;u&gt;half&#x20;reactions&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;written in the <i>forward</i> direction:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ΔG°<sub>net </sub>= (ΔG°<sub>1</sub>) + (ΔG°<sub>2</sub>)</span><br></div>"	"Equation to calculate the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b><i>net</i> ΔG°</b> for two <u>half reactions</u></span>&nbsp;written in the <i>forward</i> direction:<div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">ΔG°<sub>net </sub>= (ΔG°<sub>1</sub>) + (ΔG°<sub>2</sub>)</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Recognize that a -ΔG indicates a release of energy, while a +ΔG indicates the consumption of energy. If you're giving off 146 J but you're consuming 30.5, then you must only be giving off a net of 115 kJ.</div><div>- Ex: (-146 kJ/mol) + (30.5) = -115.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-cdc3ef0d433fedf5b250ea4c7c420755c47f5568.jpg""><div><img src=""paste-d86969ee4248d452a2a378310c564301c9e53b73.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"In a <u>lead-acid battery</u>, the solution becomes more <i>dilute</i>&nbsp;due to a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""doule&#x2D;displacement"" data-ordinal=""1"">[type]</span> reaction between <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""PbO&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;SO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[reactants]</span> - forming <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""PbSO&lt;sub&gt;4&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&amp;amp&#x3B;&#x20;H&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;O"" data-ordinal=""1"">[products]</span>."	"In a <u>lead-acid battery</u>, the solution becomes more <i>dilute</i>&nbsp;due to a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">doule-displacement</span> reaction between <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">PbO<sub>2</sub> &amp; H<sub>2</sub>SO<sub>4</sub></span> - forming <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">PbSO<sub>4</sub> &amp; H<sub>2</sub>O</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-1c5256a82d39ea795067eb7cc0109a3b02759420.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>How could you calculate <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;theoretical&#x20;yield&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>Substitute</i> the <u>moles of the limiting reagent</u> <i>in for</i> the <u>moles of the product</u>.</span><br></div>"	"<div>How could you calculate <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>theoretical yield</b></span>?<br></div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>Substitute</i> the <u>moles of the limiting reagent</u> <i>in for</i> the <u>moles of the product</u>.</span><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Use the moles in the product's MW = g/mol expression and solve for grams.</div><div><br></div><img src=""601a4138a32a8efde1b9440f821a2ddc.png""></div>"
"<div>What's the <i>difference</i> between these two equations?</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;q&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;mL&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[equation]</span>&nbsp;→ heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">it <i>is</i> in the middle of a <u>phase change</u></span>.</div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;q&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;mcΔT&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[equation]</span> → heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">it's <i>NOT</i> in the middle of a <u>phase change</u></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""><br></div>"	"<div>What's the <i>difference</i> between these two equations?</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q = mL</b></span>&nbsp;→ heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">it <i>is</i> in the middle of a <u>phase change</u></span>.</div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>q = mcΔT</b></span> → heat gained/released by a substance when <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">it's <i>NOT</i> in the middle of a <u>phase change</u></span>.</div><div><br></div><img src=""ec50cd0fe14e2e597d41dc3944277d4f.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""6b006434bcd732e8b258d9e1fdec5b6d.png""><br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>q = mL describes a phase change since it takes into account the latent heat (L).</div><div><br></div><img src=""2e30e0c68e6bec5bb2c5675e135663dc.png""></div>"
"In acid-base titrations, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acidic</i> <b>indicators (HIn)</b></span> will <u>change colors</u> once they're <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;deprotonated&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;to&#x20;In&lt;sup&gt;&#x2D;&lt;&#x2F;sup&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;- signifying that the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pK&lt;sub&gt;a&#x28;indicator&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x3D;&#x20;pH&lt;sub&gt;&#x28;equivalence&#x20;point&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In acid-base titrations, <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>acidic</i> <b>indicators (HIn)</b></span> will <u>change colors</u> once they're <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>deprotonated</i> to In<sup>-</sup></span>&nbsp;- signifying that the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a(indicator)</sub>&nbsp;= pH<sub>(equivalence point)</sub></span>.

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><img src=""paste-642e02422cf9ec84c0b43b9c95c49d4145f72e75.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-e22cddc3324604a8785b3f6c8abfc03fa83ba97a.jpg""></div>"
"In acid-base titrations, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>endpoint</b></span> is reached when the pH indicator reaches its full color, which happens when the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pK&lt;sub&gt;a&#x28;indicator&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x20;&#x3D;&#x20;pH&lt;sub&gt;equivalence&#x20;point&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"In acid-base titrations, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>endpoint</b></span> is reached when the pH indicator reaches its full color, which happens when the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pK<sub>a(indicator)</sub> = pH<sub>equivalence point</sub></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Indicators should have pK<sub>a</sub> values close to the <b>equivalence point's</b> pH.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a91dc8b3cbdf8b7e6bbef5a89fbbbc4d769eebc9.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>When the <u>pressure</u> of a <b>gas</b> is significantly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increased</i></span>&nbsp;&amp; the&nbsp;<u>volume</u> is significantly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreased</i></span>...</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Gases&#x20;experience&#x20;&lt;i&gt;attractive&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;intermolecular&#x20;forces&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Volume&#x20;of&#x20;the&#x20;individual&#x20;gas&#x20;molecules&#x20;becomes&#x20;more&#x20;significant&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span></div>"	"<div>When the <u>pressure</u> of a <b>gas</b> is significantly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increased</i></span>&nbsp;&amp; the&nbsp;<u>volume</u> is significantly <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreased</i></span>...</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Gases experience <i>attractive</i> intermolecular forces.</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">Volume of the individual gas molecules becomes more significant.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>a</b> is the <u>attraction coefficient</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""b3c4aea491e23b2221ce7e4dc88018a6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""699c01e9dc507b4979fb5bdd8c44a397.png""><br></div></div>"
"<div>When the <u>pressure</u> of a <b>gas</b> is significantly <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;increased&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increased/decreased]</span>&nbsp;&amp; the&nbsp;<u>volume</u> is significantly <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;decreased&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increased/decreased]</span>...</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Gases experience <i>attractive</i> intermolecular forces.</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Volume of the individual gas molecules becomes more significant.</span></div>"	"<div>When the <u>pressure</u> of a <b>gas</b> is significantly <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>increased</i></span>&nbsp;&amp; the&nbsp;<u>volume</u> is significantly <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>decreased</i></span>...</div><div><br></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Gases experience <i>attractive</i> intermolecular forces.</span></div><div>- <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">Volume of the individual gas molecules becomes more significant.</span></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div><b>a</b> is the <u>attraction coefficient</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""b3c4aea491e23b2221ce7e4dc88018a6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""699c01e9dc507b4979fb5bdd8c44a397.png""><br></div></div>"
"When the <u>pressure</u> of a <b><i>real</i> gas</b> is <i>doubled</i>, its <u>volume</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""slightly&#x20;&lt;i&gt;above&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;half&#x20;of&#x20;its&#x20;original"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> volume."	"When the <u>pressure</u> of a <b><i>real</i> gas</b> is <i>doubled</i>, its <u>volume</u> will be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">slightly <i>above</i> half of its original</span> volume.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>This is because the volume of the individual gas molecules becomes more significant - resisting further compression.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""b3c4aea491e23b2221ce7e4dc88018a6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""699c01e9dc507b4979fb5bdd8c44a397.png""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Charles' law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>directly</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressure&#x20;is&#x20;constant"" data-ordinal=""4"">[condition]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Charles' law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>temperature</u></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>directly</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4"">pressure is constant</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>PV = nRT → V = T (directly proportional) since the other variables are held constant.</div><div>- <b>Isobaric process</b> in thermodynamics (i.e. pressure is constant...no changes to internal energy U=Q-W).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""fcc31c63994906d0c57a292eafc989c6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""9a8d34360113b555042092dc38e5dd40.png""><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-a52a9e49e53dd2a5520a1aeb2e8606147ad76db3.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"According to <b>Charles' Law</b>, an&nbsp;<i>increase</i> in <u>temperature</u> <i>increases</i> <u>volume</u> by giving gas particles&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater&nbsp;</i>kinetic energy</span> - leading to more collisions &amp; expansion of the gas - when <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""pressure&#x20;is&#x20;constant"" data-ordinal=""2"">[condition]</span>."	"According to <b>Charles' Law</b>, an&nbsp;<i>increase</i> in <u>temperature</u> <i>increases</i> <u>volume</u> by giving gas particles&nbsp;<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>greater&nbsp;</i>kinetic energy</span> - leading to more collisions &amp; expansion of the gas - when <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">pressure is constant</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Normally a greater number of collisions results in increased pressure, but Charles' Law works under the assumption that <u>pressure is held constant</u>.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""fcc31c63994906d0c57a292eafc989c6.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-53ab44ebc23161cba67f830bc3d12fb765c04935.jpg""></div>"
"Two atoms are said to be <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;isoelectronic&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span> when they have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">the&nbsp;<i>same</i> <u>number of electrons</u></span>."	"Two atoms are said to be <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isoelectronic</b></span> when they have <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">the&nbsp;<i>same</i> <u>number of electrons</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Mg<sup>2+</sup> and Neon are isoelectronic for example, but Helium and Neon aren't (different number of electrons).</div><div><br></div><img src=""MCAT+Periodic+Table.png""></div>"
"Two atoms are said to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isoelectronic</b></span> when they have <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""the&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&lt;i&gt;same&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&#x20;&lt;u&gt;number&#x20;of&#x20;electrons&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>."	"Two atoms are said to be <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>isoelectronic</b></span> when they have <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">the&nbsp;<i>same</i> <u>number of electrons</u></span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Mg<sup>2+</sup> and Neon are isoelectronic for example, but Helium and Neon aren't (different number of electrons).</div><div><br></div><img src=""MCAT+Periodic+Table.png""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Boyle's law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressure</u></span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> under a <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""constant&#x20;temperature"" data-ordinal=""4"">[condition]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>Boyle's law</b></span> says that <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>pressure</u></span> and <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>volume</u></span> are <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><i>inversely</i></span> proportional <i>only</i> under a <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""4"">constant temperature</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>PV = nRT → nRT simplifies to some constant when temperature is constant, so the only things that can change are pressure and volume.</div><div>- <b>Isothermal process</b> in thermodynamics (since temperature is constant... Q=W).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""8ff1ed999b992347379c2a92b39f5a0b.png""><br></div><div><br></div><img src=""68eb2304f68e45d0d86c110de3af3baa.png""></div>"
"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;kinetic&#x20;energy&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;&#x20;needed&#x20;for&#x20;an&#x20;&lt;i&gt;effective&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;collision"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;is known as the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>activation energy</b></span>.<br></div>"	"<div>According to the kinetic collision theory, the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u>kinetic energy</u> needed for an <i>effective </i>collision</span>&nbsp;is known as the reaction's <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>activation energy</b></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>Effective collsions require this amount of energy along with being oriented the right way in order to form products.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""5c9e7bce973617b5f4cb7a8f595383e5.png""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>To find the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;b&gt;equilibrium&#x20;constant&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&amp;nbsp&#x3B;&#x28;&lt;b&gt;K&lt;sub&gt;eq&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;&lt;&#x2F;b&gt;&#x20;for&#x20;the&#x20;&lt;i&gt;reverse&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;direction"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>&nbsp;in a reaction, take the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>reciprocal</u> of K<sub>eq</sub> (1/K<sub>eq</sub>)</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>To find the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><b>equilibrium constant</b>&nbsp;(<b>K<sub>eq</sub>)</b> for the <i>reverse </i>direction</span>&nbsp;in a reaction, take the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><u>reciprocal</u> of K<sub>eq</sub> (1/K<sub>eq</sub>)</span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- This is because K<sub>eq</sub> = k<sub>forward</sub>/k<sub>reverse</sub>. If you want the inverse (i.e. to go backwards), you'll have to take the inverse of the equation (1/K<sub>eq</sub> = <b>k<sub>reverse</sub>/k<sub>forward</sub></b>).</div><div>- Remember, k represents the reaction constant (k) (related to kinetic collision theory).</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""0d5b45716e4b6f46ebc0a55ab574d9a9.png""><br></div></div>"
"""Iodide of zinc"" is the <i>same thing</i> as <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Zinc&#x20;iodide&#x20;&#x28;ZnI&lt;sub&gt;2&lt;&#x2F;sub&gt;&#x29;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span>."	"""Iodide of zinc"" is the <i>same thing</i> as <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Zinc iodide (ZnI<sub>2</sub>)</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

</div>"
"Consider the compound AB<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>Molecular weight of A: 65 g/mol</div><div>Molecular weight of B: 125 g/mol</div><div><br></div><div>You add 10 g of A and 10 g of B together in a sample. Which is the <b>limiting reagent</b>?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""B&#x20;is&#x20;the&#x20;limiting&#x20;reagent&#x20;because&#x20;10&#x20;grams&#x20;of&#x20;B&#x20;&#x28;which&#x20;has&#x20;a&#x20;higher&#x20;molecular&#x20;weight&#x29;&#x20;would&#x20;give&#x20;you&#x20;far&#x20;fewer&#x20;moles&#x20;than&#x20;10&#x20;grams&#x20;of&#x20;A&#x20;when&#x20;you&#x20;consider&#x20;that&#x20;there&#x27;s&#x20;a&#x20;1&#x3A;2&#x20;mole&#x20;ratio&#x20;needed&#x20;of&#x20;A&#x3A;B&#x2E;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[...]</span></div>"	"Consider the compound AB<sub>2</sub>.<div><br></div><div>Molecular weight of A: 65 g/mol</div><div>Molecular weight of B: 125 g/mol</div><div><br></div><div>You add 10 g of A and 10 g of B together in a sample. Which is the <b>limiting reagent</b>?</div><div><br></div><div><span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">B is the limiting reagent because 10 grams of B (which has a higher molecular weight) would give you far fewer moles than 10 grams of A when you consider that there's a 1:2 mole ratio needed of A:B.</span></div>

"When an atom is said to be in its ""lowest-energy electron configuration,"" the atom is <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">uncharged (<i>neutral</i> electron configuration)</span>.

"<b>Acid-base</b> reactions <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;do&#x20;not&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;"" data-ordinal=""1"">[do/do not]</span> involve changes in the <u>oxidation states</u> of reactants to products."	"<b>Acid-base</b> reactions <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>do not</i></span> involve changes in the <u>oxidation states</u> of reactants to products.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

NH<sub>3</sub> + H<sup>+</sup> → NH<sub>4</sub><sup>+</sup> does not involve any change in oxidation state - both N and H stayed the same.</div>"
"An atom's <b style="""">atomic</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">mass</b></span> is the <i>same</i> as its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""mass"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span> number."	"An atom's <b style="""">atomic</b> <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b style="""">mass</b></span> is the <i>same</i> as its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">mass</span> number.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

Protons + neutrons<div>- <b>Atomic weight </b>is the average weight of all of its existing isotopes.</div></div>"
"The <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;&lt;i&gt;higher&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[higher/lower]</span><i>&nbsp;</i>the atom's Z<sub>eff</sub>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i><u>higher</u></i></span> its <b>ionization</b>&nbsp;<b>energy</b>."	"The <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><u><i>higher</i></u></span><i>&nbsp;</i>the atom's Z<sub>eff</sub>, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i><u>higher</u></i></span> its <b>ionization</b>&nbsp;<b>energy</b>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>I.e. harder it is to remove an electron.</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-7f38de24231072df639df5147db58f59a1f4dff1.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>frequency factor (A)</b></span> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;i&gt;amount&#x20;&lt;&#x2F;i&gt;of&#x20;&lt;u&gt;molecular&#x20;collisions&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""2"">[...]</span>.<br></div>"	"<div>According to kinetic collsion theory, the <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><b>frequency factor (A)</b></span> represents the <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>amount </i>of <u>molecular collisions</u></span>.<br></div>
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Increasing frequency factor (A) increases the reaction's rate constant (k).</div><div>- Shown in the&nbsp;<u style=""font-weight: bold;"">Arrhenius equation</u>:</div><div><br></div><div><img src=""paste-47e6be036e4c76d58d32d4da7b74a37649d88cc8.jpg""><br></div><div><br></div><div><img src=""collision_theory2.jpg""><br></div></div>"
"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Increasing</span> the <u>temperature</u> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reaction shifts it to favor its <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""products"" data-ordinal=""2"">[reactants/products]</span>."	"<span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1"">Increasing</span> the <u>temperature</u> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reaction shifts it to favor its <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""2"">products</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Endothermic reactions ""absorb"" heat (think of heat like a reactant). When you add heat, according to LeChateliers principle, the reaction is shifted forwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7640ed558a308e456d802bd63bb0eeaadf864ac4.jpg""></div>"
"<span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""Increasing"" data-ordinal=""1"">[increasing/decreasing]</span> the <u>temperature</u> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reaction shifts it to favor its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">products</span>."	"<span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""1"">Increasing</span> the <u>temperature</u> of an <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""3""><b>endo</b></span><b>thermic</b> reaction shifts it to favor its <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2"">products</span>.
<hr>

<div style=""font-style:italic;color:gray;"">

<div>- Endothermic reactions ""absorb"" heat (think of heat like a reactant). When you add heat, according to LeChateliers principle, the reaction is shifted forwards.</div><div><br></div><img src=""paste-7640ed558a308e456d802bd63bb0eeaadf864ac4.jpg""></div>"
"A <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <b>pH</b> value signifies a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-cloze=""&lt;u&gt;acid&lt;&#x2F;u&gt;"" data-ordinal=""3"">[acid/base]</span>."	"A <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""1""><i>lower</i></span> <b>pH</b> value signifies a <span class=""cloze-inactive"" data-ordinal=""2""><i>stronger</i></span> <span class=""cloze"" data-ordinal=""3""><u>acid</u></span>.
